title: J9 BB - Paired Top River Bluff-Catch After Turn Check-Through
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets small on Js 7c 3d, checks back turn 2h, then bets 75% pot on river Jd after you check. You hold Jh9h. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: Jh9h sits high enough in BB's river bluff-catching region that folding would over-defend BTN's delayed floats. The turn check-through removes part of BTN's strongest value density, the paired jack cuts remaining trip value, and this combo still beats the missed backdoor pressure hands that arrive to river.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, the turn check still preserves enough delayed bluffing and thinner jack-x value that Jh9h remains a necessary call.

B.explanation: Most passive live players do not find enough river bluffs here, but trips is still too high in range to fold once the turn checked through.

C.explanation: Aggressive gamblers bring the most delayed-float bluffs to this node, so trips should never be folded.

diagnostic_prompts.concept: river bluff catching
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn check removes part of BTN's cleanest value while preserving delayed floats and missed backdoor pressure, so trips still defend.

options.label: The river bet is mostly boats and strong jack-x, so trips should fold often.
The turn check keeps in enough delayed floats and missed backdoors that trips still sit above threshold.
The 75% size is big enough that the prior street barely matters.

coaching_context.key_concept: Trips on a paired river should not be folded just because the river size looks uncomfortable.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Over-folding paired top-card rivers without rechecking how the turn action changed villain's value density.
common_mistakes: Treating any sizeable paired-river bet as value-heavy even after a turn check-through.
Ignoring that holding a jack trims part of the trip value region while leaving missed floats intact.
coaching_context.range_context: BTN reaches river with some trapped value, but also with delayed floats, missed backdoor pressure, and thin jack-x that does not want an even larger size.
range_notes: The turn check is the key filter. It removes part of BTN's clean double-barrel value and preserves more air.
Jh9h is not the nuts, but it is too strong to fall below the defending threshold.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The small flop c-bet keeps BTN's range wide instead of sharply polar.
The turn check removes some natural value barrels and preserves more delayed bluffs.
The paired jack cuts value combos while leaving enough missed backdoor pressure in the line.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Trips feels like an easy continue, but the real skill is understanding why the turn check matters more than the river size alone.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Jh9h still beats the missed floats that survive the turn check and blocks part of the remaining value region.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study paired-river bluff-catching after turn check-through before moving to overbet nodes.

title: QJ BB - Scare Ace River Bluff-Catch After Turn Check-Through
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 33% on Kc Qd 7c, checks back turn 4s, then bets 66% pot on river As after you check. You hold Qh Jc. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: The ace is a scare card that improves BTN's bluffing story more than its clean value density once the turn checked through. QhJc remains a bluff-catcher because it blocks some queen-x continues, beats missed clubs and gutshots, and the delayed line still contains enough air.

A.explanation: Versus a competent regular this is close, but QJ still has enough bluff-catching value when the ace arrives after a turn check-through.

B.explanation: Passive live players under-bluff scare aces after taking a free turn, so QJ drifts below threshold and can fold.

C.explanation: Aggressive gamblers attack the ace river far too often after checking back turn, so QJ should continue.

diagnostic_prompts.concept: scare card pressure
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The ace improves BTN's bluffing story while the turn check still caps part of the clean value region, so one-pair bluff catchers do not auto-fold.

options.label: The ace mainly adds strong value, so QJ should just fold.
The ace strengthens BTN's bluffing story and the turn check still caps some value, so QJ can keep bluff-catching.
Once the ace hits, the turn check is no longer important.

coaching_context.common_mistake: Over-folding scare aces without asking whether the prior street capped villain's value first.
common_mistakes: Treating the ace as a pure value card even after villain declined the turn barrel.
Ignoring that missed clubs and straight draws gain a much cleaner bluff story on the ace river.

range_notes: The turn check reduces some strong ace-x and two-street value candidates.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The small flop c-bet keeps BTN's range wide and light.
The turn check caps part of BTN's cleaner value while preserving delayed floats.
The ace gives those floats a stronger bluff story without magically making every delayed line value-heavy.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: QhJc still beats the missed draws that exploit the ace river, and the turn check keeps enough of those hands in range.

title: TT BB - River Overbet Bluff-Catch Versus Aggro Rec
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN bets flop on Ah Kd 9c, checks back turn 2s, then overbets 140% pot on river 5d after you check. You hold Ts Td. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: TT is not a pretty bluff-catcher on AK952, but the turn check-back followed by a large river overbet creates a line that contains too many missed floats and broadway air against aggressive live opponents. Folding all second-pair bluff-catchers here would let the overbet print.

A.explanation: Versus a competent regular, the 140% river overbet is more disciplined and TT drifts below threshold.

B.explanation: Aggressive recreational players overbet too many brick rivers after giving up the turn, so TT should continue.

C.explanation: Tight live players almost never produce this overbet bluff line often enough, so TT can fold.

diagnostic_prompts.concept: river bluff catching
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn check-back preserves enough missed floats and broadway air that folding every second-pair hand would over-fold to the overbet.

options.label: Second pair is simply too weak versus a 140% river overbet.
The line still contains enough missed air that some second-pair hands must continue, and TT is high enough to qualify in baseline live pools.
If TT is too good to fold, it should become a bluff-raise.

coaching_context.key_concept: River overbet defense is about range thresholds, not about whether the hand looks pretty.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Auto-folding every second-pair hand to a live overbet without checking whether the line still contains enough bluffs.
common_mistakes: Assuming overbet always equals nuts after villain checked back the turn.
Turning a bluff-catcher into a raise when all worse hands would fold.
coaching_context.range_context: The flop bet keeps broadway air, gutshots, and backdoor floats alive. The turn check preserves those hands. The river overbet is polar, but the line still contains enough air in baseline and aggro live pools.
range_notes: TT is not calling because it is strong in absolute terms.
TT is calling because overbet defense still needs some second-pair density once the turn checked through.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The flop c-bet is wide enough to include many non-made hands.
The turn check preserves more missed air than a second barrel would.
The polar overbet pressures bluff-catchers, but it does not erase the delayed-bluff density created by the turn check.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: This is an uncomfortable threshold defense node, which is exactly why it matters for live exploitation.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Calling with some second-pair hands prevents the overbet from succeeding against an over-folding defense.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study which second-pair bluff-catchers remain in range against polar river overbets after turn checks.

Raise

title: QT BB - Tight River Fold Versus Passive Delayed Value
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 40% on Qs 7d 3c, checks back turn 2s, then bets 90% pot on river Qd after you check. You hold Qh Tc. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: Top pair on a paired top-card river is not automatically a call. The delayed 90% river stab from a live player who skipped the turn is often weighted toward slowplayed trips, boats, and thin value that passive players still choose to bet when checked to. QT falls below threshold here.

A.explanation: Against competent opponents the line still carries enough delayed bluffs that QT can mix into calls.

B.explanation: Passive live pools under-bluff this delayed paired-river node, so QT should fold.

C.explanation: Aggressive gamblers attack paired rivers more recklessly, so QT can re-enter the calling region.

diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Passive players under-bluff this delayed paired-river line, so medium trips and top-pair bluff-catchers lose their calling equity.

options.label: Trips are too strong to fold on a paired top-card river.
The pool under-bluffs this delayed paired-river line, so QT becomes a fold.
The 90% size is so polar that it should force a bluff-catch.

coaching_context.key_concept: Pool assumptions can move a hand from near-threshold call to disciplined fold.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Treating every paired-river trip as a mandatory continue even when the pool under-bluffs the node.
common_mistakes: Overweighting the absolute strength of trips and ignoring the population profile.
Confusing a theoretically mixed defend with a practical live fold.
coaching_context.range_context: Passive live players arrive here with slowplayed queen-x, boats, and some thin value much more often than with enough delayed bluffs to justify a wide bluff-catching range.
range_notes: QT is not a huge mistake as a baseline theory call.
It becomes a practical fold once the pool does not supply the bluff density.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The small flop c-bet keeps the line wide at first.
The turn check does cap some value, but passive pools still arrive river with enough strong hands.
The large delayed river bet from a passive player tilts the node back toward value.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: This is a practical live fold that many players talk themselves out of because the hand class looks too strong.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Folding QT avoids paying off value-heavy delayed lines that are not bluffing often enough in passive pools.

title: K9 BB — Trips on K-High Paired River After Turn Check-Through
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 33% on Ks 8h 4c, checks back turn 2d, then bets 66% pot on river Kc after you check. You hold Kh 9d. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: Kh9d makes trips kings and sits comfortably above threshold. The turn check-through is the decisive filter: BTN's range of hands that would double-barrel for value — AK, KQ that want two streets of protection — is thinned considerably. What survives the check is a mix of slow-played boats, delayed floats (JT, QJ, T9s that took a free turn), and thin K-x that prefers to stab only once. Trips with a 9 kicker beats all of the delayed air and thin stabs. The 66% sizing is medium — not a shove — which is actually consistent with both thin value and bluffs rather than exclusively nutted hands.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, the turn check meaningfully removes double-barrel value. AK that would two-barrel for protection is largely absent. The river range is bimodal — value plus delayed bluffs — and trips with a 9 kicker beats everything on the bluff side.

B.explanation: Passive live players under-bluff delayed paired rivers. However, Kh9d is trips — high enough in range that folding would still over-surrender equity even against a tight river betting range. This is a thin call in passive pools, not a clear fold. The kicker matters: K9 survives, K2 would not.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players attack paired rivers far too often with missed floats and backdoor air after taking a free turn. Trips is a mandatory call and folding would let the line print.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Which part of BTN's range does the turn check-through remove, and why does it matter for calling with trips?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: paired river bluff catching
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn check removes most of BTN's natural two-barrel value (AK, KQ that need two streets of protection), which shifts the river betting range toward delayed floats and thin stabs — hands that hero's trips comfortably beats.

options.label: BTN checked to set a trap, so the river bet is mostly boats and nutted K-x — trips with a weak kicker should consider folding.
The check removes hands that would two-barrel for value and keeps in delayed floats and thin stabs, so the river still contains enough air for trips to call.
The kicker is the only thing that matters — trips with a 9 is strong enough regardless of what the turn action means.

coaching_context.key_concept: Trips on a paired top-card river after a turn check-through is a call — the skill is knowing why the turn action is the deciding factor, not the hand strength label alone.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Calling trips automatically without identifying which part of the turn action created the bluff density that justifies the continue.
common_mistakes: Treating any sizeable bet on a paired river as value-heavy without adjusting for the turn check.
Confusing kicker quality (K9 vs K2) — the threshold shifts meaningfully on paired rivers as the kicker weakens.
coaching_context.range_context: BTN c-bets 33% on a dry K-high flop — this is a very wide, range-bet style. The turn 2d is a blank; nothing connected. The check keeps in backdoor air, delayed floats, and thin K-x that chooses one stab instead of two.
range_notes: The small flop c-bet on a dry board keeps BTN's range wide and bluff-heavy going into the turn.
The turn check removes the hands most motivated to barrel twice for protection: AK, KQ. These hands want to charge draws and thin out hero's range.
The river range contains boats (K8s, K4s, 88, 44, 22), thin K-x, and delayed floats — it is bimodal, not value-heavy.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 33% range c-bet keeps BTN's entire range alive including all backdoor combos and speculative floats.
The check removes hands with strongest two-barrel motivation (AK, KQ) and preserves delayed floats (JT, QJ, T9) and thin K-x.
Paired K tightens the value region (boats now dominate value) but the bluff side — missed backdoors, delayed floats — is largely unchanged.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: This is a conceptually clean call. The challenge is articulating why the turn check matters more than the river size.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Kh9d beats the entire delayed-air region. The Kh blocks one combo of K-x value while leaving all the natural floats untouched.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study how kicker quality shifts the threshold on paired-top-card rivers — K9 calls, at what kicker does folding become correct?

title: J8 BB — Thin Trips Fold on Paired J River Versus Passive Pool
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 40% on Jd 9c 5s, checks back turn 3h, then bets 85% pot on river Jc after you check. You hold Jh 8d. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: Jh8d makes trips jacks but the 8 kicker is thin on a board that has connected value — J9 is in BTN's range as flopped two pair, and J5s exists. The 85% sizing after a free turn is large enough that passive live players are not bluffing often enough to justify continuing with a hand this weak in kicker. The turn check is the key: on a J-9-5 board with straight-draw texture, BTN had strong incentive to barrel the turn with semi-bluffs and value. The check removes most of those semi-bluffs, which perversely makes the river range more value-heavy — BTN's continued range on this board is: slow-played sets, flopped two pair, and thin J-x that finally decided to bet. The bluff count is lower than it looks because natural semi-bluffs (T8, 87, QT for gutshots) would have barreled the turn on this texture.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, the turn check preserves some delayed floats and the river range still has enough air for thin trips to mix into calls. This is close — 8 kicker is thin but the line has some bluff density.

B.explanation: Passive live players under-bluff delayed river spots on connected boards. BTN on J-9-5 had many turn-barrel candidates and declined all of them. The 85% river bet from a passive player represents the value that survived — J9, J5s, sets. Trips with an 8 kicker folds here.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players find enough delayed floats and bluff-attempts to make trips with an 8 kicker a necessary call. Folding would let the line exploit hero's over-folding.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Why does the connected J-9-5 flop change how you read the turn check-through on this paired-jack river?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: paired river bluff catching
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: On a connected board, BTN had strong incentive to barrel the turn with semi-bluffs (QT, T8, 87 for gutshots) and value protection. The check removes most of those — so the river range is more value-weighted than it would be on a dry board, raising the threshold for trips to call.

options.label: A connected board does not change the analysis — trips is trips and should always call a paired top-card river.
On a connected board BTN had more turn-barrel candidates. The check removes most semi-bluffs, so the river range is more value-heavy than on a dry board — thin trips folds.
A connected board means more missed draws arrive as bluffs on the river, so thin trips should call even more easily.

coaching_context.key_concept: Board texture determines how much bluff density survives a turn check-through. On connected boards, more of BTN's bluffing range would have barreled the turn — so the check filters out more air than on a dry board.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Treating all paired-top-river turn-check nodes the same regardless of flop texture.
common_mistakes: Assuming the turn check always means plenty of bluffs survive — on connected boards, most natural semi-bluffs barrel turn.
Calling thin trips (8 kicker) at 85% sizing without asking what bluffs remain after the check on this specific texture.
coaching_context.range_context: J-9-5 has straight connectivity. BTN would barrel QT, T8, 87, 76 as semi-bluffs on the turn. Those hands also gain the most from immediate pressure. The check filters them out — BTN's river range is more concentrated in value and in delayed one-street floats.
range_notes: Natural semi-bluffs on J-9-5 (QT for OESD, T8 for OESD, 87 for gutshot) would almost always two-barrel. The check removes them.
What survives: J9 (flopped two pair, slow-played), J5s (slow-played two pair), sets (JJ removed by hero, 99, 55), delayed air (A-high, K-high that floated).
The bluff count on a connected board after a turn check is lower than on a dry board where semi-bluffs were scarce anyway.

3h is a blank but on J-9-5, BTN had many strong semi-bluff candidates for turn. The check removes most of them — far more filtering than the same check on K-8-2.

title: Q7 BB — Turn Check vs Probe, Then Trips on Paired Q River (Two-Step)
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 33% on Qd 6c 2s. You hold Qh 7d. Turn 8h checks through. River Qs — you check, BTN bets 70% pot. This drill has two decision points.

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: Qh7d now makes trips queens with a 7 kicker. The turn check-through on a dry Q-6-2 board removes BTN's natural two-barrel candidates — AQ and KQ that want protection — and preserves delayed floats and missed backdoor hands. On this blank turn, there was little incentive for BTN to check anything except slow-plays, air, and very thin Q-x. The 7 kicker is thin, but trips is still above threshold because: the paired Q means BTN's boats require a six or a two specifically (Q6s, Q2s, 66, 22 — low-density combos), and the remaining betting range contains enough delayed floats to justify the call.

A.explanation: Competent regulars find delayed bluffs on blank turns and fire the paired river. Trips with a 7 kicker is above threshold; the 70% size is not so large that it signals only nutted hands.

B.explanation: Passive players under-bluff paired rivers, but Q-6-2 with a blank turn produces an extremely thin betting range for BTN — even passives rarely check back AQ and then bet river for thin value. Trips is a thin call in passive pools; it is not a clean fold.

C.explanation: Aggressive gamblers attack the paired river after a free turn regardless of board texture. Trips should never fold here.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: On the turn (8h, BTN checked), why is checking back the right play with top pair Q-7 rather than probing?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: OOP range discipline
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Hero's top pair is a legitimate bluff-catcher but not a strong enough hand to build a pot OOP. Checking keeps hero's range wide (including stronger hands hero would also check here), preserves the pot size for a river call, and lets BTN bluff the river. Probing inflates the pot with a hand that is not well-protected.

options.label: Top pair should always probe the turn for value when BTN checks behind.
Checking protects range balance, avoids inflating pot OOP with one-pair, and sets up a river bluff-catch — the pot stays manageable.
Checking is giving up — if top pair is good enough to call flop, it should bet turn.

steps.prompt: Turn 8h: BTN checks. You hold Qh 7d — top pair on Qd 6c 2s 8h. You act first. Check or probe?

options.label: Check
Bet (probe)

answer.explanation: Top pair is a bluff-catcher, not a hand that benefits from building a larger pot out of position. Checking here keeps hero's range wide (hero checks strong hands here too), sets up a clean river call, and avoids the problem of facing a turn raise with one pair OOP. BTN's range after a check is full of delayed floats and air — let them fire the river.

steps.prompt: River Qs: top card pairs. You check, BTN bets 70% pot. You now hold trips (Qh 7d on Qd 6c 2s 8h Qs). Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: Trips queens with a 7 kicker is above threshold. The turn check removed BTN's double-barrel value and preserved the delayed air that now fires the river. Boats on this board require Q6s, Q2s, 66, or 22 — a thin slice of BTN's range. The 7 kicker is weak but the line justifies calling.

coaching_context.key_concept: Turn range discipline (checking top pair OOP) sets up a clean river bluff-catch even when the top card pairs. The two-step structure teaches both: don't over-play the turn, and don't over-fold the river.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Probing the turn with top pair OOP and then facing an awkward spot — or folding trips on the river because the kicker is thin without accounting for what the turn check removed.
common_mistakes: Building a large pot out of position with top pair that has no strong protection when called.
Treating thin trips (7 kicker) as automatic fold on a paired river without checking boat density.
coaching_context.range_context: Q-6-2 is a dry flop. The 8h turn is a blank. BTN had limited semi-bluff candidates on this texture (no flush draw, minimal straight draws). The check is consistent with: slow-played sets, thin Q-x that prefers one bet, and all the air that floated the small c-bet.
range_notes: Dry board means fewer natural turn barrels — the check here is less filtering than on a connected board.
BTN's river range: boats (Q6s, Q2s, 66, 22 — thin), trips (AQ, KQ, QJ), delayed floats (J9, KT, AT that floated).
Boats are possible but low-density on Q-6-2. The paired Q threatens but does not dominate.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 33% range c-bet on a dry Q-6-2 keeps every hand in BTN's range.
Blank 8h. BTN checks — removes double-barrel value (AQ, KQ that wanted protection) and keeps in all air and slow-plays.
Qs pairs top card. The betting range is bimodal: boats/trips (value) and delayed floats (bluffs). 7 kicker is thin but above threshold.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Two decisions are required. The turn discipline is the harder conceptual piece — knowing not to bloat the pot with a hand that will become a bluff-catcher.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Checking turn keeps pot small and range wide. Calling river captures the equity from delayed floats that survived the check-through.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study OOP probe discipline — which one-pair hands benefit from probing vs checking when BTN checks back the turn?

title: QJ BB — Scare Ace River on K-Q Board After Turn Check-Through
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 33% on Kd Qc 8h, checks back turn 3s, then bets 60% pot on river Ah after you check. You hold Qd Jh. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: QdJh is second pair on a K-Q-8 board with an ace river. The ace is a scare card but not a pure value card in this spot. The turn check-through is critical: BTN had clear incentive to barrel AK, AQ, and KQ for two streets of value or protection on a K-Q-8 flop — those hands would charge flush draws and straight draws. The check removes them. What remains in BTN's range: slow-played hands (rare on this texture), delayed broadway air (JT, JTo missed, T9 for straight), and hands that use the ace to tell a strong story they didn't have. QdJh is not comfortable, but it still beats all of BTN's delayed air. The 60% sizing is medium — large enough to apply pressure, small enough that it could be a bluff or thin value.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, AK and AQ are thinned by the turn check. The ace river does give BTN's bluffs a better story, but the line still contains enough air (JT, T9, JTo) that QJ can continue. This is close.

B.explanation: Passive live players rarely construct this delayed story — check back top pair or two pair on K-Q-8 and then fire the ace river. When a passive player bets here, the range is value-heavy. QJ folds.

C.explanation: Aggressive gamblers attack scare-ace rivers after a free turn far too often. QJ should call and capture the over-bluffing equity.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: The ace river on a K-Q-8 board is a scare card. What makes QJ still a candidate bluff-catcher rather than an automatic fold?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: scare card pressure
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn check removed BTN's strongest AK, AQ, and KQ hands that would barrel twice. The ace improves BTN's bluffing story (missed JT, T9 can now represent a strong range) without adding as much clean value as it looks — so QJ still beats the delayed air.

options.label: The ace river on K-Q-8 is only called by AK or AQ — QJ should just fold.
The turn check removes BTN's strongest value hands, and the ace improves bluffing stories more than value density after a free turn — QJ still beats enough air to call.
60% is a moderate size so QJ should call regardless of what the ace river means.

coaching_context.key_concept: A scare ace is scarier when BTN had a free turn to barrel and declined. The ace adds a bluffing story to hands that got a free turn, but not as much clean value as it looks — because the value hands that would love two streets declined the turn.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Auto-folding second pair to any scare ace without asking whether the prior line restricts BTN's value region.
common_mistakes: Treating the ace river as only adding value when BTN checked the turn on a K-Q-8 flop.
Ignoring that JT, T9, and other broadway air gained a much cleaner bluffing story on the ace river.
coaching_context.range_context: K-Q-8 is a high-card, connected flop. BTN had strong motivation to double-barrel AK, AQ, KQ, KJ (protection + value). The check removes them. River range is: AK/AQ (slow-played — rare), thin KQ, plus broadway air (JT, T9, JTo, KJo that floated and uses ace story).
range_notes: AK, AQ, KQ that two-barrel would have done so on this flop texture — their absence after the check is significant.
Delayed broadway air (JT for gutshot, T9 for two-card straight) gained equity on the ace river and now bluffs.
The Qd in hero's hand blocks some of BTN's KQ and QJ hands — minor blocker benefit.

what_changed_by_street.detail: K-Q-8 is a strong board for BTN as the preflop aggressor. Small c-bet keeps range wide but hands like AK, AQ, KQ have two-barrel incentive.
Blank 3s. BTN's check removes the strongest two-barrel candidates. These hands want to protect against flush draws and broadway. The check is an unusual pass.
Ace arrives. The ace adds a strong story for JT, T9, and broadway floats. It also adds value for AK/AQ — but those were mostly removed by the turn check.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: The key insight is that the ace adds more bluff narrative than clean value in this specific line. Getting there requires knowing which hands BTN would have barreled on K-Q-8.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: QdJh beats all of BTN's delayed air and blocks some Q-x value. The turn check-through is the enabler.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study which boards create strong turn-barrel incentives for BTN, making the check-through a stronger filter against clean value.

title: 76 BB — Ace River Kills Second Pair on K-7-3 Board, Passive Pool Fold

Call
Raise

answer.explanation: 7h6h is second pair on a K-7-3 board, and the ace river drops hero to third pair on the final runout K-A-7-5-3. Hero beats only pure bluffs. On K-7-3, BTN had strong incentive to two-barrel AK and KQ for value and protection. The turn check removed those double-barrel hands — but it also means BTN's river betting range on the ace is sourced from: slow-played AK (the ace is now top pair), delayed A-x hands that called with backdoor equity, and delayed floats. Crucially, in passive live pools, players rarely construct delayed ace-river bluffs after a free turn. The 75% size after x/x is large for a passive player — it represents the value they slow-played or a strong pair they now chose to bet. Third pair is below threshold against this population.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, there are more delayed A-x floats and constructed bluffs on the ace. This is close — third pair is very thin, but the turn check preserved some air. Fold is still the baseline play.

B.explanation: Passive live players under-bluff ace rivers after taking a free turn. The 75% sizing signals value. Third pair with a 6 kicker is a clean fold.

C.explanation: Aggressive gamblers attack scare aces after free turns often enough that third pair can mix into calls. This is still thin — it is not a mandatory call even against this pool.

title: 98 BB — Scare Ace River Overbet on J-9-5 Board, Second Pair Defends
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 35% on Jh 9d 5c, checks back turn 2s, then overbets 120% pot on river Ac after you check. You hold 9h 8h. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: 9h8h is second pair on a J-9-5 board with an ace river and a 120% polar overbet. The overbet is the critical tell about BTN's range construction: it declares a polar range — either strong value or a bluff. The ace river on J-9-5 after a turn check-through is a textbook delayed-bluff spot. BTN's natural semi-bluffs on J-9-5 (QT for OESD, T8 for OESD, 87 for gutshot) all took a free turn and now have a credible bluffing story on the ace. The 120% size selects for a polarized strategy. Second pair 9s with an 8 kicker is not a comfortable call, but at this sizing and this line, the range still contains enough air to justify defending. Hero does not need to be right all that often at 120% to break even.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, the 120% overbet on a delayed ace river after x/x is a real polar range. Second pair sits near threshold — call in baseline, though it is close.

B.explanation: Passive live players rarely construct polar overbets with a bluffing range on ace rivers after a free turn. When a passive player overbets here, the range is value-heavy — AJ, A9, A5, slow-played J9. Second pair folds.

C.explanation: Aggressive gamblers overbet scare aces after free turns far too often with any backdoor hand and missed draw. Second pair should call and capture the over-bluffing equity.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Why is the 120% overbet sizing particularly informative about what the turn check-through means on this board?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: river polarization
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The overbet declares a polar range — either strong value or a bluff. On J-9-5 after x/x, BTN had natural semi-bluff candidates that took the free turn and now fire the ace as a bluff. The overbet collects more chips when it works and selects for a range that contains both nuts and air.

options.label: A 120% overbet is always value — second pair should always fold to polar overbets.
The overbet signals a polar range — strong value or a bluff. On J-9-5 after a free turn, the bluff frequency is high enough that second pair must defend.
The ace and sizing do not matter much — what matters is that hero has second pair and should always call.

coaching_context.key_concept: A polar overbet on a scare-ace river after a turn check-through does not auto-mean value. The overbet declares a bimodal range, and the connected board created enough semi-bluff candidates that the bluff fraction is real.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Folding to all polar overbets without checking whether the line and board created a credible bluff population.
common_mistakes: Treating 'overbet' and 'scare ace' as two independent signals that both point to value — they are often connected to a bluffing story.
Ignoring the break-even math: at 120% sizing, hero needs to be right about one-third of the time to call profitably.
coaching_context.range_context: J-9-5 is connected. BTN had natural semi-bluffs: QT (OESD), T8 (OESD), 87 (gutshot), 76 (gutshot). All took the free turn. The ace river gives all of these a strong bluffing story. AJ, A9, A5 that slow-played are the value side.
range_notes: QT and T8 are OESDes on J-9-5. They took the free turn. The ace did not complete them — they bluff the ace overbet.
87 and 76 are gutshots on J-9-5. Same story — they bluff the ace.
Value: AJ (top two pair), A9 (top pair + pair), A5 (top pair), slow-played J9s. The value is real but so is the air.

what_changed_by_street.detail: J-9-5: highly connected board. BTN c-bets 35% — wide range including all semi-bluffs and speculative hands.
2s blank. BTN checks. On J-9-5, natural semi-bluffs (QT, T8, 87) survive the check — they could not barrel profitably without more equity.
Ac: a scare card that improves BTN's bluffing story without adding much clean value (those hands would have barreled turn). The overbet declares a polar range.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: This is a sizing and line-reading drill. The discomfort of calling second pair into a large overbet is the exact mechanism the aggressor relies on.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: 9h8h beats the entire semi-bluff population that survived the turn check. The 120% sizing requires less than one-third bluff frequency to justify calling.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study the break-even math on polar river overbets — how often must villain bluff at 120% for second pair to call profitably?

On J-9-5 after a turn check, the semi-bluff population (QT, T8, 87, 76) is large enough to approach that threshold in baseline and aggro pools.

8h removes one 87h combo from the bluff population — slightly negative blocker effect.

title: JT BB — Backdoor Air Folds to Polar Overbet on A-K-7-4-2 Board
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 40% on Ah Kc 7d, checks back turn 4s, then overbets 150% pot on river 2h after you check. You hold Jd Ts. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: JdTs is J-high on a final board of A-K-7-4-2. Hero beats exactly nothing except pure air. The problem is compounded by hero's blocker profile: Jd blocks JT, J9, QJ — natural broadway air bluffs that would attack this board. Hero is not holding bluff-catching equity; hero is holding bluff-blocking equity. At 150% sizing, the polar overbet requires a high bluff frequency to justify calling — and JdTs is poorly positioned to capture that equity because it removes some of the bluffs hero needs BTN to have. Fold here is disciplined range management, not cowardice.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, a 150% polar overbet after a turn check on A-K-7 is a disciplined range — value-heavy with some constructed bluffs. J-high with bad blockers folds.

B.explanation: Passive players almost never overbet bluff at 150% on a brick river after a free turn. This is a very clear fold.

C.explanation: Even against an aggressive gambler who overbets too wide, J-high with bad blockers is below threshold. Hero can defend with other hands — not this one.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: JdTs has J and T — cards that appear in many natural bluff combos. Why is that a problem for hero as a bluff-catcher here?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker effects
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Hero's Jd and Ts remove some of BTN's natural bluff combos (JTo, J9, QT, etc.). When hero holds cards from the bluffing region, the remaining bluff density in BTN's range drops — meaning BTN's bet is more value-weighted than it looks from the board alone. A bad-blocker hand should not call a polar overbet.

options.label: The J and T don't matter — hero should just look at the board and decide based on hand strength.
J and T appear in BTN's natural bluff combos. Holding them reduces bluff density, making BTN's range more value-heavy — bad blocker hands should not call polar overbets.
J and T block some of BTN's J-high value — this is actually a blocker benefit for calling.

coaching_context.key_concept: Blocker effects determine which hands can hero-call a polar overbet. J-high with cards from the bluffing region is a bad bluff-catcher — it removes some of the bluffs hero needs BTN to have.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Calling a polar overbet with hands that hold cards from the bluffing region, not the value region.
common_mistakes: Thinking J-high 'has nothing to lose' by calling because it loses to most of BTN's range anyway — but EV is still negative when the call frequency is wrong.
Ignoring that blockers to bluffs hurt the profitability of calling more than blockers to value help.
coaching_context.range_context: A-K-7-4-2 is a high-card, unconnected board. BTN's bluffing region after a turn check consists of: broadway air (QJ, JTo, QTs), missed backdoors. Hero holds J and T — two cards that appear in those natural bluffs. BTN's value: AK, AQ, KK, A7s, K7s — none of those are blocked by hero's hand.
range_notes: Natural bluff combos on A-K-7-4-2: QJo (Q-high blocked by hero's J), JTo (blocked by hero's J and T), QTs (partially blocked).
Hero's hand reduces the density of exactly the hands that make calling viable.
The 150% size requires an unusually high bluff frequency to be profitable — and hero's blockers make that threshold harder to reach.

what_changed_by_street.detail: A-K-7 is a high-card board. Hero floats with JT — no pair, backdoor gutshot potential. The flop call is speculative.
4s: blank. BTN checks. JT gained nothing — still a float with no equity. The check preserves the hand as a future bluff or marginal value hand.
2h: total brick. JT is J-high. BTN overbets 150%. Hero's hand holds cards from BTN's bluffing region — bad blocker profile for a call.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: This drill teaches the blocker quality standard for polar overbet calls. J-high fails on two levels: hand strength and blocker profile.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Folding J-high with bad blockers is correct range management. Hero can call with hands that have better blocker profiles — K-x, Q-high with hearts, A-high — but not JT.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study which hand types make good overbet calls on A-K-7 runouts — focus on what each hand blocks in BTN's value vs bluff region.

value_buckets.label: Strong value on A-K-7
value_buckets.note: These hands slow-played or floated with top pair on A-K-7 and now bet large for value. None are blocked by JdTs.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Core value side of the polar range

bluff_buckets.label: Broadway air — the natural bluff population
bluff_buckets.note: These are BTN's natural bluffing hands on A-K-7 after a turn check. JdTs removes Jd from JTo and Ts from QTs — hero holds cards that reduce this bluff population.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Bluff density reduced by hero's hand

bluff_catchers.label: Good bluff-catchers on A-K-7-4-2
bluff_catchers.note: These hands do not hold cards from BTN's bluffing region. They are better positioned to call a polar overbet.

bluff_catchers.label: Hero hand — bad blocker, wrong position
bluff_catchers.note: J-high is too low in showdown and holds bad blockers. Double failure.

threshold_notes: At 150% sizing, hero needs BTN to bluff more than 40% of the time for a call to break even.
With JdTs blocking some natural bluffs, the effective bluff frequency in BTN's remaining range is lower — further below threshold.
blocker_notes: JdTs blocks JTo (one of the cleanest delayed bluff combos on A-K-7).
Ts blocks QTs — another natural delayed bluff.
Neither card blocks any of BTN's value hands. This is a pure blocker-to-bluffs situation — exactly what a caller does not want.

hero_hand_bucket.summary: Bad bluff-catcher — blocks bluffs, not value
hero_hand_bucket.note: Hero removes some of the bluffs needed to justify calling, while not touching the value that beats hero. Fold cleanly.

title: Q5 BB — Top Pair Defends Polar Overbet on Q-8-3-6-9 Runout
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 35% on Qh 8d 3c, checks back turn 6s, then overbets 130% pot on river 9d after you check. You hold Qs 5h. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: Qs5h is top pair on a board that ran Q-8-3-6-9. The river 9d added some straight possibility (6-7-8-9 needs a 5 or T; 7-8-9 needs a 6 or T) but the turn 6s did the real connecting work. BTN c-bets Q-8-3 small, checks the turn, then fires 130% polar on the 9d. At 130%, BTN declares a polar range. The question is whether the bluff side of that polar range is populated enough. On Q-8-3, BTN had natural semi-bluffs: JT for OESD, T7 for gutshot, 97 for gutshot, plus backdoor diamonds. All took the free turn. The river 9d is not a clean value card for BTN after a turn check (QQ that slow-played is rare; most Q-x that would overbet for value would have second-barreled). The bluff population on the 9d is real — hands using the connectedness of the board. Top pair Q with a 5 kicker is not pretty but sits above threshold as a bluff-catcher in this polar spot.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, the polar 130% overbet after a turn check on Q-8-3 is a structured spot. Top pair is above threshold — the overbet line contains enough missed draws to justify calling.

B.explanation: Passive live players rarely overbet bluff at 130% on connected run-outs after a free turn. When a passive player overbets this river, the range is value-heavy — Q9, Q8, 89s, sets. Top pair with a 5 kicker can fold in passive pools.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players overbet river draws and missed hands far too often. Top pair should call and punish the over-bluffing frequency.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Why is top pair Q with a 5 kicker above threshold for a 130% polar overbet call here, when it clearly beats nothing strong?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: river polarization
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Top pair is above threshold not because it beats strong hands, but because the overbet declares a polar range and the bluff side (JT, T7, 97 that took free turn on connected board) is real. At 130%, the break-even bluff frequency is achievable on this runout.

options.label: Top pair always defends polar overbets — it is too strong to fold.
The polar range declares both strong value and real bluffs. On this connected runout the bluff density from missed draws is sufficient for top pair to call.
Top pair with a 5 kicker cannot beat enough of BTN's value range, so it should always fold to a 130% overbet.

coaching_context.key_concept: Top pair as a bluff-catcher in a polar overbet spot is justified by bluff density, not hand strength. The question is whether the board and line produced enough air on the bluff side.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Folding top pair to any large river bet without evaluating whether the overbet declaration implies a meaningful bluff population.
common_mistakes: Treating top pair with a weak kicker as always below threshold for overbet defense.
Forgetting that at 130% sizing, BTN only needs to be bluffing roughly 35% of the time for a call to break even.
coaching_context.range_context: Q-8-3 is a semi-connected board. Natural semi-bluffs: JT (OESD), T7 (gutshot), 97 (gutshot), plus backdoor suited hands. Turn 6s added more connectivity. All these hands took the free turn and now attack the polar river. BTN's value: Q8s, Q9s, 89s, 99 — these are real but limited.
range_notes: On Q-8-3, BTN's semi-bluffs are meaningful: JT, T7, 97 all have straight equity and took the free turn.
The 9d connects with the 6-8 region — 8-9 makes two pair for some of BTN's calling hands, but BTN would more likely barrel those.
BTN's value from slow-plays: Q8s (flopped two pair, checked to disguise), 99 (overpair, slow-played), 88 (set). Low density.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Q-8-3: semi-connected. BTN c-bets 35% — wide range. Natural semi-bluffs (JT, T7, 97) are in BTN's continuing range.
6s adds connectivity (T7 now has more outs, 97 pairs the 6). BTN checks — removes double-barrel value but keeps semi-bluffs.
9d: adds more connectivity. BTN overbets 130%. The polar declaration means this is either value (Q8, 99, 88) or air (JT, T7, 97 that took free turn). Top pair sits in the middle.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: This is an uncomfortable call because top pair with a 5 kicker is not a strong hand. The discipline is recognizing that overbet defense is about range math, not hand strength alone.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Qs5h beats all the missed draws that fire the polar overbet. The semi-bluff population from the connected board is sufficient to justify the call at 130%.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study which runouts and boards produce enough bluff density to defend top pair against polar overbets — not all connected boards are equivalent.

value_buckets.note: These hands are the value side of BTN's polar range. Real but lower density than on a dry board.

bluff_catchers.note: Beats all bluffs. Loses to two pair, sets, and better Q-x hands. Above threshold at 130% given the semi-bluff population.

bluff_catchers.note: Lower pairs fall below threshold at 130% sizing — they sit below too much of BTN's value range.

threshold_notes: At 130% sizing, hero needs BTN to bluff roughly 35% of the time for a profitable call.
On Q-8-3-6-9 after a turn check, the semi-bluff population (JT, T7, 97) is large enough to support that frequency.

5h has no meaningful blocker impact on this board.

title: 65 BB — Turn Call with Two Pair, Then River Overbet Defense (Two-Step)
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 35% on Th 6c 2d. You hold 6d 5d. Turn 5s: BTN bets 55%. River Jd: BTN overbets 140%. This drill has two decision points.

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: 6d5d makes two pair (sixes and fives) and sits near the top of hero's range after calling two streets. BTN double-barreled (flop and turn) and then fired 140% on the river Jd. The Jd is an overcard that improves BTN's bluffing story (KQ, KJ, QJ can now represent a strong hand) but it does not complete any obvious draws on T-6-2-5-J. BTN's polar overbet declares either strong value or a deliberate bluff. The bluff candidates on the river: missed gutshots from T-6-2-5 (A4, 43, 74), backdoor hands that took two shots and now fire the J. Two pair is near the top of hero's calling range after two calls and must defend the polar overbet — folding would let three-street lines print.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, three-street polar overbets are structured and real. Two pair is near the top of hero's calling range and should defend.

B.explanation: Passive live players rarely construct three-street lines with bluffs. When a passive player double-barrels and then overbets, the range is concentrated in value. Two pair can fold against this profile.

C.explanation: Aggressive gamblers who three-barrel with bluffs are exactly the pool where two pair at the top of hero's range should call and capture the over-bluffing equity.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: On the turn, BTN bets 55% after hero made two pair with 6d5d on T-6-2-5. Why is calling better than raising the turn?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: OOP two pair defense
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Raising two pair OOP inflates the pot when BTN can have T5s, T6s (two pair ahead), or sets (TT, 66, 22) that are ahead or close. Calling keeps BTN's bluffing range in, avoids over-committing with a hand that is not protected OOP, and sets up a river call with the best bluff-catching hand.

options.label: Two pair is strong — raise the turn immediately to build the pot for value.
Calling keeps bluffing hands in BTN's range, avoids building a pot OOP against hands that have hero beaten, and sets up a strong river call.
BTN double-barreling is too strong for two pair — hero should fold the turn.

steps.prompt: Turn 5s: BTN bets 55% pot. You just made two pair (6s and 5s) with 6d5d on T-6-2-5. Call, raise, or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: Two pair is strong but not protected enough to raise OOP without concern. BTN's double-barrel range still contains TT, 66, 22 (sets) and T6s, T5s (better two pair). Raising bloats the pot against those hands. Flat-calling conceals hero's strength, keeps BTN's bluffs in the hand, and positions hero for a river call with the top of their range.

steps.prompt: River Jd: you check, BTN overbets 140% pot. You hold 6d5d — two pair on T-6-2-5-J. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: Two pair is near the top of hero's calling range after two streets. The polar 140% overbet declares strong value or a bluff. BTN's bluff candidates: missed gutshots (A4, 43), backdoor air that attacked twice. Two pair must defend — folding lets the polar three-street line print unchallenged.

coaching_context.key_concept: Two pair OOP requires turn patience (call, not raise) and river courage (defend the polar overbet). The two-step teaches: don't over-commit on turn, don't under-defend the river when you are at the top of your range.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Raising two pair OOP on the turn and then being unable to call the river comfortably, or folding two pair on the river after calling two streets.
common_mistakes: Raising two pair on the turn OOP when BTN's range still contains sets and better two pair — this inflates the pot in a bad spot.
Folding two pair to a river overbet when it sits at the top of hero's calling range after two calls — this surrenders too much to three-street polar attacks.
coaching_context.range_context: T-6-2: dry board. Hero floated with second pair and backdoor flush draw. Turn 5s improves to two pair. BTN double-barrels. River Jd is an overcard — BTN's river range is polar: value (sets, better two pair) and bluffs (missed gutshots, backdoor air). Two pair is hero's best bluff-catching hand here.
range_notes: After calling two streets, hero's range is concentrated in made hands. Two pair is near the top.
BTN's three-street line narrows to genuine value and deliberate bluffs — the bluff fraction still matters.
6d5d removes some diamond bluffs (slightly negative blocker on river) but this is a minor effect.

what_changed_by_street.detail: T-6-2: hero calls with second pair and backdoor. BTN c-bets 35% — wide range.
5s: hero improves to two pair. BTN fires 55% — representing continued strength. Hero calls with the best hand in a concealed way.
Jd: an overcard. BTN overbets 140% — polar declaration. Two pair is near the top of hero's range and must defend.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Two decisions across streets. Turn patience (not raising) and river courage (calling the overbet) are both required. Either mistake is costly.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Calling turn keeps pot manageable and bluffs in. Calling river defends the polar overbet with the best hand in hero's calling range.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study when two pair OOP should raise vs call on the turn — the key variables are protection need and vulnerability to being counterfeited.

value_buckets.label: BTN's strong value — three streets
value_buckets.note: Sets and better two pair are in BTN's three-street range. Hero's two pair loses to all of these.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Real but limited density

value_buckets.label: Overpairs and top pair
value_buckets.note: These hands might three-barrel on this runout, especially JJ which hit the river.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Possible

bluff_buckets.label: Missed gutshots and backdoor air
bluff_buckets.note: These floated T-6-2 with weak equity and double-barreled as semi-bluffs, then fire the river. The main bluff constituency.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Present — meaningful bluff population

bluff_catchers.label: Two pair — hero hand
bluff_catchers.note: Near the top of hero's calling range after two streets. Defends the polar overbet. Loses to better two pair, sets, overpairs.
bluff_catchers.frequency_hint: Call in baseline and aggro pools

bluff_catchers.label: Bluff-catchers below threshold
bluff_catchers.note: Lower pairs fall below threshold at 140% sizing.

threshold_notes: At 140% sizing, break-even bluff frequency is roughly 42%. Two pair is near the top of hero's range after two calls — this is where hero takes a stand.
In passive pools, BTN's three-street line is more value-heavy. Two pair can fold there.

5d removes some diamond flush bluffs slightly — minor negative blocker effect on river.

title: QJ BB — Q-High Folds Despite Broadway Blockers on K-High Brick Runout
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 40% on Kh 8d 3c, checks back turn 7s, then bets 80% pot on river 5d after you check. You hold Qd Jc. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: QdJc is Q-high on a K-8-3-7-5 board. Hero has no showdown value against any made hand. The Q and J are cards that appear directly in BTN's natural bluff combos on K-high boards: QJo, QTs, JTo, J9s are all delayed floats that would attack this spot. Hero holds two of the key bluffing cards, which reduces the bluff density in BTN's range — the exact opposite of what a caller wants. A blocker-to-bluffs hand was already below threshold; the blocker analysis confirms the fold and removes any case for a hero call. This drill is about recognizing when blockers are pointing in the wrong direction.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, Q-high with bad blockers (Q and J block natural bluffs) is a clear fold. Bluff density is reduced by hero's hand.

B.explanation: Passive live players under-bluff this delayed spot even at full bluff frequency. Q-high folds regardless of blockers.

C.explanation: Even against an aggressive gambler, Q-high with bad blockers is below threshold. Other hands in hero's range — 8-x, 3-x pairs — are better bluff-catchers. Q-high folds.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Qd Jc — cards that appear in many natural bluff combos on K-8-3. Does this make QJ a better or worse bluff-catcher, and why?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker effects
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Holding cards from the bluffing region reduces BTN's bluff density, making the remaining bet more value-weighted. QJ is a worse bluff-catcher — it removes some of the bluffs hero needs BTN to have, while Q-high was already below threshold without the blocker effect.

options.label: Qd blocks KQo — this reduces BTN's value density and makes QJ a better bluff-catcher.
Q and J appear in BTN's natural bluff combos (QJo, QTs, JTo). Holding them reduces bluff density, making the remaining bet more value-heavy — QJ is a worse bluff-catcher.
Q-high always folds — blocker analysis does not matter at this hand strength.

coaching_context.key_concept: Holding broadway cards on a K-high board means holding cards from the bluffing region, not the value region. This reduces the bluff density that justifies calling and confirms the fold.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Treating broadway holdings as 'blocker cards' without specifying what they actually block — value or bluffs.
common_mistakes: Thinking 'I block KQ so BTN has fewer strong value hands — I should call more.' On K-8-3, QdJc blocks KQo (one value combo) but also blocks QJo, QTs, JTo (multiple natural bluffs). Net blocker effect: negative for calling.
Hero-calling with Q-high because of broadway holdings without asking what direction those holdings block.
coaching_context.range_context: K-8-3 is a dry K-high board. BTN c-bets 40%. The turn 7s and river 5d are both bricks. BTN's bluff population: QJo, QTs, JTo, J9s that floated the small c-bet and fire the brick river. Hero holds Q and J — cards that appear in all these natural bluffs.
range_notes: QdJc blocks: KQo (one value combo — good), QJo (one bluff — bad), QTs (one bluff — bad), JTo (one bluff — bad), J9s (one bluff — bad).
Net effect: one value combo removed, multiple bluff combos removed. This is a net negative for calling.
The remaining river betting range after QdJc's blocking effect is more value-heavy than without the blocker.

what_changed_by_street.detail: K-8-3: hero floats with QJ. No pair, no draw. Pure speculative float with backdoor potential.
7s: brick. BTN checks. QJ is still Q-high — a delayed float candidate with no improvement.
5d: brick. BTN bets 80%. QJ is Q-high with bad blockers. The Q and J remove bluff combos from BTN's range. Clean fold.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: The lesson requires knowing which direction blockers point. Q and J look like strong cards — but on K-high boards they sit in the bluffing region, not the value region.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Folding Q-high with bad blockers is correct range management. Hero can call with hands that have pair value (8x, 3x) or better blocker profiles — not Q-high with broadway cards.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study the two blocker directions on K-high boards: which combos block value (good for calling) and which block bluffs (bad for calling)?

title: 87 BB — Second Pair Calls Despite Partial Bluff-Blocking on T-8-4-2-Q Board
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 35% on Th 8h 4s, checks back turn 2d, then bets 70% pot on river Qc after you check. You hold 8c 7d. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: 8c7d is second pair (pair of 8s) on T-8-4-2-Q. The 8c blocks some 8-x floats from BTN's range — a partial negative blocker effect. The key question is whether this partial blocker drops second pair below threshold. It does not. The Q river creates a strong delayed bluffing story for broadway hands (KJ, KQ-type floats, JTo that now use the Q) — none of which are blocked by hero's 8c or 7d. Second pair beats all of these non-8 bluffs. A partial blocker to one category of bluffs (8-x floats) does not eliminate the remaining bluff population (broadway delayed, Q-story bluffs). Second pair remains above threshold in baseline and aggro pools.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, second pair on T-8-4-2-Q after a turn check-through is above threshold. The Q river creates delayed bluffing stories for many broadway hands. Call.

B.explanation: Passive live players under-bluff this delayed Q river spot. Second pair with a partial bluff-blocker (8c reduces some 8-x bluffs) tips below threshold in passive pools. Fold.

C.explanation: Aggressive gamblers attack Q rivers after free turns frequently. Second pair with an 8 is a mandatory call in this pool.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero's 8c blocks some 8-x floats in BTN's range — a partial blocker to bluffs. Does this push second pair below threshold on T-8-4-2-Q, and why or why not?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker effects
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The 8c removes some 8-x bluffs but not all bluffs. The Q river creates a large delayed broadway bluff population (KJ, JTo, KQ-type floats using the Q story) that 8c does not block. Second pair beats all of these non-8 bluffs. A partial blocker does not flip a hand from above-threshold to below-threshold when the remaining bluff population is still meaningful.

options.label: 8c blocks 8-x bluffs — this reduces bluff density enough that second pair should fold.
8c removes some 8-x bluffs but the Q river creates a large delayed broadway bluff population that 8c does not touch. Second pair remains above threshold.
Blocker effects are too small to matter — just decide based on hand strength alone.

coaching_context.key_concept: A partial blocker to one bluff category does not flip the calling decision if a separate bluff population remains unaffected. On T-8-4-2-Q, 8c blocks 8-x floats but cannot block the broadway delayed bluff population created by the Q river.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Treating any blocker-to-bluffs as justification to fold, without first identifying which bluffs remain unaffected.
common_mistakes: Folding second pair because '8c blocks bluffs' without checking how many bluffs (specifically broadway delayed bluffs) remain.
Conflating partial blocker effects with decisive blocker effects — these are very different in terms of threshold impact.
coaching_context.range_context: T-8-4 with a flush draw (two hearts). BTN c-bets 35% — wide. Natural floats: JTo (OESD), J9 (gutshot), 97 (OESD), 65 (gutshot), plus flush draws. Turn 2d blank; BTN checks. River Qc: adds a strong story for KJ, KQ-type floats, JTo that missed. None of these broadway bluffs are blocked by 8c.
range_notes: 8c blocks A8, K8, Q8 floats — a real but partial effect.
8c does NOT block JTo, KJs, J9s, 97s — the broadway delayed bluff population from the Q river.
The Q river's contribution to bluff density is unaffected by hero's 8. This is the key insight.

what_changed_by_street.detail: T-8-4 with flush draw: BTN c-bets wide. Hero calls with second pair. BTN's range includes all semi-bluffs.
2d: blank. BTN checks. Removes double-barrel value (TT that wanted protection) and keeps all delayed broadway floats.
Qc: adds strong story for KJ, KQ-type, JTo — none of these are blocked by 8c. Bluff density from broadway remains real.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: This drill requires knowing which bluffs a partial blocker affects and which it does not — the skill is specificity in blocker analysis.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: 8c7d beats all the broadway delayed bluffs (KJ, JTo, J9) that survived the turn check and now use the Q river story. The partial 8-x blocker effect does not drop bluff frequency below threshold.
coaching_context.follow_up: Compare a full blocker (hero holds the primary bluff card category) vs a partial blocker (hero holds cards from one of several bluff categories). The threshold impact is very different.

7d has minimal blocker impact.

title: K6 BB — Thin Top Pair Folds: Kicker Fails Even With a Blocker Benefit
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 40% on Kd 8c 5d, checks back turn Jh, then bets 90% pot on river 2c after you check. You hold Kh 6h. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: Kh6h is top pair K with a 6 kicker on K-8-5-J-2. The turn Jh should have triggered BTN to barrel AJ, KJ, JJ for protection and value — but BTN checked. That removes some of those. However, the check does not touch BTN's value region that beats hero: K8s (flopped two pair), K5s (flopped two pair), 88, 55, 22 (sets), and KJ that slow-played. Hero's Kh blocks some K-x delayed bluffs (KQ, KT trying a one-street story), which is a genuine positive for calling. But the 6 kicker means hero loses to every K-x better-kicker hand in BTN's value range. The blocker benefit (Kh removes K-x bluffs) does not overcome the kicker problem: hero still loses to K8, K5, KJ, KQ with better kicker at showdown. At 90% sizing, the math does not work — the value region that beats K6 is too wide.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, K6 is marginally below threshold. The Kh blocker is real, but the 6 kicker loses to too much of BTN's value region at 90% sizing. Fold is the baseline play.

B.explanation: Passive live players under-bluff delayed K-high board spots. K6 is a clean fold in passive pools — the blocker benefit does not overcome the thin kicker against a tight betting range.

C.explanation: Against an aggressive gambler who attacks brick rivers after free turns, K6 can mix into calls. The Kh blocks K-x bluffs and aggro players over-bluff enough to bring K6 near threshold. This is still a thin continue.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Kh — a genuine blocker to K-x delayed bluffs. Why does this positive blocker benefit not save the call with K6?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker effects and kicker quality
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Kh removes some K-x delayed bluffs, which reduces BTN's bluff density — a real benefit. However, K6 still loses to every K-x better-kicker hand (K8, K5, KJ, KQ) in BTN's value region. Blocking bluffs does not remove the value hands that beat hero at showdown. The kicker is too thin to absorb the remaining value region at 90% sizing.

options.label: Kh blocks K-x bluffs — this is enough to justify calling with top pair at 90% sizing.
Kh does reduce K-x bluff density, but K6 still loses to K8s, K5s, and sets — the value region. The 6 kicker is too thin for the blocker benefit to tip the decision at 90%.
Top pair should never fold to a delayed river bet regardless of kicker — the blocker makes this an automatic call.

coaching_context.key_concept: A genuine positive blocker (Kh reduces K-x bluffs) does not save a call when the kicker is so thin that hero loses to the entire K-x better-kicker value region. Blocker benefit and kicker quality must both be in hero's favor — or the benefit must be decisive.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Using a positive blocker to justify calling with a thin kicker, without accounting for how much of the value region the kicker still loses to.
common_mistakes: Calling K6 because 'I block K-x bluffs' without noting that K6 loses to K8, K5, KJ, KQ — all K-x hands with better kicker in BTN's value range.
Treating any blocker benefit as sufficient for a call regardless of kicker depth and sizing.
coaching_context.range_context: K-8-5 is a K-high board with flush potential (diamonds). BTN c-bets 40%. Turn Jh: BTN checks — removes AJ, KJ that would two-barrel for protection. River 2c: brick. BTN bets 90%. Value: K8s, K5s, 88, 55 (sets), KJ that slow-played. Bluffs: K-x delayed (KQ, KT) — blocked by Kh. Non-K bluffs: A9, missed floats. Hero's Kh blocks the K-x delayed bluffs but leaves all the K-x value and sets intact.
range_notes: Kh blocks KQo, KTo (K-x delayed bluffs) — genuine positive blocker effect.
Kh does NOT block K8s, K5s (two pair), 88, 55, 22 (sets), KJs (slow-played) — the value region.
Net: bluff density reduced (good), value density unchanged (K6 still loses to it). At 90% the math does not work.

what_changed_by_street.detail: K-8-5: hero calls with top pair 6 kicker. BTN c-bets 40%.
Jh: BTN checks. Removes AJ, KJ that would two-barrel for protection. Sets and two pair remain.
2c: brick. BTN bets 90%. The range: K-x better-kicker value (K8, K5) plus sets (88, 55, 22) plus K-x delayed bluffs (blocked by Kh) plus non-K air. K6 loses to too much value.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: The tension between a real positive blocker (Kh is genuinely helpful) and a thin kicker (6 loses to all K-x value) requires precise threshold reasoning. Both factors must be weighed simultaneously.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Folding K6 avoids the loss from calling into a value-weighted range where K6's kicker fails at showdown, even accounting for the partial K-x bluff reduction from Kh.
coaching_context.follow_up: Study which K-x kickers are above threshold at 90% sizing on K-high boards — when does the Kh blocker benefit tip the decision?

6h has no meaningful blocker impact.
But Kh does NOT block K8s, K5s, 88, 55 — the value hands that beat hero. Blocking bluffs helps; it just does not help enough at this kicker depth and sizing.

title: T8 BB — Thin Trips Fold on Paired T River vs Passive Pool
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. You hold Td 8c. BTN c-bets 50% on Th 7d 3c, checks back turn 2s, then bets 75% pot on river Tc after you check. Effective stacks 100bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero makes trip tens (Td on Th-7-3-2-Tc) but the kicker is 8 — below the threshold for calling a passive recreational pool. The turn check-through is the key filter: BTN skipped two-barrel opportunities with hands like T9 and AT that would normally fire twice. That removes some natural value, yes — but in passive pools, the residual bluff density is also very low. Passive players check back the turn and then bet the river with strength, not as a delayed bluff. Hero's Td does reduce villain's Tx value combos (T7s, T3s combos with Td are gone), but that blocker is insufficient when the bluff side is already thin. Trips with an 8 kicker does not clear the calling threshold here. Against a competent regular who double-delays correctly, the call is reasonable — the bluff density would be higher.

A.explanation: A competent regular checks back the turn with delayed floats (J9, 98, 65) and fires the river after the top card pairs to represent a boat. That bluff frequency is real. Hero's Td blocks some Tx value combos. Trips with an 8 kicker is a marginal call that earns a small positive EV against a balanced river betting range.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero has trip tens with an 8 kicker and folds. What is the deciding factor — kicker quality alone, or something else?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: pool-adjusted threshold on paired top-card river
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The deciding factor is the intersection of kicker quality and pool type. An 8 kicker is marginal, but the fold becomes clear when the pool under-bluffs rivers structurally — passive players simply do not reach the river with enough delayed air to make the call profitable.

options.label: Trips is always a call — you cannot fold three of a kind to a 75% bet.
The 8 kicker is marginal, and passive pools fire rivers with too little bluff frequency to make that kicker profitable — both factors combine into the fold.
When the board pairs the top card, villain always has a boat, so folding anything less than a full house is correct.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Td, which removes some of villain's Tx value combos. Does that blocker change the decision?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker effect vs structural bluff deficit
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The Td removes combos like Td7x and Td3x from villain's value range. At GTO frequencies this is a meaningful nudge toward calling. Against a passive pool, the bluff frequency is the binding constraint — even with reduced value combos, the ratio of bluffs to value does not reach the calling threshold.

options.label: Holding the Td removes enough value combos to flip the decision — this is a call.
The Td removes a few value combos but the structural bluff deficit in passive pools still makes folding correct.
Blockers only matter before the river — on the river you just count value vs. bluffs.

coaching_context.key_concept: Kicker quality and pool type jointly determine whether thin trips is a call or fold on a paired top-card river.
common_mistakes: Auto-calling trips without checking pool bluff frequency.
Treating the blocker effect as decisive when the structural bluff deficit is the dominant factor.

value_buckets.label: Slow-played boats
value_buckets.note: Passive players slow-play sets and two-pair more than GTO — these arrive at the river at elevated frequency.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Elevated vs GTO in passive pools

value_buckets.label: Trips with better kicker
value_buckets.note: Many of these two-barrel turns for protection, but some check back — they beat hero's 8 kicker.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Reduced by turn check but still present

bluff_buckets.label: Delayed floats
bluff_buckets.note: GTO villain fires these after a free turn. Passive players give up much more often — the bluff density is structurally low.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Below GTO frequency in passive pools

bluff_catchers.label: Trips with 8 kicker — hero hand
bluff_catchers.note: Above all delayed floats in hand strength, but the pool does not generate enough bluffs to justify the call.
bluff_catchers.frequency_hint: Fold vs Pool B; marginal call vs Pool A

threshold_notes: T9 trips would be a closer call even vs Pool B — the extra pip of kicker matters near the threshold.
The turn check does reduce some natural value, but it also signals the passive player did not want to build a pot — consistent with slow-play or marginal holding, not delayed bluff.
blocker_notes: Td removes Td7s and Td3x combos from villain's two-pair/trips value — a minor positive that matters more at GTO.
Against passive pools, the blocker is not load-bearing; pool adjustment is.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Thin trips, pool-contingent threshold
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Fold vs passive pool; marginal call vs competent regular
hero_hand_bucket.note: The hand is not the deciding factor — the opponent type is.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on T-7-3 keeps BTN's full range alive — all Tx value (AT, KT, T7, T3), sets (77, 33), and backdoor floats (J9, 98, 65). No range-narrowing signal yet.

2s blank check-back removes hands most motivated to two-barrel for protection — AT, KT that want to charge draws and collect equity. What survives: slow-played boats (T7s, T3s, 77, 33) plus delayed floats (J9, 98, 65). In passive pools, the check also signals comfort with showdown rather than delayed bluff construction.

Tc pairs the top card. Value tightens toward boats (T7s, T3s, 77, 33) while the delayed float bluff region is largely unchanged. In passive pools the bluff density after a turn check-back is already thin — the structural deficit is the binding factor, not the hand strength label alone.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study where the kicker threshold shifts on paired-top-card rivers after turn x/x: is T9 a call vs Pool B, and at what kicker does the pool-adjusted fold threshold kick in?
