title: J8 BB — Top Pair Calls Triple-Barrel on Ace River vs Pool A
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. You hold Jc 8s. BTN bets 50% flop on Jh 9d 5c, bets 66% turn on 3s, then fires 75% pot on river Ad after you check twice. Effective stacks 80bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero holds Jc8s — top pair with an 8 kicker — on a Jh-9d-5c-3s-Ad board. The ace is a scare card that benefits villain's bluffing range more than his value range. Against a competent regular who three-barrels, the ace river is exactly when QT, T8, and Q8 turn into air — those hands wanted the straight that never came, and the ace gives them a representable card. Villain's actual value range (AJ, A9, sets, two-pair) is narrower than it looks because hero's Jc blocks some AJ combos and because not all AJ combos barrel three times on J-9-5-3. The 75% sizing is balanced — villain uses it for value and bluffs. Hero has top pair with an 8 kicker, which is thin, but it beats the entire bluff region. Call against pool A.

B.explanation: A passive recreational player who three-barrels into an ace river is heavily weighted toward value — AJ, A9, sets, two-pair. They do not construct delayed bluff sequences with QT or T8 that fire three times after the ace kills their straight draw. Against pool B, hero's top pair with an 8 kicker is well below the calling threshold. Fold.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Which hands in BTN's range turn into bluffs on the ace river after two-barreling, and why does that matter for hero's call?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: ace river bluff candidate identification
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: QT, T8, and Q8 all two-barrel with straight draw equity but miss on the ace river. These hands become natural bluff candidates on the ace river against a pool A villain who understands that the ace is a good bluffing card — it represents AJ, A9 value that hero would need to fold weaker holdings to.

options.label: When the ace rivers, villain always has it — QT and T8 would have given up on the turn.
QT, T8, Q8 had straight draws on J-9-5-3 and missed. Against Pool A they fire the ace river as a bluff — hero's top pair beats all of them.
The ace helps hero because it reduces villain's bluff range — hands with aces become value, not bluffs.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Why does the correct answer flip between call and fold based purely on opponent pool?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: pool-contingent calling threshold on ace river triple-barrel
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The pool changes the composition of the betting range. Pool A constructs delayed bluff sequences with missed straight draws and fires ace rivers. Pool B does not — they reach river three-bets with value-heavy ranges. The hand (J8) does not change, but the required bluff frequency to call does.

options.label: Top pair is top pair — the pool type does not change whether J8 is a call or fold.
Pool A generates enough triple-barrel bluffs on ace rivers to make J8 profitable. Pool B does not — so the same hand flips from call to fold.
The decision only depends on the bet size, not the pool — any 75% bet requires the same call frequency.

coaching_context.key_concept: Ace river triple-barrels from pool A contain a meaningful bluff region from missed straight draws — top pair with a weak kicker can call.
common_mistakes: Folding top pair on ace rivers because the ace looks scary without asking who bluffs ace rivers.
Calling the same hand vs passive pools who don't bluff enough.

value_buckets.label: Ace-top pair and two pair
value_buckets.note: AJ is villain's nut hand here. Partially blocked by hero's Jc. A9s, A5s pick up two pair on the river.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Core value region

value_buckets.label: Sets and made two pair
value_buckets.note: Sets and early two pair that three-barrel. Lower combo count than it looks.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Present but not dominant

bluff_buckets.label: Missed open-ended and gutshot straight draws
bluff_buckets.note: Had 8-out or 4-out equity on J-9-5-3 and fired two streets. Miss on ace. Natural river bluffs vs Pool A.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Primary bluff constituency in Pool A

bluff_catchers.label: Top pair 8 kicker — hero hand
bluff_catchers.note: Beats all missed draws. Jc partially blocks AJ combos. Call vs Pool A; fold vs Pool B.

threshold_notes: Hero needs villain to bluff at least ~40% of the river betting range for the call to be profitable at 75% sizing.
Pool A generates that frequency via missed draws. Pool B does not.
blocker_notes: Jc removes JcAx combos from villain's AJ — reduces some value combos.
8s has minimal blocker effect on J-9-5-3-A board.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Top pair weak kicker, pool-contingent call
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Call vs Pool A; fold vs Pool B
hero_hand_bucket.note: The hand sits right at the threshold — pool type is the deciding variable.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on J-9-5 builds the pot with a wide range: Jx value (AJ, KJ, J9, J5), sets (99, 55), and equity draws (QT, T8, Q8 all have 8 outs on an open-ended board). Nothing is filtered yet.

66% second barrel on 3s blank. The 3s changes nothing structurally. This barrel removes weak floats and overcards without equity while preserving: Jx value that needs continued protection and semi-bluffs (QT, T8, Q8 still have 8 live outs). Both halves of the range survive the turn.

Ad arrives. This is one of the best bluffing cards for a pool A villain: it gives missed draws (QT, T8, Q8) a credible story representing AJ or A9, while AJ and A9 become genuine two-pair value. The ace benefits the bluffing half of the range almost as much as the value half — rare symmetry that supports calling.

title: 87 BB — Second Pair Folds When Ace Rivers on K-High Board
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. You hold 8d 7s. BTN bets 50% flop on Kh 8c 4d, checks back turn 2s, then bets 75% pot on river Ac after you check. Effective stacks 100bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero holds 8d7s — second pair with a 7 kicker — on Kh-8c-4d-2s-Ac. The ace rivers in one of the worst spots possible for hero: second pair, weak kicker, against a passive pool that checked back the turn. Passive players who bet-check-bet on a K-high board are not constructing elaborate bluff sequences — they have AK for top two pair, A8 for two pair, sets of 8s or 4s they slow-played, or AK that checked back the turn because they did not need protection. The ace crushes hero's holding: 8d7s does not beat a single value combo in villain's river betting range. There is no implied 7-kicker advantage here — hero's second pair is simply below threshold. Even versus pool A, 8d7s would be a fold: the ace river blocks hero's usual calling range and 87 cannot beat anything in villain's plausible value region. Fold clearly.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero has second pair with a 7 kicker. What would need to be true for this hand to be a profitable call?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: required bluff frequency for second pair on ace river
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: For 8d7s to be a call, villain would need to bluff at least ~40% of river betting range at 75% sizing. Passive players who bet-check-bet reach the river with near-zero bluffs. Even at GTO, 87 offsuit on a K-8-4-2-A board barely has enough equity against the bluff region to justify calling because the ace directly hits villain's value range — AK, A8 two pair, A4 two pair.

options.label: Second pair is never a fold to one bet — you always need to peel at least once.
For 87 to call, villain would need to bluff around 40% of the time — passive pools after bet-check-bet on an ace river are nowhere near that frequency.
The ace on the river blocks villain's overpair hands like AA, so hero can call more liberally.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: BTN checked back the turn on a blank 2s. How should that inform your read of the river bet?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn check-back signal in passive pool context
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: In passive pools, the turn check on a blank indicates the villain was not motivated to build a big pot — often because they hold middle pairs, weaker K-x, or floated the flop planning to give up. When an ace rivers and the same passive player bets, it is rarely because they constructed a delayed bluff — it is because the ace gave them two pair or top pair. The turn check signals range composition that now fires value on the ace.

options.label: The turn check means villain has lots of delayed bluffs that now fire the ace as a bluff — hero can call.
In passive pools, a check-back on a blank turn followed by an ace river bet signals the ace improved villain's hand — not a delayed bluff.
BTN checked back, so his range is capped — hero should never fold to capped ranges.

coaching_context.key_concept: Second pair with a weak kicker on an ace river is a clear fold against passive pools — the question is just identifying why quickly.
common_mistakes: Calling second pair because 'it is only one street' without checking the pool and the ace river dynamics.
Confusing a delayed bluff read (pool A) with passive pool behavior after a turn check.

value_buckets.label: Ace-high two pair
value_buckets.note: AK is the nut hand here. A8s, A4s pick up two pair on the ace river. These are the primary bet combos for a passive player.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: High frequency — ace river strongly hits BTN's opening range

value_buckets.label: Slow-played sets
value_buckets.note: Sets of 8s and 4s that checked back the turn and now value bet the river.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low count but high strength

bluff_buckets.label: Delayed floats (structural minimum)
bluff_buckets.note: Hands that floated the flop c-bet and fired back after a free turn. In passive pools this is a very thin category.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Near-zero in passive pools after bet-check-bet

bluff_catchers.label: Second pair 7 kicker — hero hand
bluff_catchers.note: Loses to the entire value region. Only beats the thin bluff category that passive pools barely generate.
bluff_catchers.frequency_hint: Clear fold in all pool contexts

threshold_notes: This is not a close threshold spot — hero's holding is well below fold threshold in all pool types.
The primary learning is speed of recognition: second pair with a weak kicker on an ace river versus a passive bet-check-bet line is a fast fold.
blocker_notes: 8d blocks some sets of 8s (8d8h, 8d8s, 8d8c) — minor reduction in villain's nut hands.
7s has no significant blocker effect on this board.
The blocker help is small and does not change the fold decision.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Second pair below threshold
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Clear fold — not a close spot
hero_hand_bucket.note: Recognizing the fast fold quickly is the skill being tested here, not deliberation.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on K-8-4. BTN's range includes all K-x hands (AK, KQ, KJ, K7, K3), sets (88, 44), and backdoor floats (A7, A6, A5 type hands floating to improve). Hero calls with second pair, keeping a wide range in the BB.

2s blank. BTN checks back on a dry K-8-4-2 board. This signals BTN was not motivated to build the pot — in passive pools, this check reflects comfort with showdown (marginal K-x) or a float without strong equity. Ax backdoor hands that floated now get a free turn while keeping all their two-pair outs live.

Ac rivers. The ace directly hits the hands that floated with Ax equity: AK becomes top two pair, A8 becomes two pair, A4 becomes two pair, A2 becomes two pair. In passive pools, a bet after a turn check-back on the ace river is almost pure value — passive players do not construct delayed bluff sequences after skipping a turn bet opportunity.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study K8 on this same line — hero's second pair would be 8s rather than 7s, with an 8 blocker to sets. Does holding the 8 materially change the fold, and what does the pool context do to that blocker edge?

title: J6 BB — Blocker-Driven Call on Paired J River
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. You hold Js 6d. BTN c-bets 40% on Jc 8h 4d, checks back turn 3s, then bets 80% pot on river Jh after you check. Effective stacks 100bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero holds Js6d and makes trip jacks on the Jh river — with a 6 kicker, which is thin. The key blocker fact: with Js in hand and Jc and Jh on the board, only Jd remains in the deck. Villain cannot have any Jx hand except those containing Jd. This removes JJ (impossible — three jacks accounted for), J8s (only Jd8x), J4s (only Jd4x), J3s (only Jd3s) from villain's value range. The value region is significantly compressed. Against a competent regular who checks back the turn and fires the Jh river, the range contains slow-played 88, 44, delayed floats (T9, 97, 65, Q9), and very few remaining Jx value combos. Hero's trips with the Js is doing two things at once: it holds a bluff-catching hand that beats all floats, and it blocks the primary value region so decisively that the call becomes positive. Six kicker is thin on a standard paired top-card river, but the Js blocker elevates this call above where a non-Js holding would sit.

B.explanation: Against a passive recreational pool, the check-back turn signals a range that fires the Jh river for value — slow-played sets (88, 44) and Jd8x, Jd4x two pair. The blocker is real, but passive players do not construct the delayed float sequences that Pool A uses. The remaining bluff density after accounting for passive tendencies may not cover the 6-kicker at 80% sizing. This is a fold or mix against Pool B.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Js with Jc and Jh on the board. What does that leave for villain's Jx value hands?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: exhaustive jack blocker mechanics
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Three jacks are visible: Jc (board), Jh (board), Js (hero). Only Jd remains in the deck. Villain's only Jx hand must contain Jd: Jd8x (J8s one combo), Jd4x (J4s one combo), Jd3x (J3s one combo), and JdAx. This is a massive reduction from the 12 Jx combos normally possible. The value region is nearly emptied of Jx, leaving sets and two-pair as the primary value.

options.label: Hero has trips, so villain cannot also have a stronger trips — the blocker is minor.
With Jc and Jh on board and Js in hand, villain can only have Jd — reducing Jx value combos from 12 to a handful containing Jd only.
When three jacks are on the board, villain is always going for a boat — trips with any kicker should fold.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: The 6 kicker is weak. How does the Js blocker interact with that weakness to determine the overall decision?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker-elevated threshold on paired top-card river
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Without the Js, trips with a 6 kicker on a paired J river would likely be a fold at 80% sizing — the kicker does not clear the threshold when the value region is full. The Js blocker collapses the value region so substantially that the residual betting range becomes bluff-heavy enough to make the thin kicker profitable. The blocker does the heavy lifting the kicker cannot.

options.label: The 6 kicker is the binding constraint — no blocker makes trips with a 6 kicker a call at 80%.
The Js blocker reduces villain's value range so sharply that the residual range is bluff-heavy — the blocker compensates for the weak kicker and supports the call.
Trips is always a call regardless of kicker — the threshold is hand class, not kicker.

coaching_context.key_concept: A near-exhaustive blocker on the top card can compensate for a weak kicker by collapsing the value region.
common_mistakes: Folding trips with a weak kicker without checking how many value combos the blocker actually removes.
Treating all 'trips with weak kicker' spots as equivalent — the specific card matters enormously.

value_buckets.label: Sets (slow-played)
value_buckets.note: 88 and 44 checked back turn and now bet the river as boats. Only 3 combos of each.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Narrow but high strength

value_buckets.label: Jd-x two pair (only remaining Jx value)
value_buckets.note: The only Jx combos possible for villain. Severely reduced from the normal universe of 12 Jx combos.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Drastically compressed by Js blocker

bluff_buckets.label: Delayed floats that fire paired Jh river
bluff_buckets.note: These combos floated the small flop c-bet, checked back turn with showdown, then fire the Jh river as a bluff representing a boat.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Primary bluff constituency — elevated relative to value after Js blocker

bluff_catchers.label: Trips with 6 kicker — hero hand
bluff_catchers.note: Beats all delayed floats. Loses only to 88, 44, and the few Jdx value combos. The Js blocker makes this profitable.

threshold_notes: The standard J-kicker threshold on a paired J river at 80% sizing sits around J9 or better. Js6d clears that bar because the blocker inflates the bluff-to-value ratio.
Without the Js, J6o would be a clear fold at 80% — the blocker is the margin.
blocker_notes: Js with Jc + Jh on board = only Jd left in deck. This is a near-complete block on Jx value.
The blocker effect here is one of the strongest examples in the bluff-catch lane — 75% of normal Jx combos removed by a single held card.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Thin trips elevated by exhaustive blocker
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Call vs Pool A; mix or fold vs Pool B
hero_hand_bucket.note: This hand illustrates how blockers interact with threshold — the specific card changes the answer more than the kicker does.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 40% small c-bet on J-8-4 is a range-bet style — keeps BTN's entire range alive at low cost. All backdoor draws (T9, Q9, 97, 65), Jx value, and equity hands (T9s open-ended) float without committing. No significant filtering at this size.

3s blank check-back removes hands most motivated to two-barrel for protection — AJ and KJ that want to charge T9 and Q9 for their equity. What survives: slow-played 88 and 44, Jdx two pair (the only Jx combos possible for villain with Jc + Js + Jh taken), and delayed floats (T9, Q9, 97) that preferred a free card.

Jh pairs the top card. With Js in hand and Jc + Jh on board, only Jd remains for villain's Jx combos — a near-total block on Jx value. The value region collapses to 88, 44 (slow-played boats), and the handful of Jd-x combinations. Delayed floats (T9, Q9, 97) are fully intact and fire the paired river to represent a boat, making the bluff-to-value ratio strongly positive despite the 6 kicker.

coaching_context.follow_up: Find the kicker threshold on this board without the Js blocker — if hero held Q6 instead of J6, trips would not be available, but what would the Jd on river mean for a top-pair Q6 holding?

title: 98 BB — Blocker to Sets Supports Brick River Call vs Overbet
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. You hold 9s 8c. BTN bets 50% flop on Kc 9h 5d, bets 66% turn on 4c, then overbets 120% pot on brick river 2s after you check. Effective stacks 80bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero holds 9s8c — second pair — on Kc-9h-5d-4c-2s. The river is a brick. Villain overbets 120% pot after two streets of betting. This sizing polarizes villain's range: it is either a strong made hand or a bluff. The blocker analysis matters here: hero's 9s removes two of three combos of set-of-nines from villain's range (9s9c and 9s9d are impossible; only 9c9d remains). That is a 67% reduction in villain's strongest value on this board. Additionally, the 4c turn created flush draw equity for hands like AcQc, AcJc, QcJc — those hands that picked up 9 outs on the 4c turn and now arrive on the 2s river with zero equity and a natural incentive to bluff with the overbet. Hero's second pair beats the entire bluff region. The overbet says 'I have KK or I'm bluffing' — and the 9s blocker reduces the set-of-nines side of the value range meaningfully enough to support a call against pool A.

B.explanation: Passive recreational players rarely construct overbet bluffs with missed flush draws. An overbet from a passive player on a brick river after two streets of betting is heavily weighted toward KK, 99 (partially blocked by hero), or K9s. Even with hero's 9s reducing the set-of-nines range, the bluff frequency does not reach the calling threshold for an overbet. Fold second pair vs Pool B.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds 9s. 9h is on the board. How many combos of set-of-nines can villain hold, and what does that reduction mean for the call?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: precise blocker removal on made hand that competes with hero's holding
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: 9h is on the board and hero has 9s. Villain's potential 99 combos: 9s9c (impossible — hero has 9s), 9s9d (impossible), 9c9d (possible — this is the only remaining combo). Hero's 9s reduces set-of-nines from 3 combos to 1 — a 67% reduction in that specific value hand. This meaningfully shifts the bluff-to-value ratio upward.

options.label: With 9h on the board, villain can never have a set of nines anyway.
Hero's 9s removes two of three 99 combos, leaving only 9c9d. That 67% reduction in villain's primary value supports the call.
Blockers only matter when you have the nuts — holding second pair, the blocker cannot make a call correct.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Villain overbets 120% pot on a brick river after two streets of betting. What two-part range does that sizing most likely represent?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: overbet polarization on brick river
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: An overbet on a brick river after a two-barrel line polarizes to: (1) very strong hands — KK, remaining set of nines, K9s two pair; and (2) missed flush draws or straight draws choosing the overbet as a bluff. The sizing eliminates thin value — thin value does not use overbets. Against pool A, the bluff half of that polarized range is real and substantial.

options.label: Overbets are always value — no one bluffs 120% pot after three streets.
An overbet on a brick river is polarized: very strong value or missed draws as bluffs. Pool A constructs this range — hero must assess the bluff proportion.
A large bet is a blocking bet — villain is trying to buy a cheap showdown.

coaching_context.key_concept: A precise blocker to a specific value hand can shift the bluff-to-value ratio enough to support a call with second pair against a polarized overbet.
common_mistakes: Folding all second-pair holdings to overbets without first checking blocker reductions.
Treating 'overbet = value' as a rule rather than a signal that requires decomposition.

value_buckets.label: Sets and strong two pair
value_buckets.note: KK is the nut hand. 9c9d is the only remaining set-of-nines (hero blocks others). K9s and K5s make two pair. Lower total count than it appears.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — blocker significantly reduces 99 count

bluff_buckets.label: Missed flush draws (4c turn created them)
bluff_buckets.note: These hands picked up 9-out flush draws on the 4c turn and miss on 2s. Natural overbet bluffs in pool A.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Primary bluff constituency — elevated by 4c turn

bluff_buckets.label: Missed gutshots and overcards
bluff_buckets.note: Floated the flop with equity, double-barreled with backdoors, and arrive with nothing on the brick river.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Secondary bluff constituency

bluff_catchers.label: Second pair — hero hand
bluff_catchers.note: Beats all bluffs. The 9s blocker reduces the value range materially. Call vs Pool A; fold vs Pool B.
bluff_catchers.frequency_hint: Call vs Pool A at 120% sizing

threshold_notes: At 120% pot, hero needs villain to bluff approximately 55% of the polarized range to break even.
Missed flush draws from the 4c turn (4+ combos) plus missed gutshots approach that threshold in pool A.
blocker_notes: 9s removes 9s9c and 9s9d from villain's sets. Only 9c9d remains (1 combo vs normal 3).
8c provides minor blocker effect — villain cannot hold 8c flush draw combos.
The 9s is the material blocker; the 8c is secondary.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Second pair with blocker to primary value
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Call vs Pool A; fold vs Pool B
hero_hand_bucket.note: The 9s blocker is doing real work — without it, second pair would fold to an overbet even in pool A.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on K-9-5. Range is wide: KK, K9s, 99 (set of nines — 3 combos pre-hero's 9s), 55, K5s, and backdoor draws. The 9h on board already limits set-of-nines to 3 combos; hero's 9s will reduce it further.

4c turn is the critical event. 66% barrel after the 4c creates flush draws for Ac-suited hands (AcQc, AcJc, QcJc, Ac8c). These hands pick up 9-out flush draws and barrel correctly with equity. The 4c reloads the bluff bucket — hands that were pure air on the flop now have genuine equity.

2s brick. All flush draws from the 4c turn miss. AcQc, AcJc, QcJc arrive at the river with nothing and a natural incentive to overbet as a bluff representing the top of the polarized range. Hero's 9s reduces set-of-nines to 1 combo (9c9d only), further collapsing the value side of the overbet's polarized range.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study what changes on this spot if the turn is 4s instead of 4c — no flush draws are created, the bluff bucket does not reload, and the overbet call becomes much thinner. At what point does second pair fold to the overbet without the flush draw bluff inventory?

title: KT BB — Top Pair Folds Brick River When Hero Blocks Bluffs
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. You hold Kd Ts. BTN bets 50% flop on Kh 7d 4c, checks back turn 3s, then bets 75% pot on river 2h after you check. Effective stacks 100bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero holds KdTs — top pair K, T kicker — on Kh-7d-4c-3s-2h. The board is a brick river. Versus a passive pool, the fold is supported by two converging factors. First, passive players who bet-check-bet on a dry K-high board are heavily value-weighted — they checked back the turn because they did not need protection (K7s, K4s, sets of 7s or 4s) and now fire when checked to on the river. Second, the Ts in hero's hand actually removes several natural bluff candidates from villain's range. On K-7-4-3-2, the main bluff hands would be hands with T-connected equity: JTs, T9s, T8s. Hero holds Ts, which removes those suited-T combos entirely from villain's possible bluffs. Fewer bluff combos plus a passive pool that under-bluffs structurally equals a fold for top pair T-kicker — even though KT is 'strong-looking.' The lesson: blockers can confirm a fold as well as enable a call.

A.explanation: Against a competent regular, the check-back turn still preserves delayed floats (Q9, J8, 86, 65) that fire the brick river. Even though hero's Ts removes some T-high bluff combos, pool A generates enough other bluffs (Q-high, J-high missed draws) to make KT a call. The fold vs Pool B; call vs Pool A split shows how blockers interact differently across pools.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: How does holding Ts make folding top pair easier on this board — rather than harder?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker that removes bluffs rather than value — fold-confirming
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Normally blockers help hero call by removing villain's value combos. Here, the Ts removes hero's own bluff-catching equity: JTs, T9s, T8s are the natural bluffing hands on K-7-4-3-2, and hero holds Ts, making all suited-T bluffs impossible for villain. The bluff region shrinks because hero's card prevents villain from having those hands. This is a fold-confirming blocker — it reduces bluff frequency rather than value frequency.

options.label: Ts blocks villain's T-high value hands like KT — so hero has fewer hands to lose to.
Hero's Ts removes JTs, T9s, T8s from villain's possible bluffs — making the bluff region thinner and the fold cleaner.
Blockers always support calls — if you block something, you call more.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: What does a passive player's bet-check-bet pattern on a dry K-high board with brick streets tell you about their range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: passive pool bet-check-bet pattern interpretation
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: A passive recreational player who bets the flop, checks the turn on a blank, and then bets the river after being checked to is almost certainly not constructing a delayed bluff sequence. The most natural passive river betting range here is: K-x two pair (K7, K4), sets slow-played, and possibly thin K-x that prefers one bet. The turn check signals comfort — not uncertainty — in a passive player's range.

options.label: A turn check from a passive player means they are weak — the river bet is a desperation bluff hero can call.
Passive players who bet-check-bet on dry boards are predominantly doing so for value — two pair or better — not bluffing.
Only the bet size matters — 75% is a medium bet, so hero can always call medium bets.

coaching_context.key_concept: Blockers can confirm folds by removing bluff combos from villain's range — the direction of the blocker effect determines whether it supports calling or folding.
common_mistakes: Assuming all blockers support calls — sometimes your card removes the bluffs, not the value.
Calling top pair on brick rivers versus passive pools because 'the board is dry.'

value_buckets.label: K-x two pair
value_buckets.note: Two pair that checked turn (did not need protection) and now bets river for value. Core passive-pool bet range.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Primary value — elevated in passive pools

value_buckets.label: Sets (slow-played)
value_buckets.note: Sets of 7s and 4s that checked back the turn to let hero catch up, now bet the river.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low combo count, high strength

bluff_buckets.label: Remaining non-T-suited floats
bluff_buckets.note: Hands that floated the small flop c-bet and fire the brick river. Lower volume than normal because hero's Ts removes T-connected suited combos.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Reduced by Ts blocker; further reduced by passive pool tendency

bluff_catchers.label: Top pair T kicker — hero hand
bluff_catchers.note: Strong-looking hand, but bluff density is thin. Fold vs Pool B; call vs Pool A.

threshold_notes: KT is above the threshold against a GTO-calibrated regular whose bluff region is full.
Against Pool B with a Ts blocker removing T-connected bluffs, the bluff density falls below the 40% required for a 75% bet call.
blocker_notes: Ts removes JTs, T9s, T8s, T7s from villain's suited bluff candidates — these are the most natural bluffing hands on K-7-4-3-2.
Kd slightly reduces AKd combos for villain (minor positive, less relevant since AK would mostly be value-bet).
Net blocker direction: fold-confirming, not call-enabling.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Top pair fold — blocker removes own bluff-catching equity
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Fold vs Pool B; call vs Pool A
hero_hand_bucket.note: This is the blocker paradox: a strong hand folds because it removes the bluffs it needed villain to have.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on K-7-4 is a standard opening board. BTN's range includes all K-x value (AK, KQ, KJ, K7, K4), sets (77, 44), and backdoor floats (JT, T9, T8, J9 type hands that play for equity). The dry board structure means equity draws are limited — floats are mostly air hoping to bluff later.

3s blank check-back on K-7-4-3. BTN did not want to build the pot. In passive pools this signals comfort with showdown — likely marginal K-x or an air float that lost motivation. T-connected hands (JTs, T9s, T8s) were BTN's natural bluff candidates, but hero's Ts removes those exact combos, making the float inventory thinner than it looks.

2h brick. K-7-4-3-2 final board. BTN fires 75% after checking back a blank turn. In passive pools, this is value: K7s, K4s, K3s, K2s two pair, or slow-played sets. Hero's Ts removes JTs, T9s, T8s from villain's possible bluff candidates — the bluff region was already thin structurally, and the blocker confirms the fold rather than enabling a call.

coaching_context.follow_up: Compare this spot to holding K9 instead of KT on the same board — the 9 does not block T-connected bluffs the way Ts does. Does K9 become a call vs Pool B, or does the passive pool adjustment still dominate?

title: J8 BB — Top Pair Calls King Scare River vs Triple-Barrel Pool A
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. You hold Jc 8d. BTN bets 50% flop on Jd 9h 5c, bets 50% turn on 3s, then bets 75% pot on river Kh after you check twice. Effective stacks 80bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero holds Jc8d — top pair with an 8 kicker — on Jd-9h-5c-3s-Kh. The king is a scare card that is not an ace. Against a competent regular who has been three-barreling, the king river is one of the best bluffing cards available to BTN: it represents KJ, K9, KK for value while turning the missed QT, T8, and Q8 straight draws into credible bluffing hands that can now represent the king. Villain's actual value range on KK on the river is narrower than it looks: KJ loses some combos to hero's Jc, sets (99, 55) were a small starting range, and KK is possible but thin. The three-barrel line against a J-9-5 board with a 3 turn and K river is structurally consistent with bluffing. Hero has top pair — it beats the entire bluff region. At 75% sizing, hero needs villain to bluff approximately 40% of the river betting range. Against pool A three-barreling through a king river, that frequency is met. Call.

B.explanation: A passive recreational player who three-barrels through a king river is not constructing a balanced bluffing range with missed QT and T8. They have value — KJ, K9, KK, sets. Hero's top pair with an 8 kicker is well below the threshold against a passive pool that does not generate enough scare-card bluffs. Fold.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: The king rivers rather than an ace. How does that change the bluff-catch calculus compared to an ace river?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: king scare vs ace scare — bluff density and value density comparison
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: An ace river is a stronger scare because it hits BTN's opening range more densely (AX hands are plentiful). A king river is slightly less dense in value (KX is still strong but BTN opens fewer KX combos by proportion). Crucially, the king river still creates new bluffing opportunities from missed QT, T8, Q8 straight draws that now represent KQ, KT. The bluff logic is similar — scare card = bluff opportunity — even if the value density is slightly lower than an ace river.

options.label: King and ace rivers are interchangeable scare cards — the logic is identical.
The king river creates bluff opportunities from missed draws just like an ace river, but with slightly less value density — the bluff-catch logic still applies.
Only aces are scare cards on J-high boards — kings do not change the analysis.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: BTN three-barrels on J-9-5-3-K. Which value hands survive three streets, and does that support calling or folding?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: three-barrel value vs bluff composition on J-high board
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Value that three-barrels: KJ (new two pair, some combos blocked by hero's Jc), K9 (new two pair), sets of 9s and 5s, possibly KK. But BTN's opening range includes a large volume of straight-draw candidates (QT, T8, Q8) that two-barrel with equity and then face the king river with nothing — these become bluffs. The bluff volume vs value volume is roughly balanced for a pool A regular, supporting the call.

options.label: No one three-barrels without value — if BTN bets three streets, they have KJ or better.
Value (KJ, K9, sets) three-barrels for protection and value, while missed straight draws (QT, T8) three-barrel the king as a bluff — the range is balanced enough to call against Pool A.
Three barrels with a bet-check-bet line would be bluffing — but villain here bet all three streets so they have value.

coaching_context.key_concept: Scare card rivers create bluffing opportunities for pool A regardless of whether the scare card is an ace — king rivers are structurally similar.
common_mistakes: Folding top pair to king rivers because 'villain has a king' without asking whether missed draws also use that card as a bluff.
Treating non-ace scare cards as non-threatening to hero's bluff-catch range.

value_buckets.label: King river two pair and sets
value_buckets.note: KJ and K9s pick up two pair on the king river. Sets and KK complete villain's value range. Some blocked by hero's Jc.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Core value region — moderate density

bluff_buckets.label: Missed straight draws — natural king river bluffs
bluff_buckets.note: These hands two-barrel with open-ended or gutshot equity on J-9-5-3 and arrive at the king river with nothing. Pool A fires the king as a bluff representing KQ, KT.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Primary bluff constituency in Pool A

bluff_catchers.label: Top pair 8 kicker — hero hand
bluff_catchers.note: Beats all missed draw bluffs. Jc blocks some KJ combos. Call vs Pool A at 75% sizing.
bluff_catchers.frequency_hint: Call vs Pool A; fold vs Pool B

threshold_notes: At 75% pot, hero needs villain to bluff ~40% of the river range.
Pool A's missed straight draw inventory on J-9-5-3 is sufficient to reach that threshold on the king river.
blocker_notes: Jc blocks KJc combos — reducing villain's two-pair value slightly.
8d has minimal blocker effect on this board.
Net effect: mild blocker positive on value side.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Top pair on scare king river
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Call vs Pool A; fold vs Pool B
hero_hand_bucket.note: The key lesson is that scare cards create bluffing opportunities regardless of denomination — not just on aces.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on J-9-5. The board is draw-heavy: BTN's range includes strong made hands (JJ, 99, 55, J9s, J5s, 95s) and a large equity-draw constituency (QT, T8, Q8 all have 8 outs for an open-ended straight). Both halves are well-represented.

50% second barrel on 3s blank. The 3s does not resolve any draws. Villain's continued betting narrows to: strong value that wants protection (sets, two pair) and semi-bluffs that still have 8 live outs (QT, T8, Q8). Both categories survive — the turn barrel preserves the full bluff inventory going into the river.

Kh arrives. QT, T8, and Q8 had equity for two streets and all miss on the king. The king creates a bluffing story: villain can represent KJ, K9, KK. Pool A villains fire the king as a bluff precisely because it is a high-equity bluffing card — represents plausible new value while disguising the missed draw. The king river inflates the bluff density, not just the value density.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study T8 on the same board (J-9-5-3-K) — T8 missed its straight draw and villain three-barreled. How does evaluating the bluff inventory from hero's perspective (holding T8) change the call/fold calculation for hero if he were on the other side?

title: T6 BB — Top Pair Folds Queen Scare River vs Passive Bet-Check-Bet
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. You hold Ts 6c. BTN bets 50% flop on Th 7d 4c, checks back turn 2s, then bets 75% pot on river Qh after you check. Effective stacks 100bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero holds Ts6c — top pair with a 6 kicker — on Th-7d-4c-2s-Qh. The queen rivers. Against a passive recreational pool, the bet-check-bet pattern means: BTN bet the flop for value or protection, checked back the turn without needing more protection, and fires the queen river after hero checks. In passive pools, this sequence on a T-high board with a blank turn and queen river contains hands like QT (now two pair), Q7, Q4, QQ for value, plus slow-played sets of 7s and 4s. Passive players who produce this line do not bluff missed straight draws — they give up. Hero's Ts6c has top pair with a 6 kicker: it loses to all of villain's value and only beats an extremely thin bluff region that passive players do not populate. The queen hitting is not a scare card that creates massive bluffing opportunity for a passive player — it simply gives them new two-pair value. Fold.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: How does the queen on the river change villain's value range, and why does that matter for hero's call?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: queen river value expansion for passive pool villain
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The queen adds QT (two pair), Q7, Q4, and QQ to villain's value range. These are all new value combos that improved on the river. In a passive pool, villain's check-back turn followed by river bet after the queen means these new two-pair hands are the primary betting range — not bluffs with missed draws. Hero's top pair 6 kicker loses to all of this new value.

options.label: The queen creates new bluffing opportunities for villain — hands that had queen-high can now bet as bluffs representing value.
The queen gives villain new two-pair combos (QT, Q7, Q4) that are the primary river betting range in passive pools — hero loses to all of them.
A queen on a T-high board is irrelevant — it doesn't connect with the flop structure.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero has top pair. When is top pair ever a fold to a single bet on the river?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: top pair fold threshold against passive pool value-weighted range
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Top pair folds when the kicker is weak, the villain's betting range is value-heavy, and the pool does not generate enough bluffs to cover the calling frequency. Here all three conditions hold: 6 kicker is near the bottom of hero's range, passive bet-check-bet on a queen river is value-weighted, and passive pools structurally under-bluff. Top pair 6 kicker folds.

options.label: You never fold top pair to a single bet — you need to see a showdown before you can fold top pair.
Top pair folds when kicker is near-bottom, villain's betting range is value-heavy (queen rivers new two pair), and the pool under-bluffs. All three apply here.
Top pair only folds to overbets — 75% is too small to fold top pair to.

coaching_context.key_concept: Non-ace scare cards in passive pools often generate value — not bluffs. Hero's fold of top pair is correct when the scare card directly hits villain's range.
common_mistakes: Calling top pair because 'top pair is strong' without checking what the queen river does to villain's value range.
Assuming all scare cards create bluffing opportunities regardless of pool type.

value_buckets.label: Queen river two pair
value_buckets.note: These combos hit the queen river to make two pair. The primary betting range for passive villain after a check-back turn.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Elevated by queen river in passive pool

value_buckets.label: Slow-played sets
value_buckets.note: Sets of 7s and 4s that checked the turn and now bet the river for value.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low combo count, high strength

bluff_buckets.label: Delayed floats (structural minimum)
bluff_buckets.note: Passive players rarely fire these after a turn check. The bluff density is near zero.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Near-zero in passive pools

bluff_catchers.label: Top pair 6 kicker — hero hand
bluff_catchers.note: Loses to all of villain's value region. Only beats the thin bluff category that passive pools barely generate.

threshold_notes: At 75% sizing, hero needs ~40% bluff frequency to break even. Passive pool on queen river bet-check-bet is near zero.
Even at 50% sizing the fold would be correct — the pool adjustment is the binding factor.
blocker_notes: Ts blocks some QTs combos (hero can't hold Ts if villain does — hero has Ts).
Actually: hero holds Ts, villain cannot have QTs (since Ts is with hero). This removes one suit of QT from villain's range — minor reduction.
The blocker effect is insufficient to change the fold decision.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Top pair near-bottom kicker — clear fold vs passive pool
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Fold — not a close spot
hero_hand_bucket.note: Speed of fold recognition is the primary skill being tested.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on T-7-4. BTN's range includes Tx value (AT, KT, T7, T4), sets of 7s and 4s, and backdoor draws (98s, 86s, 65s, Q9 type hands). The board is semi-connected enough that equity draws exist. Hero's Ts removes QTs from villain's range as a side effect.

2s blank. Check-back on T-7-4-2. BTN did not want to build a pot. In passive pools this indicates comfort with showdown — likely thin Tx, marginal draws that gave up, or hands that floated and lost motivation. The delayed bluff construction that pool A villains use here is absent from passive player ranges.

Qh arrives. The queen gives BTN new two-pair value: QT, Q7, Q4 all improve. A passive player who checked back the turn and then fires when the queen hits has the goods — those overcards they floated the flop with have now connected. Passive players do not use queen rivers as bluffing cards after turn check-backs; they use them because the queen improved their hand.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study the same board (T-7-4-2-Q) with hero holding T9 instead of T6 — the 9 kicker is stronger and the 9 provides minor blocker effect on villain's Qx range. Does the pool-adjusted fold threshold shift, or does passive pool context still dominate?

title: T6 BB — Turn Call Sets Up Paired T River Bluff-Catch (Multi-Step)
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. You hold Tc 6d. Board runs out Td 8h 5c — 3s — Th. BTN bets 66% pot on the turn after you check. You must decide to call or fold the turn, then face a river decision. Effective stacks 120bb.

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero called the turn with top pair marginal kicker (Tc6d on Td8h5c-3s), which was the correct play to set up this river spot. The Th pairs the top card and hero now has trip tens. With hero holding Tc and the board showing Td and Th, only Ts remains in the deck for villain's Tx holdings. This is a near-exhaustive blocker on Tx value: villain's Tx two-pair and trip combinations are reduced to combos containing Ts only. The main value hands remaining for villain are: 88 (set, possible but slow-play was unlikely with 8 outs to a better hand on the flop), 55 (set), T8s with Ts, T5s with Ts. The double-barrel after flop and turn, combined with the paired top card, creates a real bluff constituency: villain holds hands like J9, 97, 76 that had straight draw equity and now arrive with nothing. Calling turn to reach this spot was correct — folding turn would have denied hero access to a profitable river call.

steps.prompt: Turn is 3s. Board: Td 8h 5c 3s. You hold Tc 6d (top pair, 6 kicker). BTN bets 66% pot after you check. Call or fold — knowing the next decision may be a river bluff-catch?

options.label: Call — top pair is enough to continue, and I may face a better spot on the river
Fold — marginal kicker makes this too thin against a two-barrel

answer.explanation: Tc6d is top pair on a T-8-5 board with a blank 3 turn. The 66% turn bet is consistent with both value (T8s, T5s, 88, 55) and semi-bluffs (J9, 97, 76 that still have straight draw equity after 3s). Hero's equity is real — top pair on a connected board — and folding here would over-surrender the range and deny hero access to profitable river spots. Call and continue, planning to use the river texture to make a decision.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Calling the turn with top pair on a connected board is correct because villain's range contains enough semi-bluffs to make the call profitable, and hero's position in the hand sets up a potential river bluff-catch.

value_buckets.label: Turn value hands
value_buckets.note: Sets and two pair that bet for value and protection.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — not dominant

bluff_buckets.label: Semi-bluffs with equity
bluff_buckets.note: Straight draw combos that bet with 8 outs on the turn. Still have live equity — do not over-assign them to value.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial in pool A

hero_hand_bucket.label: Top pair marginal kicker — turn continue
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Call — too strong to fold, sets up river spot
hero_hand_bucket.note: The turn call is necessary to access the profitable river bluff-catch scenario.

steps.prompt: River Th pairs the top card. Board: Td 8h 5c 3s Th. You hold Tc 6d — trip tens with a 6 kicker. BTN bets 75% pot. Call or fold?

options.label: Call — trips is strong enough, and the Tc blocker reduces villain's Tx value
Fold — 6 kicker is too weak, villain has a boat

answer.explanation: Hero has Tc with Td and Th on the board. Only Ts remains for villain's Tx combos. Villain's Tx hands that arrive at the river with two barrels are reduced to: Ts8s (one combo), Ts5s (one combo), Ts3s. Villain's straight draws (J9, 97, 76) that semi-bluffed the turn all miss on the Th. The Th river creates a credible bluffing card for those missed draws — fire to represent a boat. Hero's trips with 6 kicker clears the threshold here because the Tc blocker compresses the value region and the bluff side is populated by missed semi-bluffs.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: The Tc is doing double duty: it gives hero trips and it blocks villain's remaining Tx value combos. Combined with missed straight draws that arrive as bluffs, the river call is profitable.

value_buckets.label: Remaining Tx value (Ts-only combos)
value_buckets.note: With Tc in hand and Td + Th on board, only Ts survives. Villain's Tx two-pair is reduced to Ts-suited combos only.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Severely compressed by Tc blocker

value_buckets.label: Sets
value_buckets.note: Sets that arrive as boats on the river — low combo count.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low but high strength

bluff_buckets.label: Missed straight draws from turn
bluff_buckets.note: These semi-bluffed the turn with equity and now fire the Th river representing a boat with Tx.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Primary bluff region

hero_hand_bucket.label: Trips with 6 kicker — elevated by Tc blocker
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Call — Tc blocker supports the call even with thin kicker
hero_hand_bucket.note: The turn call set up this profitable river spot. Both steps combine to show the complete decision chain.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Why was calling the turn necessary to access the profitable river decision?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: multi-street decision sequencing — turn sets up river
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: If hero folds the turn, the river decision never happens. The turn call with top pair preserves hero's equity and puts hero in position to catch the river bluff with trips. Folding the turn would be an over-surrender — top pair on a connected board has enough equity to continue, and the downstream river spot has positive EV that makes the turn call profitable in combination.

options.label: Folding the turn with a marginal kicker saves money in the long run — the river call is just chasing.
The turn call is necessary to access the river spot where hero has trips and a blocker. Folding the turn surrenders the future EV of the bluff-catch.
Always call two barrels with top pair regardless of kicker — the turn call has nothing to do with the river.

coaching_context.key_concept: Multi-street bluff-catching: the turn decision determines whether hero accesses the profitable river spot. Top pair with a marginal kicker on a connected board is worth calling to see the river.
common_mistakes: Folding the turn with top pair marginal kicker and missing the profitable river bluff-catch.
Folding the river with trips because '6 kicker is too thin' without accounting for the Tc blocker.

value_buckets.label: Boats and sets
value_buckets.note: Villain's remaining value after Tc blocker collapses the Tx universe. Lower density than a non-blocker spot.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Compressed by Tc blocker

bluff_buckets.label: Missed turn semi-bluffs
bluff_buckets.note: Had equity on turn, missed on Th, now fire representing a boat.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Main bluff constituency

threshold_notes: The turn call is slightly thin on its own, but EV-positive when combined with the downstream river call.
Both decisions evaluated together create a profitable line — each step should be evaluated in context.
blocker_notes: Tc + Td + Th on board = only Ts left for villain's Tx combos.
This near-exhaustive blocker is what makes 6 kicker trips a call rather than a fold on the river.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Multi-step: turn call + river trips call
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Call turn; call river — both steps profitable in combination
hero_hand_bucket.note: The drill tests the understanding that multi-street decisions must be evaluated as a sequence, not in isolation.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on T-8-5. Connected board — BTN's range includes Tx value (T8s, T5s), sets (88, 55), and a substantial straight-draw constituency (J9, 97, 76 all have 8 outs). Both halves of the range are well-populated from the first bet.

66% second barrel on 3s blank. The 3s does not resolve any draws. J9, 97, and 76 still have 8 live outs and continue barreling with equity. Hero's turn call with top pair is necessary to access the river spot — folding here surrenders the downstream bluff-catch equity against the full semi-bluff inventory.

Th pairs the top card. With hero holding Tc and board showing Td + Th, only Ts remains for villain's Tx combos — near-exhaustive blocker. The missed straight draws (J9, 97, 76) arrive with nothing and fire the paired river to represent a boat. The Tc blocker collapses the value region while the bluff constituency is unchanged, making trips with a 6 kicker a profitable call.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study what turn holding would make the turn-call-into-river-trips sequence unprofitable: at what kicker strength on the turn does hero prefer folding the turn rather than investing two streets to reach the trips spot?

title: K5 BB — Turn Call, Then Fold When Ace Rivers vs Passive Pool (Multi-Step)
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. You hold Kh 5d. Board runs out Kc 7d 3h — 2s — Ad. BTN bets 66% pot on turn after you check. You must decide to call or fold the turn, then face a river decision. Effective stacks 120bb.

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero called the turn with top pair 5 kicker (Kh5d on Kc7d3h-2s) — a defensible call with top pair on a dry board. The ace rivers and villain bets 75% pot. Hero's Kh5d is now effectively second pair (Kh vs Ad). The passive pool frame is decisive: a passive recreational player who two-barreled a dry K-7-3-2 board and then fires when the ace arrives is holding AK, A7, A3, A2 for two pair, or slow-played sets. Passive players do not construct ace river bluffs after two streets of betting — they arrived on the river betting because the ace improved them. Hero's 5 kicker on a king-high board was never going to beat villain's river value range. The river fold is clean and fast: ace hit villain's range, pool B does not bluff ace rivers, hero has no kicker worth fighting for.

steps.prompt: Turn is 2s. Board: Kc 7d 3h 2s. You hold Kh 5d (top pair, 5 kicker). BTN bets 66% pot after you check. Call or fold?

options.label: Call — top pair is worth continuing even with a 5 kicker on a dry board
Fold — 5 kicker is too thin to call two barrels on a dry K-high board

answer.explanation: Kh5d is top pair on Kc-7d-3h-2s. The turn is a blank — nothing connected. Villain's two-barrel range includes K-x value (KQ, KJ, K7, K3) and semi-bluffs with backdoor equity on the prior streets. Hero's top pair, even with a 5 kicker, has enough equity to call one more street on a dry board against a range that includes semi-bluffs. The call is thin but correct — folding here surrenders too much of the range with a made hand.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Top pair on a dry board is worth one more street of equity. The turn call keeps hero's range robust and allows hero to see the river, where the fold decision will be straightforward if the ace rivers.

value_buckets.label: K-x value two-barrels
value_buckets.note: Villain bets two streets for value and protection with K-x hands.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Core of villain's turn value

bluff_buckets.label: Semi-bluffs and floats
bluff_buckets.note: Hands with backdoor draws or float equity that choose to bet the turn. A7s and A3s pick up equity if the ace rivers.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Present but not dominant

hero_hand_bucket.label: Top pair thin kicker — turn call
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Call — defensible with top pair on dry board
hero_hand_bucket.note: The turn call is correct; the river decision will depend on the runout.

steps.prompt: River Ad. Board: Kc 7d 3h 2s Ad. You hold Kh 5d — top pair but ace rivers. BTN bets 75% pot. Call or fold vs passive pool?

options.label: Fold — passive pool two-barrels then bets ace river for value, not a bluff
Call — I have top pair kings and should not fold to one river bet

answer.explanation: The ace rivers and villain bets after a two-barrel line. Against a passive pool, this is a fast fold. Passive players who fire two streets on a dry K-7-3-2 board and then bet when the ace arrives have: AK (now top two pair), AK that improved, A7 or A3 that picked up two pair, or slow-played sets. They do not construct ace river bluffs after building the pot with two bets. Hero's Kh5d is second pair in hand strength now — kings against an ace-high two pair range is a clear fold.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: The ace is one of the worst rivers for hero's hand and one of the best rivers for villain's range. The pool context removes any argument for calling — passive players betting into ace rivers after two streets of betting have value.

value_buckets.label: Ace-improved value
value_buckets.note: AK for top two pair, A7 and A3 for two pair — all made hands that villain now bets for value.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Dominant — the ace directly hits BTN's opening range

bluff_buckets.label: Ace river bluffs (pool B near-zero)
bluff_buckets.note: GTO villain would bluff some hands here. Passive pool villain almost never constructs ace river bluffs after two barrels of value-heavy betting.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Near-zero in passive pools

hero_hand_bucket.label: Top pair on ace river — clear fold vs passive pool
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Fold — fast and clear
hero_hand_bucket.note: The two-step exercise shows that calling the turn was correct, and folding the river is also correct — the ace river changed the calculation completely.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero called the turn correctly and then folds the river when the ace hits. What changed between the turn call and the river fold?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: runout-dependent threshold shift — ace river changes the pool-adjusted calling threshold
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: On the turn, villain's range contained semi-bluffs with live equity — top pair was worth calling. On the ace river, villain's two-barrel range that picked up the ace (AK, A7, A3) becomes pure value. The bluff density in a passive pool on an ace river after two streets of betting collapses to near zero. The same hand (K5) was worth calling on the turn against the turn range, but is a clear fold on the river against the ace-river range.

options.label: Hero made a mistake calling the turn — if you fold the river anyway, the turn call was bad.
The turn call was correct against the turn range. The ace river collapses the bluff density in a passive pool — the fold is correct now even though the call was correct then.
The rule is always fold to ace rivers — once the ace hits, no hand below top pair is worth calling.

coaching_context.key_concept: Multi-street decisions are runout-dependent. Calling the turn and folding the river are both correct — the ace river changes the pool-adjusted threshold dramatically.
common_mistakes: Calling the river because 'I already called the turn' — sunk cost fallacy in bluff-catching.
Folding the turn to avoid the hard river decision — this over-surrenders equity when the turn range still contains semi-bluffs.

value_buckets.label: Ace-river value range
value_buckets.note: The ace directly hits BTN's range. AK is the primary value. A7, A3, A2 pick up two pair.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: High frequency — ace river directly hits BTN opening range

bluff_buckets.label: Passive pool bluffs (near zero)
bluff_buckets.note: Even GTO would bluff some hands here. Passive pools almost never do after two streets of value betting.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Near-zero vs Pool B

threshold_notes: Turn call threshold: top pair on a dry blank turn = call. Correct.
River fold threshold: top pair on ace river vs passive two-barrel = fold. Also correct.
The two thresholds are street-specific — evaluate each street independently.
blocker_notes: Kh blocks some AK combos (AKh is impossible with hero holding Kh) — minor positive.
This blocker is insufficient to change the fold — the value region extends well beyond AKh.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Multi-step: correct turn call + correct river fold
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Call turn; fold river — two correct decisions on two different thresholds
hero_hand_bucket.note: The exercise trains runout-dependent threshold adjustment, not mechanical rules.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on K-7-3. Dry board with limited equity draw volume. BTN's range is K-x value (AK, KQ, KJ, K7, K3) and backdoor floats (Ax suited hands that called planning to improve if an ace arrives, A7s A3s A2s). No strong equity draws, so the range is value-heavy relative to board complexity.

66% second barrel on 2s blank. K-7-3-2 is still dry. BTN fires again, narrowing to: K-x value that wants continued protection and semi-bluff equity hands (A7s, A3s, A2s now have two-pair outs if the ace rivers). The Ax backdoor category is important — these hands barrel correctly with live equity, and the ace river will crystallize their value.

Ad arrives. The ace converts the semi-bluff category (A7s, A3s, A2s that two-barreled on the turn) into genuine two-pair value. AK becomes top two pair. In passive pools, this entire Ax constituency is now betting for value — there are no delayed bluff sequences being constructed after two streets of value-heavy betting on a dry K-high board.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study K9 on the same runout — the 9 kicker is stronger and Kh9x blocks some AK combos more than K5. At what kicker does hero have a profitable call vs Pool B on the ace river, if any?

title: Qd9h BB — Top Pair Calls Brick River vs Aggro Rec Triple-Barrel
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Qd 9h in BB. BTN c-bets 67% on Qc 7s 3h, bets 67% turn 2d, then bets 75% river 5c. Effective stacks 80bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: Against an aggro recreational player, the threshold for top pair shifts upward — their bluffing frequency on a three-barrel line far exceeds a competent regular. The runout Qc-7s-3h-2d-5c is a near-complete brick: no flush, no straight, no scare card. A regular polarizes correctly here, but aggro recs barrel missed gut-shots, floated hands, and arbitrary overcards. Qd9h is top pair with second-best kicker. Villain's value range is QK, QJ, plus overpairs — but aggro recs arrive here with KJo, 6d4d, AdTd type runs. At 80bb, hero gets roughly 32:24 — about 1.3:1 — needing around 43% bluffs from villain. Aggro recs on brick three-barrel lines exceed that threshold substantially. This is a real-hand pattern: players overfold top pair against aggro recs on missed-draw-heavy runouts, training villain to print.

B.explanation: Against a passive recreational player, three-barrel lines on brick runouts are heavily value-weighted. These players do not run arbitrary multi-street bluffs. Top pair with 9 kicker is below the calling threshold when villain's bluff density is structurally low.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Villain is an aggro recreational player. How does that change their bluffing frequency on this brick triple-barrel vs. a competent regular?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: pool-adjusted bluff frequency on brick runouts
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Aggro recs inflate bluffing frequency significantly — they barrel missed gut-shots, float-then-fire, and over-barrel their entire semi-draw range. A regular would check back many of these on a three-barrel brick runout.

options.label: Aggro recs bluff less — they only have aggression on draws
Much higher bluff frequency — aggro recs barrel missed draws and arbitrary hands
No difference — all pools play brick rivers the same

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: At pot odds of roughly 1.3:1, how often does villain need to be bluffing for a call to break even?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: pot odds threshold on three-barrel brick
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Hero needs approximately 43% bluff frequency. Aggro recs on three-barrel brick runouts regularly exceed this — the call is profitable.

options.label: About 43% — hero needs fewer than half of villain's bets to be bluffs
Over 50% — overbetting requires villain to bluff more than half the time
Top pair always beats threshold — the hand is not the variable

coaching_context.key_concept: Against aggro recreational players, top pair on brick triple-barrel lines is a profitable call even with a marginal kicker — their bluff frequency exceeds the pot-odds threshold.
common_mistakes: Applying regular-pool logic to aggro recs: folding top pair because 'the line is strong' without accounting for inflated bluffing frequency.
Kicker-anchoring: deciding based on '9 kicker is weak' rather than where the hand sits relative to villain's actual range.
Ignoring pot odds: at 1.3:1, hero needs only 43% bluffs — well within aggro rec territory.

value_buckets.label: Top pair better kicker / overpairs
value_buckets.note: These are villain's legitimate value hands. Hero loses to all of them.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — narrow for a three-barrel range

bluff_buckets.label: Missed draws and arbitrary barrels (aggro rec)
bluff_buckets.note: Aggro recs barrel these hands across all three streets on brick runouts — the bluff population is inflated far beyond GTO.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Well above GTO in aggro rec pools

threshold_notes: At 32bb pot and 24bb bet, hero needs villain to bluff about 43% of the time — achievable against aggro recs on three-barrel bricks.
Against pool B (passive rec), this same calculation fails: passive recs don't barrel three streets with air.
blocker_notes: Qd blocks one combo of QK and QJ — minor positive. Qd also occupies one of the four queens, very slightly reducing villain's thin-value top-pair combos.
Blocker effect is secondary — pool type is the primary decision driver here.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Villain bets Qc-7s-3h consistent with wide c-bet range — AQ, KQ, QJ for value; KJ, T8, 87, 65 for semi-bluffs. No range-narrowing signal yet.
2d blank. A regular slows down many semi-bluffs here. Aggro recs do not — they barrel whatever they were already barreling. Range stays wide for villain in this pool.

5c completes nothing. A regular checks back floats and low-equity semi-bluffs. Aggro rec fires the river with everything they felt like barreling on turn — the runout being blank is irrelevant to their process.

coaching_context.follow_up: Practice quantifying bluff frequency by pool: how often does an aggro rec triple-barrel a complete brick? Compare to a passive rec. The answer changes whether top pair is a call or fold without changing the hand.

title: 8h7c BB — Second Pair Folds Brick River vs Passive Pool Bet-Check-Bet
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds 8h 7c in BB. BTN bets 50% flop Kd 8c 4s, checks back turn 2h, then bets 75% river 3c after hero checks. Effective stacks 100bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: The bet-check-bet line from a passive recreational player carries a dramatically value-heavy distribution. Passive recs rarely check back the turn as a bluff — they either give up or they have a made hand they'll fire later. When they resume firing the river after checking turn, the range narrows sharply toward made hands: AA, KK, KQ, K8 (two pair), 65 (rivered straight on Kd-8c-4s-2h-3c), sets of 44 or 22. Hero holds 8h7c — second pair, 7 kicker — on a runout where villain's check-back signals comfort with showdown or a slow-play, not a delayed bluff construction. Against a regular, the bet-check-bet includes floats and protected-range plays. Against a passive rec, the turn check is almost never the first step in a delay bluff. At 24:18 odds, hero needs roughly 43% bluffs. Passive recs on bet-check-bet lines supply well under 15%.

A.explanation: A competent regular's bet-check-bet range includes delayed floats and range-protection plays that generate a real bluff population. Hero's second pair with 7 kicker is a borderline call at 43% required bluff frequency — the straight completing (65 hits) tightens things, but regular's bluff range supplies enough to make it breakeven or slightly positive.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Villain bet the flop, checked the turn, and is now betting the river. What does this line tell you about villain's range specifically for a passive recreational player?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: bet-check-bet line filtering in passive pools
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn check-back filters out most bluffs for passive recs — they either give up or have a made hand. The river bet is heavily value-weighted when a passive rec takes this line.

options.label: Balanced — passive recs could be bluffing or value betting equally
Heavily value-weighted — passive recs almost never construct delayed bluffs via check-back
More bluff-heavy — passive recs protect their checking range by bluffing river

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: The 3c completed a straight for 65. How does that affect villain's value range vs. hero's second pair?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: runout completion and value-range addition
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: 65 now has a straight on Kd-8c-4s-2h-3c. For a passive rec who bet flop and fired river after checking turn, 65 may arrive as a riveted straight they slow-played. This only adds to the reasons to fold.

options.label: Adds a riveted straight (65) to villain's value range — more reason to fold
Passive recs don't play 65 from BTN — irrelevant
The straight completing means villain is more likely bluffing a missed draw

coaching_context.key_concept: Against passive recreational players, the bet-check-bet line is nearly always value. The turn check-back removes the bluff population that would make a river call profitable.
common_mistakes: Treating bet-check-bet identically across pool types — a regular's version includes floats; a passive rec's version is almost exclusively value.
Calling because 'second pair has showdown value' without checking whether villain has any bluffs on this specific line.
Forgetting that the 3c completed a straight (65) — an additional value hand that beats hero.

value_buckets.label: Passive rec bet-check-bet value
value_buckets.note: All beat hero's second pair. 65 riveted a straight on Kd-8c-4s-2h-3c. This is the near-complete value range for a passive rec on this line.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: High relative frequency — this is almost all of their range

bluff_buckets.label: Theoretical bluffs (passive rec — near zero)
bluff_buckets.note: Passive recs almost never check-back turn as a bluff and fire river. A5 or QJ arriving here is an edge case at best.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Well below threshold — passive rec structural deficit

threshold_notes: At 24bb pot and 18bb bet, hero needs roughly 43% bluff frequency. Passive recs on bet-check-bet lines supply under 15% — fold is clear.
Against pool A, the same threshold becomes closer to achievable — delayed floats and range-protection plays add bluff density.
blocker_notes: Hero holds 8h, blocking K8h (villain's two-pair combo) and 8h7x. Minor positive but does not change the decision given structural bluff deficit.
The 7c in hero's hand does not block any meaningful bluff combos — 65 doesn't need a 7.

what_changed_by_street.detail: BTN bets Kd-8c-4s consistent with wide c-bet range — K-high value, sets, gut-shots (A5, QJ), open-enders. No pool-specific signal yet.

2h blank — BTN checks back. For a passive rec, this signals either comfort at showdown OR a slow-played strong hand. It almost never signals a future-bluff construction. For a regular, check-back includes delayed floats. The pool distinction is critical.

3c completes 65. BTN now bets after checking turn. The river bet from a passive rec who checked turn is almost always value — strong made hand resuming. Bluff density is structurally near-zero on this line for this pool.

coaching_context.follow_up: Build a mental model of which lines passive recs use as bluffs vs. value. Bet-check-bet on a brick runout is almost always value for passive pools. Compare to aggro recs who fire all three streets with bluffs — the same hand requires opposite decisions based on pool.

title: KsJd BB — Top Pair Calls Ace River vs Aggro Rec Who Overbluffs Scare Cards
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Ks Jd in BB. BTN bets 67% flop Kh Tc 5d, bets 67% turn 6s, then bets 75% river Ac after hero checks. Effective stacks 90bb. Call or fold?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: Against an aggro recreational player, the ace river is precisely the card that inflates their bluffing frequency. Aggro recs use scare cards as bluff triggers — they see the ace and fire with whatever they were already barreling. On Kh-Tc-5d-6s-Ac, villain's credible value: AA (riveted top set), AK (two pair), KQ (top pair ace kicker). But aggro recs arrive here with KdQd (picked up nothing), Jd9d (missed straight draw), QJo (missed straight), AdXd (hit the ace but were bluffing the draw). KsJd is top pair J-kicker — hero loses to AK, AA, and KQ but beats all bluffs. At 32:24 odds (1.33:1), hero needs about 43% bluffs. Aggro recs on ace-river triple-barrels supply that comfortably. Against pool A or B, the auto-fold on ace rivers is correct — the ace genuinely improves villain's value range and they have better bluff discipline. Pool type is the deciding variable.

B.explanation: Against a passive rec, the ace river bet after three streets is heavily value-weighted. Passive recs do not use scare cards as bluff triggers — when they fire an ace river, they have an ace. KJ is dominated by AK, AA, and the bluff frequency is far below the 43% threshold needed to call.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: How does an aggro rec's betting frequency change when an ace hits the river vs. a blank?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: scare card as bluff trigger in aggro rec pools
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Aggro recs use scare cards as bluff triggers — the ace increases their firing frequency rather than decreasing it. A passive rec behaves opposite: the ace means they have an ace.

options.label: Decreases — aggro recs slow down when the board gets scary
Increases — the ace is a bluff trigger, not a bluff filter
No difference — aggro recs bet regardless of runout

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Which hands does an aggro rec credibly arrive with at this river after betting flop and turn on Kh-Tc-5d-6s-Ac?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: bluff range construction on draw-heavy board for aggro rec
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: AA and AK for value; plus J9, QJ, T8 (missed straight draws), KdQd (picked up nothing but barreled), AdXd (hit ace but was bluffing). Bluff density well above threshold.

options.label: Only AA, AK — aggro recs have a narrow value-heavy range on this action
AA, AK for value; J9, QJ, T8 missed draws, and arbitrary overcards for bluffs
Aggro recs don't bluff three streets — they give up on rivers

coaching_context.key_concept: Aggro recreational players over-bluff ace rivers — the scare card increases their firing frequency. Top pair J-kicker is a correct call against a pool that uses the ace as a bluff trigger, not a signal to fold.
common_mistakes: Automatic fold on ace rivers — applying regular-pool logic to aggro recs who use scare cards as bluff triggers.
Not accounting for what the ace adds to villain's value range: AK makes two pair, AA already had a set — the value density increase is real but manageable.
Ignoring pot odds: hero needs under 43% bluffs, which aggro recs supply on this line.

value_buckets.label: River value (aggro rec)
value_buckets.note: Hero's KJ loses to all of these. AK and AA are the primary concerns. KQ (better kicker) also beats KJ.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — real value range exists

bluff_buckets.label: Missed draws and scare-card barrels (aggro rec)
bluff_buckets.note: Aggro rec fires ace river with missed straight draws, arbitrary overcards that bricked, and hands that 'hit' the ace but were bluffing the draw. Bluff density is high.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Well above GTO bluff frequency for aggro rec pool

threshold_notes: At 32bb pot and 24bb bet (1.33:1), hero needs approximately 43% bluffs. Aggro recs on ace-river triple-barrels regularly exceed 50% bluff frequency.
Against pool A or B, fold is correct — ace genuinely improves value range and bluff discipline is higher.
blocker_notes: Ks removes KsAx and KsKx combos (AA and AK partially blocked). Minor positive blocker effect.
Jd removes Jd9d (a key bluff combo) — slightly reduces bluff range, but net effect still favors call given aggro rec's inflated bluff total.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Kh-Tc-5d: draw-heavy board. Villain c-bets wide — KQ, KJ for value; J9, QJ, T8 semi-bluffs. No pool-specific signal yet.

6s adds some equity to open-enders (J9 now has more outs to straight). Villain continues. For aggro rec, turn bet does not indicate a disciplined two-barrel — they barrel semi-bluffs and floats regardless.

Ac: for a regular, this limits hero's continuing range and makes the triple-barrel more credible as value. For an aggro rec, the ace is a trigger — they see scare card and fire. The scare card inversion is what makes this a pool-dependent decision.

coaching_context.follow_up: Compare this spot to the same hand against a passive rec who bet-checked-bet: the action pattern and the pool both shift the threshold. Practice weighting pool type as a primary variable before applying scare-card logic.
