title: JdTh BB — Jd Blocker to KJ Two Pair Enables Brick River Call
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Jd Th in BB on Jh 9s 4c. BTN bets 67% flop, 67% turn 3d, then bets 67% river Kc after hero checks. Effective stacks 100bb. Does the Jd change the decision?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: The Jd directly removes KJd and QJd from villain's river two-pair range. On Jh-9s-4c-3d-Kc, villain's natural value from betting three streets: KK (riveted top set), KJ (riveted two pair — primary concern), KQ (top pair better kicker), QQ, TT (flopped sets). Hero's Jd removes KsJd, KhJd, KcJd — three of the twelve KJ combos (25% reduction on the most dangerous two-pair). Hero also holds Th, slightly reducing TT. The Kc adds KK and KJ to villain's value range but also triggers bluff-firing in a regular's range: QT (open-ended on flop), T8 (open-ended on flop), QhJh (OESD + pair that missed), J9 (riveted two pair with worse kicker than KJ — wait, J9 would have top two pair which beats JT). At 32:21 odds, hero needs about 40% bluffs. Draw-heavy board (QT, T8, QJ all picked up straight draws on Jh-9s-4c) supports realistic bluff frequency. The Jd blocker on top of that pushes the call into positive EV.

B.explanation: Against a passive rec, the three-barrel line on Kc river is value-heavy. Passive recs do not run three-barrel bluffs on connected boards. KK, KJ, KQ are all plausible, and the bluff range (QT, T8 missed draws) is below the 40% threshold for passive pool.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Jd. Which specific combos does this remove from villain's river value range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: Jd removing KJ two-pair combos on king river
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Jd removes KsJd, KhJd, KcJd from villain's KJ two-pair combinations — about 25% of the KJ combos that represent the primary danger. The blocker doesn't eliminate KJ but meaningfully reduces its frequency.

options.label: Jd blocks JJ sets — that's the primary blocker effect on a jack-high board
Jd removes three KJ combos (KsJd, KhJd, KcJd) — reducing the most dangerous two-pair combinations
Single-card blockers rarely matter — the blocker effect is negligible

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: What is the most natural bluffing range for a regular on the Kc river after betting flop and turn on Jh-9s-4c-3d?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: missed draw range on king river after connected-board three-barrel
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: QT (open-ended on Jh-9s-4c, still firing), T8 (open-ended, still firing), QhJh (OESD + pair that bricked), AdTd (back-door that fired). These are the hands a regular commits to on connected boards and then fires the river with after the draw misses.

options.label: QT, T8 (missed OESDs), QhJh (OESD + pair), plus back-door misses like AdTd
Villain has no bluffs after betting flop and turn — the line is value-only
Villain bluffs only with hands that picked up an ace

coaching_context.key_concept: Hero's Jd removes key KJ two-pair combinations from villain's river value range. Combined with a draw-heavy board that supports real bluff frequency, the blocker makes the call correct against a regular.
common_mistakes: Treating Jd as a blocker to JJ sets only — on a king river, the value blocker against KJ two-pair is more important.
Folding automatically when a king arrives without accounting for how the Jd blocker changes the value-to-bluff ratio.
Over-weighting the king arrival: KK was always in villain's range; the king changes JJ into KK at the top, not dramatically expanding value.

value_buckets.label: River value (regular)
value_buckets.note: KK: top set. KJ: riveted two pair — partially blocked by hero's Jd (3 of 12 combos removed). KQ: top pair better kicker.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Real value range — Jd reduces KJ by 25%

bluff_buckets.label: Missed straight draws (regular)
bluff_buckets.note: QT and T8 picked up OESDs on Jh-9s-4c. A regular fires these on flop and turn and continues firing on the king river after missing. Real bluff population.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial for pool A — draw-heavy texture supports three-barrel bluffs

threshold_notes: At 32bb pot and 21bb bet (1.52:1), hero needs approximately 40% bluffs. Draw-heavy board (multiple OESDs) and Jd blocker reduce value density enough to clear the threshold.
Against pool B, bluff frequency falls below 40% — passive recs don't barrel three streets with missed draws.
blocker_notes: Jd removes KsJd, KhJd, KcJd from KJ two-pair — 25% reduction on the most dangerous value combos.
Th slightly reduces TT (flopped set) — one of four combos removed. Minor secondary effect.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Jh-9s-4c: c-bet consistent with wide range — JT, J9, J4 for value; QT, T8, QJ, J9 (villain) for semi-bluffs. Very draw-heavy.
3d blank. Villain continuing means either strong made hand (KQ, AA betting protection) or committed semi-bluff (QT, T8 still have straight draw equity). Range stays wide.

Kc: adds KK, KJ to villain's top range. Hero's Jd immediately reduces KJ by 25%. Simultaneously, missed draws (QT, T8) from flop now fire the river on the king — a regular fires these as triple-barrel bluffs after the straight draw missed.

coaching_context.follow_up: Practice isolating which card in your hand blocks the key two-pair combination on a given river. On K-high rivers, the J is more valuable as a blocker (KJ) than the T (KT is possible but less common from BTN's range). The blocker direction matters as much as the blocker itself.

title: Qh8s BB — 8s Blocks Straight Draw Bluffs, Confirming Fold on Ace River
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Qh 8s in BB on Qc 9d 5h. BTN bets 67% flop, 67% turn 3s, then fires 67% river Ac after hero checks. Effective stacks 80bb. Does the 8s help or hurt hero's call?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: The 8s in hero's hand is a fold-confirming blocker: it removes T8 and 87 — the primary straight-draw bluff combos on Qc-9d-5h — from villain's range. T8s picks up an open-ended straight draw on the flop (needs 7 or J), 87s picks up an open-ended draw (needs 6 or T). Hero holding 8s removes 8sTx and 8s7x combinations, making villain's bluff range smaller than it would appear without accounting for the blocker. Simultaneously, the Ac river adds genuine value for passive recs: AT (riveted top pair), AA (riveted top set), AQ (two pair). Against a passive recreational player, ace river bets are almost always value — passive recs do not use scare cards as bluff triggers. The combination of fold-confirming blocker (fewer bluffs in villain's range) and passive pool (structural bluff deficit) makes the fold clear. At 32:21 odds, hero needs roughly 40% bluffs. Passive recs on ace-river triple-barrels supply well under 20%, and the 8s further reduces the already-thin bluff pool.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Qh 8s. Does the 8s help or hurt hero's call on this board?

diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The 8s removes T8 and 87 — the primary straight-draw bluff combos on Qc-9d-5h. Holding the card that blocks bluffs makes the call worse, not better. This is a fold-confirming blocker.

options.label: Helps — the 8 blocks sets of eights which would be value against hero
Hurts — the 8s removes T8 and 87 straight draws from villain's bluff range
Neutral — single card blockers rarely change decisions

diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: T8 (open-ended: needs 7 or J) and 87 (open-ended: needs 6 or T) are the primary bluffs on a Q-9-5 board. Hero holds 8s, removing combos of both. JT and 76 are gut-shots — secondary bluff candidates.

options.label: T8 (needs 7 or J) and 87 (needs 6 or T) — both open-ended draws on Q-9-5
JT and 76 — gut-shots are the main bluffs on this board
Flush draws — suited connectors are the primary bluffs

coaching_context.key_concept: Holding the card that blocks villain's bluffs (fold-confirming blocker) makes a call worse, not better. The 8s on Qc-9d-5h removes T8 and 87 — the exact draws that would justify a call.
common_mistakes: Treating all blockers as call-enabling — the 8 on this board blocks bluffs, not value, making the call less attractive.
Not identifying the primary bluff candidates before assessing what holding the 8 does: T8 and 87 are open-enders on Q-9-5; hero blocks both.
Ignoring pool type: even without the blocker effect, a passive rec on a three-barrel ace river is nearly always value-heavy.

value_buckets.label: River value (passive rec ace river)
value_buckets.note: All beat hero's Qh8s (top pair with 8 kicker). Ac adds AA, AQ, AT, AK to the river value range. Passive recs fire these streets with strong made hands.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: High relative frequency — passive recs bet value, not bluffs

bluff_buckets.label: Primary bluffs (partially blocked by 8s)
bluff_buckets.note: T8s and 87s are the open-enders on Q-9-5. Hero holds 8s, removing combos with 8s: Td8s, 9h8s (not applicable), 8s7x. Bluff pool is smaller than it appears.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Below threshold — both partially blocked and passive pool reduces frequency further

threshold_notes: At 32bb pot and 21bb bet (1.52:1), hero needs approximately 40% bluffs. Passive recs supply under 20% on ace rivers. With 8s blocking primary bluffs, actual frequency is even lower.
Against pool C (aggro rec), the ace river becomes a call — they over-bluff scare cards. Pool type inverts the decision.
blocker_notes: 8s removes Td8s, 7x8s combos from villain's T8 and 87 OESD bluffs — directly reducing the bluff population that would justify a call.
Qh removes one combo of QQ (unlikely villain has) and one combo of AQ (reduces value very slightly) — but the bluff removal effect of 8s dominates.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Qc-9d-5h: draw-heavy. C-bet includes value (AQ, KQ, 99, 55) and semi-bluffs (T8, 87, JT, 76). Hero's 8s means T8 and 87 bluffs have fewer combos in villain's range from the start.
3s blank. Villain continuing means either genuine value or committed semi-bluff (T8, 87 still have 6-8 outs). Passive rec's turn bet from wide range narrows slightly.

Ac: genuine scare card that improves villain's range (AA, AQ, AT arrive). Simultaneously, hero's 8s means the OESD bluffs that didn't improve are less represented than expected. Two converging signals both point to fold.

coaching_context.follow_up: Distinguish between call-enabling blockers (you hold the card that blocks value) and fold-confirming blockers (you hold the card that blocks bluffs). The 8s here is fold-confirming. Practice identifying which type before making the decision.

title: Qh5d BB — Holding Third Queen Enables Call on Paired Queen River
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Qh 5d in BB on Qc 9h 4c. BTN bets 67% flop, 67% turn 2s, then bets 50% river Qs after hero checks. Effective stacks 100bb. Hero has trips — but can villain have a queen?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: The board shows Qc and Qs. Hero holds Qh. Three queens are accounted for — villain cannot hold any queen. This near-exhaustive blocker eliminates all Q-based value from villain's range: no QQ (quads — impossible), no Q9 (full house — impossible), no Q4 (full house — impossible), no Q2 (full house — impossible). Villain's remaining value range is limited to pocket pairs that made full houses: 99 (flopped set, now full), 44 (flopped set, now full), 22 (turned set, now full). These three combos are each unlikely pre-flop holdings for BTN. Against villain's narrow full-house range and a substantial bluff population (AcJc, AcKc, JcTc missed flush draws; 87, J8 missed straight draws that fire the river), hero needs about 38% bluffs at 32:16 odds. With villain unable to hold any queen, the value-to-bluff ratio clearly favors the call. Hero's trips with 5 kicker would normally be borderline on a paired top-card river — the blocker effect makes it a clear call.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: The board shows Qc and Qs. Hero holds Qh. What does this mean for villain's range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: exhaustive blocker — three of the four queens accounted for
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Three queens are accounted for (Qc, Qs on board, Qh in hero's hand). Villain cannot hold any queen. This removes all Q-based full houses and trips from villain's value range.

options.label: Three queens are accounted for — villain cannot hold any queen
Hero blocks some queens but villain can still have QJ or QK
The blocker effect is small — one queen in 52 cards

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Given villain cannot hold a queen, what is their realistic value range on this river?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: value range construction when top card is exhaustively blocked
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Without any queen available, villain's value is limited to pocket pairs that made full houses: 99, 44, 22. These are narrow and require specific flopped/turned sets.

options.label: 99, 44, 22 — pocket pairs that made full houses
Any two pair — villain can have 92, 42, etc.
Villain has no value — the blocker eliminates all strong hands

coaching_context.key_concept: When three of the four copies of a card are accounted for (board + hero's hand), villain cannot hold that card. This exhaustive blocker eliminates entire categories of value hands.
common_mistakes: Not counting board cards in the blocker analysis — hero blocks one queen, board blocks two more, total three accounted for.
Thinking 'trips with bad kicker = marginal hand' without checking whether villain can hold a better hand in this category.
Folding trips on a paired river by default — when hero holds the matching card, the paired river is a call-enabling situation.

value_buckets.label: Narrow full-house range (no queens possible)
value_buckets.note: All require villain to have flopped or turned a set. Three specific pocket pairs — a very narrow range that hero loses to.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low — three unlikely specific holdings

bluff_buckets.label: Missed flush and straight draws
bluff_buckets.note: Flush draws (clubs) that picked up equity on Qc-9h-4c and fired two streets. Missed straight draws (87, J8) also present. A regular fires these as triple-barrel bluffs after the river pairs.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial — draw-heavy texture with clubs flush draws plus straight draws

threshold_notes: At 32bb pot and 16bb bet (2:1), hero needs only 33% bluffs. With villain's value range limited to three specific pocket-pair sets, bluff-to-value ratio comfortably exceeds threshold.
The exhaustive queen blocker is what makes this a call instead of a marginal fold — trips with 5 kicker on a paired board without the blocker would be a closer spot.
blocker_notes: Hero holds Qh — combined with Qc and Qs on board, all three remaining queens are accounted for. Villain cannot hold Qd (the only remaining queen). This removes Q9 full house, Q4 full house, Q2 full house, and QQ quads entirely.
5d has no relevant blocker effect on this board.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Qc-9h-4c: flush draw board. Villain bets wide — AQ, KQ for value; AcJc, JcTc, KcXc for flush semi-bluffs; 87, J8, T8 for straight draws. Very wide range.

2s: blank. Villain's flush draws still live. Straight draws mostly alive. Villain continuing means strong value or committed semi-bluff. Hero's Q is an important blocker signal but becomes critical only on the river.

Qs: pairs the top card. Hero goes from top pair to trips. More importantly, three queens now accounted for — villain's entire Q-based value range is eliminated. The Qs is the card that makes this call possible.

coaching_context.follow_up: Practice board-and-hand blockers simultaneously: count how many of the key card are on the board and in your hand. When three of a rank are accounted for, villain cannot hold any. This is the most powerful form of blocker reasoning — exhaustive removal of entire value categories.

notes: BTN vs BB SRP river bluff-catch lane — Claude batch 3

title: Kc8d BB — Trips K with 8 Kicker Folds vs Passive Pool on Paired King River
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Kc 8d in BB on Kh 7c 3s. BTN bets 50% flop, 50% turn 4d, then bets 75% river Kd after hero checks. Effective stacks 100bb. Is trips good enough?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Trips kings with an 8 kicker is a strong absolute hand but loses to villain's entire credible value range on this action against a passive recreational player. Three kings are accounted for: Kh and Kd on the board, Kc in hero's hand. Villain cannot hold any king. Villain's value range therefore consists exclusively of pocket pairs that made full houses: 77 (flopped set, now sevens-full-of-kings), 33 (flopped set, now threes-full), 44 (turned set, now fours-full). These full houses all beat hero's trips. Against a passive recreational player who bet three streets on a brick runout (Kh-7c-3s-4d), the three-barrel line is overwhelmingly value — passive recs do not run three-barrel bluffs on brick boards. At 24:18 odds (1.33:1), hero needs about 43% bluffs. Passive recs on this line supply under 15%. Hero's trips are a strong hand in absolute terms but lose to everything villain credibly bets against this pool and action.

A.explanation: Against a regular, the three-barrel line on Kh-7c-3s-4d-Kd includes a real bluff population: 65 (missed straight draw on the turn), 86 (OESD that missed), A5 (gut-shot to wheel). A regular fires these on the river after the king pairs. Trips with 8 kicker is a viable bluff-catcher at 43% required bluff frequency against pool A's more balanced three-barrel range.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: A passive recreational player bets flop, turn, and river on Kh-7c-3s-4d-Kd. What does their range look like?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: passive rec three-barrel range on brick runout
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Passive recs almost never run three-barrel bluffs on brick runouts. Their three-barrel is heavily value-weighted: full houses (77, 33, 44) and any value they slow-played. Bluff population is structurally near-zero.

options.label: Balanced — equal parts value and bluff like a well-trained regular
Heavily value-weighted — passive recs rarely bluff three streets on brick runouts
More bluff-heavy — they overbluff when the board doesn't change

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero has trips kings with an 8 kicker. Which hands in villain's value range beat this?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: trips vs full house on paired board
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Full houses beat trips. Villain's value range (77, 33, 44 as full houses) all beat hero's trips. Hero cannot beat a full house.

options.label: 77, 33, 44 full houses all beat trips — hero cannot beat any of villain's value range
Only quads beat trips — hero is virtually uncounterable
K-A beats hero's K-8 kicker — kicker determines the winner

coaching_context.key_concept: Trips on a paired board is not automatically a call. The question is not whether trips is a strong hand — it is whether hero beats everything villain credibly bets in this pool on this action.
common_mistakes: Calling because 'trips is a strong hand' — absolute hand strength doesn't determine call correctness; relative strength vs. villain's range does.
Not asking 'what is villain's bluff frequency in this pool on this line?' — passive recs on three-barrel bricks are almost exclusively value-betting.
Ignoring that full houses beat trips — when villain's entire value range consists of pocket-pair full houses, hero's trips is always losing to value.

value_buckets.label: Full houses (villain can't have kings — three accounted for)
value_buckets.note: All three require villain to have flopped or turned a set. Kh and Kd are on the board; Kc is in hero's hand — villain cannot hold a king. Value range is only these pocket pairs.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low in absolute terms but this is the near-complete value range for passive rec on this line

bluff_buckets.label: Theoretical bluffs (passive rec — near zero)
bluff_buckets.note: 65 and 86 picked up some equity. A5 has a gut-shot to wheel. Passive recs almost never fire three streets with these hands on a brick runout.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Structurally below threshold for passive rec pool

threshold_notes: At 24bb pot and 18bb bet (1.33:1), hero needs roughly 43% bluffs. Passive recs on three-barrel brick runouts supply under 15% — fold is correct despite trips.
Against pool A (regular), bluff frequency increases to the point where the call becomes correct — regular's balanced three-barrel includes real missed-draw bluffs.
blocker_notes: Hero holds Kc. Combined with Kh and Kd on board, three kings accounted for. Villain cannot hold a king — this removes all K-based value from villain's range (already accounted for in value bucket analysis).
8d has no relevant blocker effect against villain's full-house range (77, 33, 44 don't involve an 8).

what_changed_by_street.detail: Kh-7c-3s: villain bets top-pair-heavy board consistent with value and occasional semi-bluffs. No strong pool signal yet.

4d blank. Villain fires again — passive rec continuing means strong made hand (KQ, AA, 77, 33) or committed value. The turn bet is more diagnostic for a passive rec — they don't double-barrel with air.

Kd pairs the top card. Hero gets trips. But three kings are now accounted for — villain's only remaining value is full houses from flopped/turned sets. Passive rec's three-barrel crystallizes into value hands (full houses) with near-zero bluffs.

coaching_context.follow_up: When the board pairs a high card you hold, immediately determine: how many of that card are now accounted for? If three are accounted for, villain cannot hold any. Then ask: what value hands can villain have that don't use that card? Those remaining value hands define whether you can call.

notes: BTN vs BB SRP river bluff-catch lane — Claude batch 3

title: Jh7c BB — Top Pair 7 Kicker Calls River Overbet After Turn Check-Back
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Jh 7c in BB. BTN bets pot on Js 8d 4h, then checks back turn 2c. On the 9s river BTN overbets 150% pot after hero checks. Effective stacks 90bb. Weak kicker or call?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: The turn check-back followed by a river overbet is a polarizing line for a competent regular. A regular who bets flop and checks turn is not betting thin value on the river — they are either slow-played strong hands (JJ, AA/KK that checked turn) or missed semi-bluffs that check-backed to set up a river bluff. Jh7c (top pair, 7 kicker) is relevant here specifically because the line is polarized. Villain is not betting J-T, J-9, or J-8 for thin value after checking the turn — those hands check river or make a small sizing bet. The overbet signals either a monster or a bluff. At 18:27 odds (0.67:1), hero needs only about 37% bluffs. The draw-heavy flop (Js-8d-4h: T7 open-ended, 76 open-ended, 65 open-ended, QT OESD) means villain's turn check-back preserved a wide range of semi-bluffs. The 9s river arrives — T7 hits the straight, meaning some 'bluffs' became value. But 76 and 65 missed, QT missed. A regular fires the overbet with these missed draws after protecting them through a turn check. The bluff frequency on this line exceeds 37%.

B.explanation: Against a passive rec, the turn check-back followed by river overbet is value-heavy. Passive recs don't construct delayed bluff sequences — the check-back means they have a made hand (slow-played JJ, AA) and the overbet is value. J7 top pair folds against a passive rec's overbet range.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: A regular bets flop, checks turn, then overbets river. What does this line tell you about their range composition?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: bet-check-overbet as polarizing line for regular
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn check-back followed by overbet is a polarizing line — villain has either slow-played strong hands or missed semi-bluffs set up as delayed bluffs. Villain is not betting thin value after checking the turn; the overbet is either a monster or a bluff.

options.label: Their range is polarized — slow-played monsters or missed draw bluffs, not thin value
The turn check means they're weak — the river overbet is always a bluff
Overbets always mean the nuts — always fold

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: At overbet odds (27 to call into 18bb pot), what bluff frequency does hero need to break even?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: pot odds calculation on overbet
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Hero calls 27 to win 45 total (18 + 27). Pot odds are 45:27 = 1.67:1. Hero needs about 37% bluffs — not as high as many players assume for overbets.

options.label: About 37% — overbets don't require villain to bluff more than half the time
Over 50% — overbets require a majority of bluffs to be profitable
Over 60% — the overbet size means hero needs a very high bluff frequency

coaching_context.key_concept: The kicker is irrelevant when villain's line is polarized. In a bet-check-overbet, villain is not betting thin value — Jh7c is a bluff-catcher against either a monster or a bluff, not a hand being beaten by J-9 or J-T.
common_mistakes: Folding J7 automatically because 'weak kicker against overbet' — the kicker only matters if villain is betting thin value; polarized overbets don't include thin value.
Miscalculating pot odds: players assume overbet calls require >50% bluff frequency; at 27 into 18, the actual threshold is ~37%.
Missing the turn check-back signal: the check-back expands the bluff range significantly vs. a three-barrel line.

value_buckets.label: Slow-played value (polarized check-then-overbet)
value_buckets.note: JJ slow-played flop/turn — credible check-back for a regular. AA/KK checked turn to polarize river range. T7s riveted a straight on 9s. All beat J7.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — narrow but real slow-play range

bluff_buckets.label: Missed semi-bluffs that checked back turn
bluff_buckets.note: 76 (OESD on Js-8d-4h), 65 (OESD), QT (OESD), 87 (turned something) all check back turn as delayed bluffs. T7 hits the straight on 9s — some of this 'bluff' range became value. 76 and 65 still fire as bluffs after missing.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial — draw-heavy board supports large delayed bluff range

threshold_notes: At 18bb pot and 27bb overbet (1.67:1), hero needs approximately 37% bluffs. Draw-heavy board provides a large semi-bluff check-back range that fires river. Threshold is achievable against a regular.
Against pool B, the check-back is not a delayed bluff setup — fold is correct regardless of pot odds.
blocker_notes: Jh removes one combo of JJ (flopped set) — minor positive, reduces top of villain's value range slightly.
7c removes one combo of T7s (riveted straight) — slightly reduces villain's river value, minor positive for hero.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Js-8d-4h: pot-sized c-bet consistent with strong top-pair value (JQ, JK) and wide semi-bluff range (T7 OESD, 76 OESD, 65 OESD, QT OESD). Many draws in villain's range.

2c blank — villain checks back. For a regular, this is a deliberate decision: protecting semi-bluffs as future bluffs and pot-controlling marginal made hands. The check-back significantly expands the bluff range that arrives on the river.

9s arrives. T7 completes the straight — some of villain's 'delayed bluffs' became value. 76 and 65 missed — these fire the overbet as bluffs. QT missed — fires overbet. A regular fires the polarizing overbet with slow-played monsters or these missed draws.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study how turn check-backs change the river range for regulars. When a regular checks the turn, they may be setting up a river bluff with missed draws. This expands the bluff range compared to a three-barrel line, making river calls with marginal hands more viable.

title: Tc8h BB — Turn Call Sets Up Ace River Decision vs Passive Pool (Multi-Step)
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Tc 8h in BB on Th 9s 4d. Two decisions: (1) BTN bets 67% on the 2c turn — call or fold? (2) If hero calls, BTN bets 75% on the As river — call or fold against a passive rec?

options.label: Fold
Call

steps.prompt: Turn is 2c. Board: Th 9s 4d 2c. Hero holds Tc 8h (top pair, 8 kicker). BTN bets 67% pot after hero checks. Call or fold?

options.label: Call — top pair is above threshold on this connected board
Fold — marginal kicker makes this too thin

answer.explanation: Tc8h is top pair on Th-9s-4d with a blank 2c turn. The draw-heavy board (87 OESD, 76 OESD, J8 OESD, QJ gut-shot) means villain's c-bet includes substantial semi-bluffs. Hero's top pair beats all bluffs and has strong equity against villain's combined range. At 14:9 odds, hero needs roughly 39% equity — top pair comfortably clears this. Folding would over-surrender the range and forfeit access to profitable river spots where hero's made hand is relevant.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Top pair with 8 kicker on a connected board is above the calling threshold when villain's range includes significant semi-bluff equity. The turn call is necessary to access the river decision, where pool type becomes the key variable.

value_buckets.label: Turn value hands
value_buckets.note: Sets, two pair, and top pair better kicker. All beat hero's T8.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — real value range on this board

bluff_buckets.label: Semi-bluffs with equity (draw-heavy board)
bluff_buckets.note: OESDs and gut-shots with 6-8 outs. These hands bet flop and continue on the turn. Substantial in pool A.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial — draw-heavy Th-9s-4d supports large semi-bluff range

steps.prompt: Hero called the turn. River: As. BTN bets 75% pot after hero checks. Hero still holds Tc 8h. Call or fold against a passive rec?

options.label: Fold — ace river vs passive rec is value-heavy
Call — already invested in the hand

answer.explanation: The sunk cost from the turn call is irrelevant to the river decision. Against a passive recreational player, the As river converts the analysis entirely. Passive recs who three-barrel with an ace river arrival are almost always value-betting: AT (riveted top two pair, beats T8), AA (top set on river), AK, AQ (top pair ace kicker). Hero's Tc8h is top pair with 8 kicker — all of villain's ace-related value beats it. Passive recs do not use scare cards as bluff triggers; unlike aggro recs, they do not fire ace rivers with missed draws. When a passive rec fires the ace river, they have an ace. At 32:24 odds (1.33:1), hero needs 43% bluffs. Passive rec's bluff frequency on this river: near zero. Fold.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Each street decision is independent. The turn call was correct on its own merits. The river fold is also correct on its own merits. Sunk cost logic — 'I already called the turn' — leads to paying off passive recs on genuine value hands.

value_buckets.label: River value (passive rec with ace)
value_buckets.note: All beat hero's top pair T8. Passive rec fires river when they hit the ace or had AA/AK all along.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Elevated — passive rec's river bet is almost exclusively Ax value

bluff_buckets.label: Bluffs (passive rec — near zero on ace river)
bluff_buckets.note: These missed draws should check back from a passive rec on the ace river. They almost never fire with air after three streets.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Structurally near-zero for passive rec pool

answer.explanation: Turn call correct (top pair vs wide villain range on connected board). River fold correct because passive recs almost never bluff ace rivers — when they fire after three streets, they have an ace. Each decision is evaluated independently: sunk cost does not obligate a river call.

C.explanation: Against an aggro rec, the ace river inflates bluffing frequency — the scare card is a trigger, not a filter. Top pair with 8 kicker becomes a call against pool C's inflated bluff frequency, even though it folds against pool B's value-heavy range.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero called correctly on the turn. Does that committed investment change what hero should do on the river?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: independent street evaluation — rejecting sunk cost logic
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Each street is evaluated independently. The turn call was correct. The river fold is also correct. Sunk cost from the turn has no bearing on the river decision — calling the river because 'I already put money in' is a leak.

options.label: No — each street is evaluated independently; the turn call was correct, the river fold is also correct
Yes — sunk cost means hero should call to protect the investment
Yes — calling the turn means hero is committed to all future streets

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: A passive recreational player fires the river after the ace lands following a bet-bet sequence. What does this tell you?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: passive rec behavior on ace rivers
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Passive recs don't use scare cards as bluff triggers. When they fire an ace river, they almost always have an ace (or a strong made hand that now bets confidently). The bluff frequency is structurally near-zero for this pool on this card.

options.label: They probably have an ace — passive recs don't bluff scare cards
They're likely bluffing — the ace is a perfect card to represent
All pools play ace rivers the same way

coaching_context.key_concept: Multi-street decisions are independent. The turn call was correct (top pair vs wide range). The river fold is correct (passive rec's ace river is value-heavy). Sunk cost from the turn does not obligate a river call.
common_mistakes: Calling the river because 'I already put in money on the turn' — sunk cost logic leads to paying off passive recs on genuine value hands.
Treating all ace rivers identically: calling against aggro recs (who bluff scare cards) is often correct; calling against passive recs is almost always wrong.
Folding the turn instead: hero's top pair is above threshold on the turn; the mistake only occurs when players call the turn and then also call the ace river against a passive rec.

value_buckets.label: River value (passive rec ace river)
value_buckets.note: All arrived via flop bet (AK, AQ as overcards with top pair equity, AT riveted two pair). These all beat hero's T8.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: High relative frequency — passive recs fire this line when they have an ace

bluff_buckets.label: Missed draws (passive rec — structurally near zero on ace river)
bluff_buckets.note: These missed OESDs check back the river in a passive rec. They do not fire ace rivers as bluffs.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Near zero for passive rec pool on ace river

threshold_notes: River: 32bb pot and 24bb bet (1.33:1) — hero needs 43% bluffs. Passive rec supplies under 10% on ace rivers. Fold is correct.
The turn call at 14:9 odds (39% equity needed) was correct: top pair on connected board has real equity against wide c-bet range.
blocker_notes: Tc blocks one combo of AT (Tc-Ah combination) and one combo of T9s (two pair). Minor positive effects that do not change the pool-driven fold decision.
8h has no relevant blocker effect against villain's Ax value range.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Th-9s-4d: draw-heavy. Wide c-bet range — AT for value; 87, 76, J8, QJ for semi-bluffs. Hero's top pair is a solid bluff-catcher.

2c blank — doesn't improve draws. Villain betting again means strong made hand (AT, T9) or committed semi-bluff (87, 76). Hero's call is correct: top pair beats all semi-bluffs and has real equity vs. range.

As: pivotal card. Hero's T8 unchanged. But villain's range shifts dramatically for a passive rec — the ace converts their weak value and semi-bluffs into strong ace-based hands. Passive recs don't bluff ace rivers; they bet them with aces.

coaching_context.follow_up: This multi-street pattern is a common real-hand mistake: call turn correctly, then face an ace river and wonder if 'pot committed.' Practice evaluating each street independently. The turn call and the river fold are both correct on their own merits.

title: Qd9s BB — Turn Call Sets Up Paired Queen River Bluff-Catch (Multi-Step)
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Qd 9s in BB on Qh 7c 3d. Two decisions: (1) BTN bets 67% on the 5s turn — call or fold? (2) If hero calls, river is Qc. BTN bets 67%. Hero now has trips. Call or fold?

options.label: Call
Fold

steps.prompt: Turn is 5s. Board: Qh 7c 3d 5s. Hero holds Qd 9s (top pair, 9 kicker). BTN bets 67% pot after hero checks. Call or fold?

options.label: Call — Q9 is a solid bluff-catcher on this board
Fold — marginal kicker on a dry board

answer.explanation: Qd9s is top pair on Qh-7c-3d-5s — a relatively dry board with some straight draw potential (64 open-ended, 46 gut-shot). Villain's turn bet includes AQ, KQ for value and semi-bluffs (64 open-ended, A6 gut-shot, 86 gut-shot). Top pair 9 kicker is a solid bluff-catcher at 14:9 odds: hero beats all of villain's draws and needs only about 39% equity against the combined range. The call sets up the river decision where the Qc will create a significant blocker effect.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Q9 on a dry board is above the calling threshold against a regular's two-barrel range. The turn call is necessary to reach the river where hero's Qd becomes an exhaustive blocker when the Qc arrives.

value_buckets.label: Turn value hands
value_buckets.note: Top pair better kicker and sets. These beat hero's Q9.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — standard BTN value range on Q-7-3

bluff_buckets.label: Semi-bluffs with equity
bluff_buckets.note: 64 open-ended after 5s. A6 gut-shot to wheel area. 86 picked up some equity. J9 has back-door equity. These are villain's draws.

steps.prompt: Hero called the turn. River: Qc. Hero now has trips (Qd-Qh-Qc). BTN bets 67% pot after hero checks. How does holding Qd affect the decision?

options.label: Call — trips with queen blocker
Fold — trips with 9 kicker may be outmatched

answer.explanation: Board shows Qh and Qc. Hero holds Qd. Three queens accounted for — villain cannot hold any queen. Villain's Q-based value (Q9 full house, Q7 full house, Q3 full house, Q5 full house) is entirely eliminated. Villain's remaining value is limited to pocket pairs that made full houses: 77 (flopped set, now full), 33 (flopped set, now full), 55 (turned set, now full). Against a regular, the Qc river is also a bluff trigger — villain perceives the paired board as reducing hero's Q-based hands (from hero's perspective, yes hero has less top pair; from villain's perspective, they can credibly rep the queen). Villain fires with missed draws (64, A6, 86 that bricked on river) as triple-barrel bluffs representing the queen. Hero's trips with three queens accounted for is a clear call: narrow value range (77, 33, 55), substantial bluff population, and 32:21 odds requiring only 40% bluffs.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: The Qd in hero's hand combined with two queens on the board creates an exhaustive blocker — villain cannot hold any queen, removing their entire Q-based value range. This is the same principle that made the turn call correct (Q9 as bluff-catcher) now applied to trips on the river.

value_buckets.label: Full houses (no queens possible)
value_buckets.note: Three specific pocket pairs. All beat hero's trips. Villain cannot hold any queen — Q7, Q3, Q5 full houses are impossible.

bluff_buckets.label: Missed draws that fire the river (regular)
bluff_buckets.note: These hands missed on the river and fire as triple-barrel bluffs. A regular uses the paired queen as a credible representation.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial for pool A — regular constructs delayed bluffs on paired rivers

answer.explanation: Turn call correct (Q9 as bluff-catcher vs wide range). River call correct because Qd + board Qh/Qc = three queens accounted for, villain cannot hold a queen. Villain's value range narrows to three specific pocket-pair full houses while bluff range (missed draws) remains substantial.

B.explanation: Against a passive rec, the three-barrel on a paired queen river is value-heavy. Passive recs don't fire the river with missed draws after checking — when they bet, they have a full house (77, 33, 55). Even with three queens accounted for, passive rec's bluff frequency is below the 40% threshold. Fold trips against pool B.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Board shows Qh and Qc. Hero holds Qd. What does this mean for villain's range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: exhaustive blocker — three queens accounted for on paired board
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Three queens are accounted for (Qh and Qc on board, Qd in hero's hand). Villain cannot hold any queen. This removes all Q-based full houses (Q7, Q3, Q5) and Q-based trips from villain's value range.

options.label: Three queens accounted for — villain cannot hold any queen
Hero reduces villain's queen combos by 25% — still has Q-K, Q-J, etc.
Board queens matter more than hero's queen — the blocker effect is small

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Given villain cannot hold a queen, what is their realistic value range on the Qc river?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: value range construction after exhaustive blocker removes top rank
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Without any queen available, villain's value consists of pocket pairs that made full houses on earlier streets: 77 (flopped set, now full), 33 (flopped set, now full), 55 (turned set, now full). This is a narrow range.

options.label: 77, 33, 55 — pocket pairs that made full houses across the streets
Q7, Q5, Q3 — two pair combinations with the queen
No value — the blocker eliminates the entire value range

coaching_context.key_concept: When the river pairs a card you already hold, and board + hand account for three of that rank, villain cannot hold any of that card. This exhaustive blocker narrows villain's value range to only pocket-pair full houses.
common_mistakes: Folding trips on a paired board without checking whether hero holds the matching card — if hero has Q9 and board shows QQ, three queens are accounted for and villain has no Q-based value.
Not tracking the blocker across streets: Q9 was a bluff-catcher on the turn; on the river the Q becomes an exhaustive blocker that changes the entire value structure.
Treating paired rivers as uniformly bad for hero: a paired river where hero holds the paired card is often call-enabling.

value_buckets.label: Full houses (no queens available)
value_buckets.note: Villain cannot hold a queen — Q7, Q3, Q5 full houses are eliminated. Remaining value is three specific pocket pairs. Very narrow range.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low — narrow specific holdings for villain's value range

bluff_buckets.label: Missed draws that fire the paired river (regular)
bluff_buckets.note: These hands missed on the river. A regular fires them as triple-barrel bluffs on the paired queen — the paired card creates a credible representation and these hands have no showdown value.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial for pool A — regular fires missed draws on paired rivers

threshold_notes: At 32bb pot and 21bb bet (1.52:1), hero needs roughly 40% bluffs. With value range limited to three pocket pairs (77, 33, 55) and a real bluff population, threshold is achievable against a regular.
Against pool B, passive recs don't fire missed draws on the river — bluff frequency is below 40%. Fold trips against pool B.
blocker_notes: Qd + Qh + Qc = three queens accounted for. Villain cannot hold any queen. This removes Q7 full house, Q3 full house, Q5 full house, and Q9 two pair entirely from villain's value range.
9s has no meaningful blocker effect against villain's remaining value (77, 33, 55 don't involve a 9).

what_changed_by_street.detail: Qh-7c-3d: relatively dry board. Villain c-bets wide — AQ, KQ for value; 64, A6, J9 for semi-bluffs. Hero's Q9 is a solid bluff-catcher.
5s: 64 picks up an open-ended draw (now 6-4 is OESD needing 3 or 8). Villain's turn bet narrows range slightly — semi-bluffs with equity and made hands continue. Hero's call is correct.

Qc: pairs the top card. Hero goes from top pair to trips. Board now shows two queens plus hero has one — three queens accounted for. Villain's Q-based full houses are impossible. The Qc is the card that transforms hero's hand from bluff-catcher to strong-but-pool-dependent caller.

coaching_context.follow_up: When the river pairs a card you hold, immediately count: board + hand = how many of that rank are accounted for? If three, villain has none. Practice applying this immediately — it changes the entire value-range analysis for paired rivers.

notes: BTN vs BB SRP river bluff-catch lane — Claude batch 3

title: Kh6d BB — Top Pair Folds Ace River in Live Hand, Should Have Called vs Aggro Rec
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Kh 6d in BB. Villain reads as an aggro recreational type. He bets around 60-70% on Kc 9s 4h, around the same on turn 2d, then fires a big river bet — you remember it as roughly 75% — on the Ad. You folded. Was that right?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: The fold was a pool-misread. Against an aggro recreational player, the ace river is a bluff trigger — they fire it with whatever they were already barreling, not because they suddenly have AK. On Kc-9s-4h-2d, both streets ran out brick. Villain's legitimate value range on this three-barrel line: AK (riveted top two pair), AA (top set on river), KQ (top pair better kicker). But an aggro rec arrives here with QJ (missed gut-shot to A-K-Q-J-T), T8 (OESD on flop — 8-9-T needs J or 7, bricked both streets), J9 (gut-shot to straight), and arbitrary overcards they barreled on momentum. You had Kh6d — top pair, not a great kicker, but that kicker is irrelevant when the relevant question is whether villain bluffs at sufficient frequency. The exact sizing (whether 70%, 75%, or 80%) does not change the decision: at any bet in the 65-85% range, hero needs roughly 38-43% bluffs. Aggro recs on three-barrel ace-river lines routinely supply that. The reconstruction mistake here was applying regular-pool logic: a competent regular would not barrel three times on a K-9-4-2-A runout with a bluff as often. An aggro rec would and does.

B.explanation: Against a passive recreational player, the ace river fires are almost always Ax value — AK, AA, AQ. Passive recs don't use scare cards as bluff triggers. K6 top pair folds against pool B on an ace river three-barrel. The exact size doesn't change this: whether 65% or 85%, passive rec's bluff frequency on ace rivers is structurally below the calling threshold.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: You folded K6 top pair when the ace arrived. What was the underlying reasoning error?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: automatic ace-river fold without pool adjustment
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The fold applied default scare-card logic without asking what pool the villain belonged to. Against aggro recs, ace rivers are bluff triggers — the card inflates their firing frequency rather than filtering it. The pool read should precede the scare-card assessment.

options.label: The fold was correct — ace rivers always improve villain's range, so top pair with a weak kicker should fold
The error was applying regular-pool logic to an aggro rec — the ace triggers more bluffing in that pool, not less
The error was the 6 kicker — with a better kicker, the call would be fine regardless of pool

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: At a river bet of approximately 75% pot, how often does an aggro rec need to be bluffing for a call to break even?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: pot odds on standard-sizing river bet
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: At 75% pot, the call is 75 into a pot of 175 (pot + bet). Odds are 175:75 = 2.33:1. Hero needs bluffs about 30% of the time. Aggro recs on three-barrel lines with a bricked runout reliably exceed this.

options.label: About 30% — aggro recs on brick three-barrels reliably supply that
Over 50% — the ace arrival means most of villain's range is Ax value
The threshold is higher because of the weak kicker — K6 needs more bluffs than K-J would

coaching_context.key_concept: Against aggro recreational players, ace rivers are bluff triggers. The pool read must precede the scare-card assessment — applying default ace-river folding logic to aggro recs is a systematic leak.
common_mistakes: Automatically folding top pair when an ace rivers without asking what pool villain is in.
Overweighting the weak kicker: the 6 kicker matters only if villain is betting thin value; in a three-barrel polarized line, villain isn't betting K-7 or K-8 for thin value.
Treating size precision as the limiting variable: the decision doesn't depend on whether the river was 70% or 80%. Pool type dominates.

value_buckets.label: Aggro rec river value
value_buckets.note: AK riveted top two pair. AA top set. KQ top pair better kicker. These are villain's credible value hands on a three-barrel K-9-4-2-A line.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Real but narrow — aggro recs have a wide three-barrel range, not a narrow value range

bluff_buckets.label: Aggro rec bluffs on ace river
bluff_buckets.note: QJ missed gut-shot. T8 open-ender that ran brick twice. J9 gut-shot. Aggro recs fire these because the ace looks like a good bluff card — and for them it is, because they use it as a trigger.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Well above GTO frequency — aggro recs inflate the bluff side of ace rivers

threshold_notes: At 75% pot bet, hero needs roughly 30% bluffs — a low bar that aggro recs clear easily on three-barrel brick-then-ace runouts.
If the river had been 2x pot (overbet), the threshold rises to ~33% and the call remains correct. Even a 1.5x overbet against an aggro rec is a call with top pair here.
blocker_notes: Kh removes one combo of KhAx (villain's AK two-pair) — minor positive. Doesn't change the decision but nudges the math very slightly toward call.
6d has no meaningful blocker effect on this board.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Kc-9s-4h: wide c-bet. Villain's range includes AK, KQ (value) and QJ, T8, J9 (semi-bluffs). No pool signal yet — aggro rec and regular look similar here.

2d blank. Villain fires again. For a regular, this double-barrel starts filtering out weaker semi-bluffs. For an aggro rec, it doesn't — they barrel everything regardless. The turn bet is not diagnostic for aggro rec pool.

Ad: for a regular, this improves value (AK, AA) and reduces hero's calling range — fold is closer. For an aggro rec, the ace is a trigger: they fire it because it looks threatening, not because they have it. The scare card inverts the call vs fold decision based on pool.

coaching_context.follow_up: The live-reconstruction lesson: when reviewing a folded hand, ask first 'what pool was villain in?' before asking 'what did the ace do to the ranges?' Pool type determines whether the scare card is a signal to fold or a signal that villain is bluffing.

notes: BTN vs BB SRP river bluff-catch lane — Claude batch 4 (live-hand reconstruction)

title: Ah6s BB — Top Pair Called Three-Barrel Brick, Passive Rec Had It
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Ah 6s in BB. The villain in seat 5 — the passive reg-ish guy who never really bluffs. He bets the flop As 8h 4c, I think he bet the turn 3d too, and he fires the river 2s. Sizes felt like around half-to-two-thirds throughout. You called the river with top pair. Was that right?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: The call was a pool-misread. The exact sizes (whether flop was 50% or 60%, river was 55% or 65%) are not the issue — what matters is that a passive recreational player fired three streets on a complete brick runout, and passive recs almost never do that as a bluff. As-8h-4c-3d-2s is a near-perfect brick: no draws completed, no scare card, nothing changed. Passive recs don't construct three-barrel bluffs on boards like this. Their triple-barrel range on this specific pattern: AA (top set), A8 (two pair), A4 (two pair), 88, 44 (flopped sets), occasionally A3 (turned two pair). All of these beat hero's Ah-6s. The reconstruction error: you remembered having 'top pair with an ace' and that felt strong. But the relevant fact was who was betting, not what you held. Against a passive rec, 'top pair ace' is a hand that loses to villain's entire betting range on this action. The exact sizes don't move the fold threshold enough to change the decision — a passive rec firing three half-pot bets on a brick runout is just as value-heavy as one firing three 75% bets.

C.explanation: Against an aggro recreational player, three-barrel brick is a calling situation — aggro recs fire this line with momentum hands, arbitrary pair+overcard barrels, and stubborn bluffs. Ah6s top pair calls against pool C's inflated bluff frequency on brick runouts.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: A passive recreational player fires three streets on a complete brick. What does this tell you about his range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: passive rec three-barrel on brick runout is structurally value-heavy
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Passive recs don't construct three-barrel bluffs on brick runouts. When this player type fires three times with nothing changing on the board, they almost always have a strong made hand: two pair, set, top pair with a better kicker than hero.

options.label: Almost always value — passive recs don't barrel three streets with air
Could be balanced — three streets of betting on any board can mean value or bluff
Passive recs sometimes over-bluff because they don't think about it — treat as unknown

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: The sizes were approximate — somewhere in the 50-65% range. Does the size uncertainty change the folding decision?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: size range and threshold stability against passive pool
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Against a passive rec with a structural bluff deficit on this line, the exact size doesn't matter. Whether the river was 50% or 70%, passive rec's bluff frequency is below the calling threshold at any of those sizings. The pool read is the binding constraint, not the sizing.

options.label: No — passive rec's bluff frequency is below threshold at any bet in the 50-70% range
Yes — a larger bet would require more bluffs, potentially flipping the decision
Without exact sizes you cannot make a correct decision in this spot

coaching_context.key_concept: Against passive recreational players, exact bet sizes are secondary to pool identity on three-barrel brick runouts. The fold is correct whether the bet was 50% or 70% — pool type is the binding variable.
common_mistakes: Calling because 'top pair ace is strong' — absolute hand strength is the wrong frame when villain's entire betting range beats hero.
Focusing on size precision: whether the bet was 50%, 60%, or 70%, the passive rec's structural bluff deficit makes this a fold at all those sizings.
Confusing 'passive rec fires' with 'passive rec bluffs' — passive recs do bet; they just bet their value, not their bluffs.

value_buckets.label: Passive rec three-barrel brick value
value_buckets.note: AA top set. A8 two pair (Ah in hero's hand removes Ah8x combos — partial block). A4 two pair. A3 turned two pair. 88 and 44 flopped sets. All beat hero's A6.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: High relative to bluffs — this is nearly the complete betting range for a passive rec on this pattern

bluff_buckets.label: Passive rec bluffs on brick three-barrel (near zero)
bluff_buckets.note: Passive recs occasionally fire with overcards they paired on the flop, but it's rare on three-barrel brick lines. Bluff density is structurally near-zero for this pool type.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Structurally below threshold — passive rec three-barrel brick is almost exclusively value

threshold_notes: At 60% pot bet (pot 24bb, bet ~14bb), hero needs ~30% bluffs. Passive rec on three-barrel brick supplies under 10%. Fold is correct by a wide margin.
Even if villain somehow bet 40% pot (a smaller size), the threshold drops to ~28% — still above what this player type supplies on this action pattern.
blocker_notes: Ah blocks Ah8x and Ah4x from villain's two-pair range — removes several A8 and A4 combos. Minor positive effect but does not change the pool-driven fold decision.
6s has no meaningful blocker effect. No relevant straight draws involve a 6 on the As-8h-4c board.

what_changed_by_street.detail: As-8h-4c: villain bets. Against a passive rec, this flop bet is consistent with AA, A8, A4, 88, 44 for value, and rarely bluffs. Already a relatively narrow range for passive recs.

3d blank. Villain fires again. For a passive rec, the second barrel removes almost all remaining weak holdings — they are not double-barreling as a bluff on a brick board. Range tightens further toward two pair and sets.

2s: another complete brick. When a passive rec fires a third time on a board where nothing changed, they have a hand. The reconstruction memory — 'sizes were around half pot' — doesn't change this. The pattern (three barrels, brick, passive pool) is the signal.

coaching_context.follow_up: When reconstructing a live hand, note the villain type before running through the hand ranges. In this spot, writing down 'passive rec, three barrels, complete brick' immediately points to fold before you even need to think about pot odds.

notes: BTN vs BB SRP river bluff-catch lane — Claude batch 4 (live-hand reconstruction)

title: 8d7c BB — Aggro Rec Fires Paired 8 River, Player Almost Folded Trips
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds 8d 7c in BB. Villain was an aggro rec type — came in raising, fired big. I think he bet the flop 8s 6h 3c, then either checked or bet small the turn 4d. The river 8h paired the top card and he fired a big bet. I had trips but almost folded. Should I have called?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: The call is correct, and the blocker effect makes it even clearer. Three eights are accounted for: 8s on the flop, 8h on the river, and 8d in hero's hand. Villain cannot hold any eight. This removes all eight-based value from villain's range: no 86 full house, no 84 full house, no 83 full house, no 88 quads. Villain's remaining value consists only of pocket pairs that flopped sets: 66 (flopped set, now full house), 33 (flopped set, now full house). Both are unlikely pre-flop BTN holdings that would fire this action pattern. Against an aggro rec who checked the turn, the river pot-sized overbet after a turn check-back is a polarizing delayed-bluff pattern — aggro recs fire paired rivers with the momentum of their earlier barreling instincts and with backdoor holdings that picked up equity along the way. The exact size (whether 100% pot or 75% pot) doesn't change the call: with an exhaustive blocker removing all of villain's eight-based value, and a narrow remaining value range of 66 and 33, the bluff-to-value ratio strongly favors calling. The memory uncertainty about the turn (bet or check?) is resolved by the observation that either way, the river decision is the same: the exhaustive eight blocker is the key fact, not the action history.

B.explanation: Against a passive rec who bets flop and fires a big river bet after checking turn, the range is heavily value-loaded. Even with three eights accounted for, passive recs almost never construct delayed bluffs via check-back and river overbet. Their check-back followed by big river bet means slow-played value: 66 or 33 for full houses. Trips with 7 kicker folds against pool B's action pattern.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: The board shows 8s and 8h. Hero holds 8d. What does this mean for villain's range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: exhaustive blocker — three eights accounted for
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Three eights are accounted for (8s flop, 8h river, 8d hero). Villain cannot hold any eight. All eight-based full houses (86, 84, 83) are impossible for villain. Villain's value range is limited to 66 and 33 (flopped sets).

options.label: Three eights accounted for — villain cannot hold any eight, removing all eight-based full houses
Hero blocks one eight — reduces villain's eight combos by 25%
Blockers don't matter on paired boards — just count full houses

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: You're not certain whether villain bet the turn or checked it. Does this uncertainty change the river decision?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: memory uncertainty vs. decision-relevant information
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The exhaustive eight blocker is the key fact on the river. Whether villain bet or checked the turn changes the action history context, but the blocker effect and pot odds on the river are the same either way. The river call is correct regardless of the turn action.

options.label: No — the blocker effect determines the river call regardless of the turn action
Yes — if villain bet the turn, his range is stronger and the river fold becomes correct
Without knowing the turn action, you cannot decide on the river

coaching_context.key_concept: When three of a rank are accounted for (board + hero's hand), villain cannot hold that card. This exhaustive blocker removes entire value categories and is often the decisive factor in calling trips on a paired river.
common_mistakes: Folding trips because 'paired board = villain has full house' — when hero holds the third copy of the paired card, villain's full-house combos using that card are all impossible.
Letting turn memory uncertainty block the river analysis: the blocker effect is independent of the turn action.
Not counting board cards in the blocker analysis: 8s (flop) + 8h (river) + 8d (hero) = three eights gone, villain has none.

value_buckets.label: Villain value (no eights possible)
value_buckets.note: Only pocket pairs that flopped sets. 66 = sixes full of eights. 33 = threes full of eights. Both are low-frequency BTN open-raise holdings.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Very low — two specific pocket pairs only

bluff_buckets.label: Aggro rec delayed bluffs (check-turn then fire river)
bluff_buckets.note: Aggro recs check back the turn with draws or floats and fire when the board pairs. T9 picked up a gut-shot on the 4 turn. 57 has a straight draw on 8-6-3-4. J9 also has connectivity. Aggro recs fire these as polarizing river bets.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial for aggro rec pool — check-then-river-overbet is a known delayed bluff pattern

threshold_notes: Villain's value range (66, 33) is very narrow — two specific pocket pairs. At pot-sized odds (2:1), hero needs ~33% bluffs. Aggro rec's delayed-bluff population on a draw-heavy 8-6-3-4 board exceeds this comfortably.
Even against a regular, the pot-sized river bet after a turn check-back is polarizing — value or bluff, not thin value. Trips with an exhaustive blocker calls.
blocker_notes: 8d removes all eight-based full houses from villain's range: 8s6x, 8s4x, 8s3x (using the flop 8), 8h6x, 8h4x, 8h3x (using the river 8). Three eights out of circulation means no villain can have any eight combination.
7c is a minor blocker on straight draws: 75s uses a 7 and 5. 7c blocks a few 75 combos — secondary but directionally positive.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 8s-6h-3c: straight-draw-heavy board. Villain bets big — consistent with 66, 33 (sets) for value and T9, J9, 57, 75 (straight draws) for semi-bluffs. Many draws in range.

4d: completes 57 straight draw (5-6-7-8? no: 4-5-6-7-8 — if villain has 57, 4d gives the open-ender for 3456... hmm). Actually 4d: villain holding 57 now has 3-4-5-6-7-8 connectivity. T9 adds 4-T9 for various draws. Villain's check-back is typical for aggro recs — they fire flop then check marginal semi-bluffs to see a free card.

8h: pairs the flop top card. Three eights accounted for — villain can't have an eight. A regular would fire a subset of their delayed bluffs here. An aggro rec fires everything they were going to fire anyway plus picks up the paired board as additional bluff credibility.

coaching_context.follow_up: When reviewing a live hand where you held trips on a paired river, the first question should be: how many of that card are accounted for? If three, villain has none. Run the value analysis from there — the blocker often resolves the decision before you need to think about pool-specific frequencies.

notes: BTN vs BB SRP river bluff-catch lane — Claude batch 4 (live-hand reconstruction)

title: Ks5h BB — King of Spades Blocks AK Two-Pair on Ace River
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Ks 5h in BB on Kh 9d 3c. He bet the flop and turn — sizes felt like around 60-70% both times. The ace hit the river and he fired again, felt like a bigger bet, maybe 75%. I was going to fold but then remembered I had the king of spades. Does that matter?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: Yes, the Ks matters — and the direction is call-enabling. On the Ah river, villain's most dangerous value hand is AK (riveted top two pair). AK combinations remaining after Kh is on the board: villain needs one of Kd, Kc, Ks (Kh is on board) paired with one of Ac, Ad, As (Ah is on board). Hero holds Ks, which removes KsAc, KsAd, KsAs from villain's AK range. With Kh on board (removing Kh from villain's K cards) and Ah on board (removing Ah from villain's A cards), villain's available AK combos are: KdAc, KdAd, KdAs, KcAc, KcAd, KcAs = 6 combos. Hero's Ks removes 3 of these (KsAc, KsAd, KsAs), reducing villain's AK combos by 33%. This is a meaningful reduction on the hand that most strongly argues for folding. Combined with villain's realistic bluff range on K-9-3-2-A (QJ missed gut-shot or OESD on flop, T8 missed OESD, J9 gut-shot), the reduced AK density tips the call into positive EV territory against a competent regular. The exact sizing — whether 70% or 80% — doesn't change this analysis. The blocker effect is the relevant fact, and it was in the right direction to call.

B.explanation: Against a passive rec, even with the Ks blocker removing 33% of AK combos, the structural bluff deficit in this pool makes the fold correct. Passive recs don't fire ace rivers as bluffs. The Ks blocker nudges the math but doesn't overcome the pool-based fold signal.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Ks. On a river of Ah, which combos does Ks remove from villain's range, and in which direction?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: Ks as call-enabling blocker on ace river (removes AK two-pair)
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Ks removes KsAc, KsAd, KsAs from villain's AK two-pair range — a 33% reduction on the hand most likely to beat hero's top pair. This is a call-enabling blocker: hero holds the card that reduces villain's primary value, not the card that reduces villain's bluffs.

options.label: Ks removes KsAx combos from villain's AK two-pair — a 33% reduction on hero's biggest threat
Ks removes some bluff draws from villain's range — it's a fold-confirming blocker
Ks is just one card — the blocker effect is negligible and doesn't change the decision

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: You remembered the river as 'around 75%' but aren't certain. If it were 65% instead, does that change the call?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: threshold stability across moderate size range when blocker is present
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: At 65%: hero needs ~28% bluffs. At 75%: hero needs ~30%. The blocker effect that reduces AK by 33% improves the call at both sizings. The call is correct across the 65-80% range — the threshold difference is small and villain's bluff range is above either threshold.

options.label: No — the call is correct at both 65% and 75%, given the blocker and villain's bluff range
Yes — a smaller bet means fewer bluffs needed, which matters at the margin
You need the exact size to make the right decision — approximate is not enough

coaching_context.key_concept: Identifying the blocker direction matters as much as identifying the blocker. The Ks here blocks villain's primary value (AK two-pair), making it a call-enabling blocker — the opposite of a fold-confirming blocker.
common_mistakes: Not following through on the blocker direction: noticing 'I have a king' is only the first step — the second step is asking whether it blocks value or bluffs.
Treating all blockers as call-enabling: a blocker that removes value is call-enabling; a blocker that removes bluffs is fold-confirming. The Ks here removes AK value, so it enables the call.
Overweighting the ace river scare: the Ks blocker directly addresses the most dangerous ace-river value hand (AK), making the call viable despite the scare card.

value_buckets.label: River value (Ks blocker applied)
value_buckets.note: These are the remaining AK combos after Kh (board) and Ah (board) and Ks (hero) are removed. 6 combos. Hero's Ks removed 3 that would otherwise exist. AA (top set on river) also exists — Ah blocked but Ac, Ad, As available for AA combos.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Reduced by 33% via Ks blocker — a meaningful difference from unblocked AK count

bluff_buckets.label: Missed draws on K-9-3-2 texture
bluff_buckets.note: QJ has a gut-shot on Kh-9d-3c (looking for T-J-Q-K-A? not quite — more like A-Q-J-T-9). T8 has some straight draw equity on the flop. J9 similarly. These fire the turn and then the ace arrives — a regular fires these as bluffs representing AK.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — K-high board is not super draw-heavy but regular's 3-barrel includes real bluffs

threshold_notes: At 75% bet: hero needs ~30% bluffs. With Ks reducing AK by 33%, the value-to-bluff ratio shifts meaningfully. Regular's bluff range on this board supplies enough to clear 30%.
Key insight: the 33% AK reduction doesn't flip a losing call into a winning one by itself — it tips a marginal spot into a clear call by reducing the most dangerous value category.
blocker_notes: Ks removes 3 of 6 available AK combos (KsAc, KsAd, KsAs) — the hand that both beats hero and is most commonly held when a regular fires an ace river with value.
5h has no relevant blocker effect on this board. The Ks is the only load-bearing blocker in hero's hand.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Kh-9d-3c: standard c-bet range. Villain's range includes AK, KQ, KJ for value and QJ, T8, J9 for draws. Wide range — no specific signal yet.
2s blank. Villain fires again. Range narrows somewhat: committed to value (AK, KQ) and semi-bluffs that bet twice. Passive recs would stop here; regulars continue with draws.

Ah: most dangerous card for hero. Adds AA (top set) and AK (two pair). But hero's Ks removes a third of AK's available combos immediately. The blocker turns what looked like an obvious fold into a close-to-clear call against a regular.

coaching_context.follow_up: When an ace rivers and hero holds a king, immediately check: 'Does my king remove AK combinations?' Count the available AK combos with both the board king and hero's king accounted for. This is a routine live reconstruction step that takes five seconds at the table.

title: Kh7s BB — 7s Blocks Primary Draws, Confirming Fold vs Passive Rec Brick Three-Barrel
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Kh 7s in BB. Passive-reg type villain. He bet the flop Kd 9c 5s, bet the turn 2h (around half pot both times), and fired the brick river 4d. I think the river was around 60-65%. I had top pair 7 kicker and called. Felt wrong afterward.

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: The call was wrong for two converging reasons. First, hero holds 7s — a fold-confirming blocker on this board. On Kd-9c-5s, the primary straight-draw bluff combos are 76 (needs 8 for 5-6-7-8-9 or needs 3 for 3-4-5-6-7 but 3 doesn't complete; actually 6-7-8-9 needs 8, and 5-6-7 with 9 on board needs 8: so 76 is open-ended needing 8 or 3-4 area) and 87 (7-8-9 with K-9-5, needs 6 for 6-7-8-9-... wait: 7-8 with 9 on board = 7-8-9 partial, needs 6 or T for a straight). Hero's 7s removes combos of 7s-6x and 7s-8x from villain's draw range — both primary bluff categories. This is a fold-confirming blocker: holding the 7 makes villain's bluff range smaller than it appears. Second, the villain is a passive recreational type who fired three streets on a complete brick. Passive recs almost never construct three-barrel bluffs on K-9-5-2-4. Their three-barrel range: KQ, KJ, K9 (two pair), K5 (two pair), 99, 55 (flopped sets). All of these beat K7. The 7s blocker further confirms a fold that was already correct on pool grounds alone. Whether the river was 60% or 65% doesn't change this — both sizings require roughly 28-30% bluffs from villain, and passive recs supply well under 10% on this action pattern.

C.explanation: Against an aggro rec, the fold-confirming blocker (7s) reduces some bluff combos but doesn't eliminate aggro rec's overall inflated bluffing frequency. Aggro recs fire three-barrel bricks with momentum and arbitrary pairings — their bluff frequency still clears the 28-30% threshold even with some 76/87 combos removed. K7 top pair calls against pool C.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds 7s. Which primary draw combos does this remove from villain's range on Kd-9c-5s?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: fold-confirming blocker removing OESD bluff combos
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: 76 and 87 are open-ended straight draws on K-9-5. Hero's 7s removes combos of 7s-6x and 7s-8x from both draw categories. This is fold-confirming: hero holds the card that blocks bluffs, not value.

options.label: 76s and 87s — hero's 7s removes combos of both primary OESD bluff categories
Hero's 7 blocks K7 two pair combos from villain — it's a value blocker
One card rarely blocks enough combos to matter on this board

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Two signals point to folding here: the pool type and the blocker direction. Which one is more binding?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: pool type as primary variable vs blocker as secondary confirmation
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Pool type is the primary binding variable: a passive rec three-barreling a brick has a structural bluff deficit regardless of blocker. The fold-confirming blocker is a secondary signal that confirms what pool type already indicated.

options.label: Pool type is primary — passive rec three-barrel brick is structurally value-heavy regardless of blocker
The blocker is primary — without the 7s, this would be a call
Both are equally important — you need both signals to fold

coaching_context.key_concept: Pool type is the binding variable; blocker direction is the confirmation. A fold-confirming blocker on top of a passive rec three-barrel is two independent signals pointing the same direction.
common_mistakes: Calling K7 because 'top pair is strong enough' without asking whether villain's range contains bluffs to pay off.
Not identifying the blocker direction: the 7 on this board blocks bluffs (76, 87 draws), not value — it's fold-confirming.
Treating size uncertainty as a decision variable: whether the river was 60% or 65%, the passive rec's bluff deficit is below the threshold at both.

value_buckets.label: Passive rec three-barrel brick value
value_buckets.note: All beat hero's K7. KQ and KJ top pair better kicker. K9 and K5 two pair. 99 and 55 flopped sets. This is the near-complete range for a passive rec firing three streets on K-9-5-2-4.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: High relative to bluffs — passive rec's three-barrel is almost all value

bluff_buckets.label: Primary bluffs (partially blocked by 7s)
bluff_buckets.note: 76 is OESD on K-9-5 (needs 8 or 3/4 to complete). 87 has some straight equity. Hero's 7s removes 7s-6x and 7s-8x combos from both. Even without the blocker, passive recs rarely fire these as three-barrel bluffs.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Near zero for passive rec pool; additionally reduced by 7s blocker

threshold_notes: At 63% pot bet, hero needs roughly 29% bluffs. Passive recs on brick three-barrels supply under 10%. With 7s blocker further reducing the already-thin bluff pool, fold is correct by a large margin.
Pool type analysis alone justifies the fold. The blocker is additional confirmation, not a necessary input.
blocker_notes: 7s removes combos of 7s-6x (blocking 76s OESDs) and 7s-8x (blocking 87s OESDs) — both primary bluff categories on K-9-5.
Kh has a minor blocker effect on KxQx, KxJx (villain's better top-pair combos). This slightly reduces value density. Not load-bearing in the analysis but a small nudge.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Kd-9c-5s: passive rec bets. Consistent with KQ, KJ, K9, K5, 99, 55 for value and rarely 76, 87 for semi-bluffs. No strong pool signal on the flop alone.
2h blank. Passive rec fires again. For this player type, the second barrel removes the last theoretical bluffs from their range. Their two-barrel on a brick board is almost entirely value.

4d: another blank. The passive rec fires a third time. For this player type, this means a strong made hand. The 7s blocker further reduces the already-depleted bluff population. Two independent reasons to fold.

coaching_context.follow_up: Live reconstruction exercise: when you catch a passive rec three-barreling a brick, write down their range before thinking about your hand. If their betting range is entirely hands that beat you, the decision is made. Your hand's kicker or blockers become secondary.
