title: Td9h BB — Three Tens Accounted For, Trips Calls Paired T River
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Td 9h in BB. He bet the flop Tc 7s 3d, and I think he bet the turn 4h — honestly I'm not sure on the turn sizing but I know I called. The river paired the T and he fired. I had trips but the paired board made me nervous. Should I have called?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: Yes, call. Three tens are accounted for: Tc on the flop, Th on the river, and Td in hero's hand. Villain cannot hold any ten. This removes all ten-based full houses and trips from villain's value range: no T7 full house, no T3 full house, no T4 full house, and no TT quads. Villain's remaining value is limited to pocket pairs that made full houses on earlier streets: 77 (flopped set, now sevens full of tens), 33 (flopped set, now threes full), 44 (turned set, now fours full). These three specific holdings are the only things villain can beat hero with — and each is an unlikely BTN pre-flop holding relative to the wide flop range villain should have. Against a regular's three-barrel range, the bluff population from missed straight draws (86, 65, 56 that picked up open-ender equity on T-7-3-4) is real and fires on the paired river as a triple-barrel bluff. The turn memory uncertainty — was it exactly 67% or 50%? — does not change the river analysis. The exhaustive ten blocker is the key fact regardless of turn sizing. Hero has trips, villain can have full houses from only three specific pocket pairs, and villain has a real bluff range. Call.

B.explanation: Against a passive rec, even with three tens accounted for, the three-barrel on a paired board signals value. Passive recs don't fire three streets with missed straight draws. Their range on this action: 77, 33, 44 (full houses). Hero's trips with 9 kicker loses to all of these and passive rec's bluff population is near zero. Fold against pool B.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Tc is the flop card, Th is the river card, and hero holds Td. What is the implication for villain's range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: exhaustive ten blocker — villain cannot hold any ten
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Three tens are accounted for (Tc, Th, Td). Villain cannot hold any ten. All T-based full houses are removed from villain's value range — T7, T3, T4 full houses are impossible.

options.label: Three tens accounted for — villain cannot hold a ten, removing all T-based full houses
Hero blocks one ten — reduces T-based value by 25%
Villain can still have T9, TJ etc. — the paired river is dangerous

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: You're unsure whether the turn bet was 50% or 67%. Does this uncertainty change the river call?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn sizing uncertainty vs river blocker fact
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn sizing affects the pot size slightly but not the fundamental decision driver: the exhaustive ten blocker. Whether the pot is 28bb or 32bb on the river, the blocker effect (three tens accounted for) and the bluff-to-value ratio drive the call.

options.label: No — the blocker effect is the key fact; pot size variation from turn sizing doesn't change the call
Yes — a larger turn bet changes villain's range composition and the river analysis
You need exact sizing history to evaluate river spots correctly

coaching_context.key_concept: Turn sizing uncertainty is an acceptable reconstruction imprecision. The river blocker fact — three tens accounted for, villain cannot have a ten — is the load-bearing insight that the approximate sizing history doesn't alter.
common_mistakes: Folding trips because 'paired board means full houses' — when hero holds the third copy of the paired card, villain has no access to that card's full houses.
Letting turn sizing uncertainty stop the river analysis: the blocker is a hard fact that doesn't depend on pot arithmetic.
Worrying about 'what if villain has 77 or 33?' — these are the only remaining value combos, and villain must have flopped sets with specific unlikely pocket pairs to have either.

value_buckets.label: Villain value (no tens available)
value_buckets.note: 77 and 33 flopped sets. 44 turned set. All are full houses. No T-based value is possible. Three specific low pocket pairs represent villain's entire value range.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Very low — each is a specific unlikely BTN holding that flopped or turned a set

bluff_buckets.label: Missed straight draws that fire river (regular)
bluff_buckets.note: On T-7-3-4 texture: 86 is OESD (5-6-7-8 needs 5 or 9, and with T and 4 available: 6-7-8-9-T? 86 = 6-8, with 7 on board = 6-7-8 partial needing 5 or 9). J9 has some connectivity on T-9 territory. A regular fires these as triple-barrel bluffs on the paired ten river.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial for pool A — T-7-3-4 is a draw-heavy enough texture to support real bluffs

threshold_notes: At 75% bet, hero needs ~30% bluffs. With value range limited to three specific pocket pairs and a real missed-draw bluff population, the threshold is comfortably cleared against a regular.
The exhaustive ten blocker is what makes this a clear call rather than a marginal one — without it, villain's T7, T3, T4 full houses would be the primary concern.
blocker_notes: Td + Tc (board) + Th (board) = three tens accounted for. Villain cannot hold Ts (the only remaining ten). All T-based full houses (T7, T3, T4, T9) are impossible for villain.
9h blocks one combo of J9h — minor secondary effect on villain's bluff combos.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Tc-7s-3d: draw-heavy board. Villain's range includes TJ, T9, T8 (value) and 86, 65, J9 (semi-bluffs). Hero's top pair is a solid bluff-catcher.

4h: adds straight draw equity to 86 (now 4-5-6-7-8... with 7 on board: 4-5-6-7-8 needs 5, or 3-4-5-6-7 needs 5/3). Villain fires again, consistent with value and committed semi-bluffs. Turn sizing memory is fuzzy but this doesn't change the river analysis.

Th: three tens accounted for. Villain's T-based value is entirely eliminated. The river bet is either the three specific pocket-pair full houses or a triple-barrel bluff. Against a regular, the bluff side is real.

coaching_context.follow_up: Build the habit: when the river pairs a card you hold, immediately count board + hero hand = how many of that rank are accounted for? If three, villian has none. This is a five-second check that resolves many paired-river uncertainty spots.

title: Qd8s BB — Exact River Size Doesn't Save This Fold vs Passive Rec Ace River
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Qd 8s in BB. Passive player. He bets the flop Qh 7d 3c, bets the turn 5s, then fires the river Ac. I'm not sure if the river was 75% or pot-sized — I remember it feeling like a big bet. I called. Was that right?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: The call was wrong, and the size uncertainty doesn't rescue it. Here is why the exact size doesn't change the decision: against a passive recreational player, ace rivers are value-heavy regardless of sizing. Whether villain bet 75% or 100%, their bluff frequency on an ace river three-barrel is structurally below the calling threshold at both sizings. At 75%: hero needs ~30% bluffs. At 100%: hero needs ~33% bluffs. Passive rec's bluff frequency on ace rivers: under 10%. The threshold does not flip anywhere in the 50-150% sizing range. The size is memory-irrelevant here because the pool type is the binding constraint. Villain's value on Qh-7d-3c-5s-Ac: AQ (riveted top two pair), AK (top pair with ace), AA (top set on river), KQ (top pair better kicker). Hero's Q8 is behind all of these. The 8s in hero's hand blocks some minor bluff combos (T8s, 87s might appear as draws on the turn-5s board), but this fold-confirming effect is secondary — the fold was already correct before accounting for blockers. The reconstruction lesson: when you recall a passive player firing an ace river but can't remember the exact size, the fold was likely correct if you had a marginal holding — the pool type trumps the sizing within any standard bet range.

C.explanation: Against an aggro recreational player, ace rivers are bluff triggers. At both 75% and 100% sizing, aggro recs fire ace rivers with a bluff frequency that clears the 30-33% threshold. Q8 top pair calls against pool C on an ace river three-barrel.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: The river was somewhere between 75% and 100% pot. Does that size range change whether the fold is correct against a passive rec?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: threshold stability across size range when pool type dominates
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: At 75%: need ~30% bluffs. At 100%: need ~33% bluffs. Passive recs supply under 10% on ace river three-barrels. The fold is correct at both sizings — pool type is the binding constraint, not whether the bet was 75% or 100%.

options.label: No — the fold is correct at both 75% and 100%; pool type dominates in this range
Yes — a larger bet size could mean villain is bluffing more, which might flip the call
Without the exact size you cannot calculate whether to call

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Why is pool type more binding than sizing in this spot?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: pool type as dominant variable over sizing on ace rivers
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Passive recs supply under 10% bluffs on ace river three-barrels regardless of their sizing. Even if their size implied they were polarizing more, the structural bluff deficit for this pool type doesn't change with bet size. Pool type creates a fixed bluff frequency constraint that sizing doesn't override.

options.label: Passive recs have a structural bluff deficit on ace rivers — this doesn't change with bet size
Sizing is the primary variable — larger bets always mean more bluffs required, which matters
Both matter equally — you need both to evaluate the call

coaching_context.key_concept: When pool type creates a structural bluff deficit, sizing becomes memory-irrelevant within standard bet ranges. The fold is correct at 60%, 75%, and 100% against a passive rec on an ace river — remembering the approximate size is sufficient.
common_mistakes: Calling because 'top pair seems too strong to fold' — Q8 is top pair but loses to everything in villain's value range on an ace river.
Anchoring on size uncertainty: 'I can't fold without knowing the exact size.' In this spot, pool type makes the sizing a secondary variable.
Missing that 8s is a fold-confirming blocker: it removes T8 and 87 draws — making villain's bluff range even thinner than expected, which only confirms the fold.

value_buckets.label: Passive rec ace river value
value_buckets.note: All beat Q8. AQ riveted top two pair. AA riveted top set. AK and KQ top pair better kicker. Passive rec's three-barrel ace river is loaded with these hands.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: High — this is nearly villain's complete three-barrel range for passive pool

bluff_buckets.label: Passive rec bluffs (structural near-zero on ace rivers)
bluff_buckets.note: J9 has some connectivity. T8 is an OESD candidate on Q-7-3-5 (7-8-9-T? or 5-6-7-8-9?). But passive recs don't fire these as three-barrel bluffs on ace rivers. Near-zero frequency.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Structurally below threshold — passive recs don't use ace rivers as bluff triggers

threshold_notes: At 88% pot bet (using 88% as the midpoint between 75% and 100%), hero needs roughly 32% bluffs. Passive rec on ace river three-barrel supplies under 10% regardless of sizing.

The sizing range at which this fold would flip to a call: villain would need to bet sub-20% pot (making the call so cheap it's worth it for equity). At any normal sizing from 40-150% pot, the fold is correct against passive rec.
blocker_notes: 8s is a fold-confirming blocker: removes T8s and 87s from the already-thin bluff range. Secondary to the pool analysis but directionally reinforces the fold.
Qd is a minor blocker on villain's AQ two-pair combos (QdAx = removes 3 combos). Directionally positive for hero but doesn't change the fold given pool type.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Qh-7d-3c: passive rec bets. Range includes AQ, KQ for value and some semi-bluffs (T8, J9 marginal). First bet consistent with wide range.
5s: passive rec fires again. Second barrel from this player type further filters out weak holdings. Range tightens toward value and committed holdings.

Ac: the ace genuinely improves villain's range (AQ, AA) and triggers zero additional bluffing from a passive rec. The question 'was it 75% or 100%?' is irrelevant — both sizings require a bluff frequency that passive recs structurally can't supply.

coaching_context.follow_up: Live reconstruction principle: when reconstructing a spot involving a passive rec and an ace river, write 'fold' before checking the sizes. The sizing only matters if it moves from a normal bet to a polar overbet (1.5x+), and even then, passive recs don't change their bluffing strategy based on size.

title: Ah7c BB — A7 Calls Aggro Rec Brick Three-Barrel Regardless of Exact River Size
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Ah 7c in BB. The villain was clearly an aggro rec — raising a lot, firing big. He bet around 60% on Ad 8s 3c, something similar on turn 2d, then river was a brick 5h. I think the river was somewhere between 50% and 70% pot. I couldn't remember. Should I call?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: Yes, call. And the size uncertainty between 50% and 70% doesn't change that. At 50% pot: hero needs ~25% bluffs. At 70%: hero needs ~29% bluffs. Against an aggro recreational player on a three-barrel brick runout (Ad-8s-3c-2d-5h — a near-perfect brick with no draws completing), the bluff frequency substantially exceeds 25-29% at any of these sizings. Aggro recs fire this line with momentum-based barrels: KQ (overcard), QJ (pure overcard bluff), T9 (gut-shot that bricked), 76 (straight draw that bricked), and any two hearts or other suit that tried to develop a flush. Villain's legitimate value: AA, A8, A3, A2, A5 (two pair with ace). These are real. But on a dry board with no interesting turn/river action, aggro recs barrel all their above-threshold air too. The Ah in hero's hand blocks Ah-8x and Ah-3x from villain's two-pair range — a mild call-enabling blocker. The key insight for reconstruction: when an aggro rec fires three streets on a complete brick and the remembered size is somewhere in the 50-70% range, the call is correct regardless of where in that range the actual size fell. The decision family is clear even with sizing fuzziness.

B.explanation: Against a passive rec on a brick three-barrel, villain's range is heavily value-loaded — A8, A3, A5 two pair, AA. Passive recs don't barrel three streets with KQ, QJ air. A7 top pair folds against pool B whether the river was 50% or 70%.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: The river was somewhere between 50% and 70% pot. Does this range change whether the call is correct vs an aggro rec?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: threshold stability across size range when aggro rec bluff frequency dominates
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: At 50%: need ~25% bluffs. At 70%: need ~29% bluffs. Aggro recs on brick three-barrels supply well above both thresholds. The call is correct across the full 50-70% range — sizing memory fuzziness within this range is acceptable.

options.label: No — aggro rec's bluff frequency clears both thresholds; call at 50% and 70%
Yes — a larger bet means villain is more polarized, requiring more bluffs for a call
The call is only correct if the bet was close to 50%; at 70% it becomes a fold

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: At what point would the size start to matter for this call decision?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: threshold flip point — when does sizing change the decision family
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The call becomes more marginal if villain overbets at 1.5x-2x pot, which requires bluff frequency above 40-50%. Even at those sizings against an aggro rec, the call may still be correct. The decision family (call) is stable up to about 1.25x pot.

options.label: At roughly 1.5-2x pot overbets — that's when the threshold rises enough to question the call
At 70% it's already close to the flip point — any bigger and you fold
Pool type fully determines the call regardless of any sizing

coaching_context.key_concept: Sizing fuzziness within the 50-70% range is acceptable when aggro rec bluff frequency clearly dominates the threshold calculation. The decision family (call) is stable across that range — reconstruction imprecision is not a problem here.
common_mistakes: Folding because 'I can't calculate without the exact size' — the decision family is determined by pool type first, and sizing only matters when it crosses into overbet territory.
Treating A7 as a thin call — against an aggro rec on brick, top pair with ace kicker is a solid bluff-catcher with a comfortable margin, not a marginal spot.
Assuming 'if the size was bigger than I remember, the call is wrong' — this is only true once sizing crosses roughly 1.5x pot.

value_buckets.label: Aggro rec brick three-barrel value
value_buckets.note: AA top set. A8, A3, A2, A5 two pair (turned or riveted on brick runout). All beat hero's A7.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — real value range exists but it's not overwhelming on a dry board

bluff_buckets.label: Aggro rec brick momentum barrels
bluff_buckets.note: Aggro recs barrel KQ, QJ (overcards that missed), T9 and 76 (straight draws that bricked both streets). These are the hands that inflate aggro rec bluff frequency on complete brick runouts.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Well above GTO — aggro recs fire these because they've committed across streets, not because they're balancing

threshold_notes: At 60% pot (midpoint of recalled range): hero needs ~28% bluffs. Aggro recs on three-barrel bricks supply 40%+ bluff frequency — a large margin above threshold.
The decision flips to fold when sizing rises to roughly 1.5x pot AND pool is passive rec. Against aggro rec, even 1.5x pot is a call if bluff frequency stays elevated.
blocker_notes: Ah blocks Ah8x, Ah3x, Ah2x, Ah5x from villain's two-pair range — removes several A-two-pair combos that would beat hero. Mild call-enabling effect.
7c has no meaningful blocker effect against villain's primary bluff combos on this board.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Ad-8s-3c: aggro rec bets. Range includes AA, A8, A3 (value) and KQ, QJ, T9, 76 (pure bluffs or semi-bluffs). Already wide bluff density for this player type.
2d: brick. Aggro rec fires again. For this player type, the second barrel doesn't filter bluffs — they keep going. A2 two pair added to value range. Bluffs still there.

5h: another brick. Aggro rec fires a third time. Nothing completing means their draws missed — but aggro recs fire missed draws on the river anyway. A5 two pair added to value. Bluff density remains high.

coaching_context.follow_up: When reconstructing a spot with an aggro rec and a brick three-barrel, the call is usually correct at standard bet sizes. The reconstruction uncertainty that matters is pool identification (was this actually an aggro rec or a regular?) not sizing (was it 55% or 65%).

title: Qh9s BB — Turn Memory Fuzzy, But Ace River vs Passive Rec Is Always a Fold (Multi-Step)
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Qh 9s in BB. Passive player in seat 3. He bet the flop Qs 8d 4c. On the turn 6h, I'm honestly not sure if he bet or checked — I think he bet something? But I remember calling twice and then the Ah arrived on the river and he fired again. Sizes? Flop felt around 60%, turn probably similar if he bet, river was bigger — maybe 75-80%?

options.label: Fold
Call

steps.prompt: Turn is 6h. Board: Qs 8d 4c 6h. Hero holds Qh 9s (top pair, 9 kicker). If villain bets around 60% pot, call or fold?

options.label: Call — Q9 is above threshold on this draw-heavy board
Fold — marginal kicker, passive player, fold the turn

answer.explanation: Top pair 9 kicker on Qs-8d-4c-6h is above the calling threshold on the turn. The board has straight draw potential (T7, 75, 97 on Q-8-4-6 texture pick up equity). Against any player type, Q9 is a clear call at 60% pot on the turn — hero's made hand beats villain's semi-bluffs and the kicker doesn't matter for turn calling equity. Whether villain bet exactly 60% or something similar doesn't change the call.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Q9 top pair on this texture is a clear continue regardless of whether the turn was bet or checked — the turn call decision is not where the mistake lives.

value_buckets.label: Turn value hands
value_buckets.note: Top pair better kicker and flopped sets. All beat Q9.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — standard BTN range on Q-8-4-6

bluff_buckets.label: Semi-bluffs with straight equity
bluff_buckets.note: T7 OESD on Q-8-4-6 (needs 9 or J? 6-7-8-9-T needs 9). 75 open-ender. J9 gut-shot. Real bluff range.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — draw-heavy texture supports real semi-bluff range

steps.prompt: Hero called the turn. River is Ah. Passive villain bets around 78% pot. Hero still holds Qh 9s. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold — passive rec fired ace river, they have it
Call — already invested in the hand, top pair is still top pair

answer.explanation: Fold. The turn call was correct; this river fold is also correct. These are independent decisions. Passive recs almost never fire ace rivers as bluffs — when they bet after the ace arrives, they have an ace. Villain's river range: AQ (two pair), AK (top pair ace kicker), AA (top set), AT (picked up top pair on river). Hero's Q9 is top pair queens — dominated by all Ax hands. At 78% pot: hero needs ~32% bluffs. Passive rec on ace river supplies under 10%. Fold. The approximate sizing memory (75-80%) doesn't change this — at both ends of the remembered range, the fold is correct.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: The turn call was correct on its own merits. The river fold is correct on its own merits. Sunk cost from the turn does not obligate a river call.

value_buckets.label: Passive rec river value (ace river)
value_buckets.note: All beat hero's Q9 top pair. Passive rec fires the ace river when they have an ace.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Elevated — passive rec's river bet on ace arrival is almost exclusively Ax

bluff_buckets.label: Passive rec bluffs on ace river (near zero)
bluff_buckets.note: These missed draws should check back from a passive rec. They almost never fire missed draws after an ace arrives.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Near zero — passive recs don't use ace rivers as bluff triggers

answer.explanation: Turn call is correct (Q9 top pair vs wide range). River fold is correct because passive recs don't bluff ace rivers — when they fire after an ace arrives, they have an ace. The turn memory uncertainty (bet or check?) doesn't change the river decision: whether villain bet-bet-bet or bet-check-bet, the passive rec firing an ace river is value-heavy at any standard sizing. Sunk cost from the turn call does not obligate the river call.

C.explanation: Against an aggro rec, the ace river is a bluff trigger. Q9 top pair calls against pool C on an ace river three-barrel — aggro recs fire this card with inflated bluff frequency that clears the ~32% threshold.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: You're not sure whether villain bet or checked the turn. Does this uncertainty change the river fold decision?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn memory uncertainty vs river decision clarity
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Whether villain bet-bet-bet or bet-check-bet, a passive rec firing the ace river is value-heavy at any standard sizing. The pattern that matters for the river is: passive player + ace river + bet. Turn action provides context but doesn't change the conclusion.

options.label: No — a passive rec firing the ace river is value-heavy regardless of turn action
Yes — if villain checked the turn, the river bet could be a delayed bluff from a passive rec
You need to know the turn action to correctly evaluate the river

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero called flop and turn already. Does that committed investment change the river decision?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: independent street evaluation — sunk cost fallacy
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Each street is evaluated independently. The turn call was correct on its own merits. The river fold is correct on its own merits. Sunk cost from prior streets never obligates a future call.

options.label: No — each street is evaluated independently; calling twice doesn't obligate the river call
Yes — after calling twice, hero is pot-committed and should call the river
The pot is large relative to the river bet — the pot odds alone justify calling

coaching_context.key_concept: Turn action uncertainty is an acceptable reconstruction imprecision when the river decision is pool-driven. For a passive rec firing an ace river, the fold is correct whether the turn was bet or checked — pool type determines the river call, not turn history.
common_mistakes: Calling because 'I already put in money on the turn' — sunk cost logic leads to paying off passive recs on genuine value bets.
Treating turn memory uncertainty as blocking the river analysis: the passive rec + ace river pattern is sufficient to fold, regardless of what the turn looked like.
Confusing the general principle (aggressive players bluff ace rivers) with this pool (passive recs almost never do).

value_buckets.label: Passive rec river value on ace arrival
value_buckets.note: All beat hero's Q9. Passive rec fires after ace because they have an ace-containing hand or a set.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: High — passive rec three-barrel on ace river is almost entirely these hands

bluff_buckets.label: Passive rec missed draws (structurally near zero)
bluff_buckets.note: These missed draws check back from a passive rec. They do not fire ace rivers with missed hands.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Near zero for passive rec pool on ace rivers

threshold_notes: At 78% pot: hero needs ~32% bluffs. Passive rec on ace river supplies under 10% bluffs. Fold by a large margin.
If turn was bet-check (not bet-bet): the pattern changes slightly but not the river conclusion. Passive recs don't run delayed bluffs via bet-check-bet as a pattern. The fold holds either way.
blocker_notes: Qh blocks Qh-Ax combos from villain's AQ two-pair — minor positive but doesn't change pool-driven fold.
9s has no meaningful blocker effect on villain's Ax value range.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Qs-8d-4c: villain bets consistent with AQ, KQ (value) and T7, J9 (semi-bluffs). Hero's top pair is a clear continue.

6h: adds some connectivity (T7 picks up equity). Villain's second bet (if it was a bet) confirms value or committed semi-bluff. If villain checked, the check sets up a potential delayed bluff — but passive recs almost never do this intentionally. Either way, hero's call is correct on equity grounds.

Ah: pivotal card. For a passive rec, this means they have an ace. Hero's Q9 is dominated by all Ax. The turn memory imprecision is irrelevant — the river conclusion is the same whether turn was bet or checked.

coaching_context.follow_up: When you can't remember whether a passive rec bet or checked the turn, ask: 'Does it matter for the river decision?' If the player is a passive rec and an ace rivers, the answer is almost always no — fold regardless of turn action history.

notes: BTN vs BB SRP river bluff-catch lane — Claude batch 4 (live-hand reconstruction)

title: Kd8s BB — Turn Memory Fuzzy, Paired K River Creates Clear Blocker Call (Multi-Step)
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN vs BB SRP. Hero holds Kd 8s in BB. Competent regular. He bet the flop Ks 9h 4c, and I remember calling the turn 3d but I'm not sure on the size — maybe around 60%? River was Kh and he fired. I remember it being around 70-75%. I had trips but wasn't sure what to do.

options.label: Call
Fold

steps.prompt: Turn is 3d. Board: Ks 9h 4c 3d. Hero holds Kd 8s (top pair, 8 kicker). Villain bets around 60-65% pot. Call or fold?

options.label: Call — K8 is a reasonable bluff-catcher on this board
Fold — marginal kicker, tough spot vs regular

answer.explanation: K8 top pair on Ks-9h-4c-3d is above the calling threshold on the turn. The board has some straight draw potential (QJ, T8, 65 picking up gut-shots or OESDs on this texture). K8 beats villain's semi-bluffs and has solid equity against the combined range. The approximate sizing (60% vs 65%) doesn't change the call — top pair on a medium-connected board continues at both sizings.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: K8 top pair is a standard continue on the turn regardless of the exact sizing within the 55-70% range. The turn call sets up the river where the Kd blocker becomes critical.

value_buckets.label: Turn value hands
value_buckets.note: Top pair better kicker and flopped sets. All beat K8.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — standard BTN range on K-9-4-3

bluff_buckets.label: Semi-bluffs on K-9-4-3 texture
bluff_buckets.note: QJ gut-shot to straight. T8 has OESD potential. 65 open-ender. J8 some straight equity. These are villain's draws that fire two streets.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Moderate — board supports real bluff range

steps.prompt: Hero called the turn. River is Kh. Hero now has trips (Kd-Ks-Kh). Villain fires around 72% pot. How does holding Kd change the decision?

options.label: Call — trips with three kings accounted for
Fold — paired board might mean villain has a full house

answer.explanation: Call. Three kings are accounted for: Ks on the flop, Kh on the river, and Kd in hero's hand. Villain cannot hold any king. This removes all K-based full houses: K9, K4, K3 full houses are impossible for villain. Villain's remaining value: 99 (flopped set, now nines-full-of-kings), 44 (flopped set, now fours-full), 33 (turned set, now threes-full). These are three specific pocket pairs — each an unlikely BTN holding. Against a competent regular's three-barrel range, the bluff population from missed draws (QJ, T8, 65, J8) is real. A regular fires these as triple-barrel bluffs on the paired king river, representing the king. Hero's trips with the exhaustive K blocker is a clear call against pool A. The turn sizing uncertainty (60% vs 65%) doesn't change the river analysis — the blocker effect is a hard fact that the approximate turn size doesn't alter.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: The Kd + Ks (board) + Kh (board) = three kings accounted for. Villain's K-based value is entirely removed. The river call is driven by this blocker fact, not by the approximate turn sizing history.

value_buckets.label: Villain value (no kings available)
value_buckets.note: Full houses from flopped/turned sets. Villain cannot hold a king. Value range is three specific pocket pairs only.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low — narrow specific holdings represent villain's entire value range

bluff_buckets.label: Missed draws that fire river (regular)
bluff_buckets.note: A regular fires these as triple-barrel bluffs after missing on the river — the paired king is a credible representation card for villain.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial for pool A — regular constructs delayed bluffs on K-9-4-3 texture

answer.explanation: Turn call is correct (K8 top pair vs wide range). River call is correct because Kd + Ks (board) + Kh (board) = three kings accounted for, villain cannot hold a king. Villain's value narrows to three specific pocket-pair full houses while a real bluff population from missed draws exists. Turn sizing memory imprecision (60% vs 65%) is acceptable — the blocker fact is the load-bearing insight.

B.explanation: Against a passive rec, even with three kings accounted for, the three-barrel on a paired board is value-heavy. Passive recs don't fire triple-barrel bluffs with missed draws. Their range is 99, 44, 33 (full houses) and near-zero bluffs. Trips with 8 kicker folds against pool B.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Board has Ks and Kh. Hero holds Kd. What does this mean for villain's range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: exhaustive king blocker — three kings accounted for
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Three kings accounted for (Ks flop, Kh river, Kd hero). Villain cannot hold any king. K9, K4, K3 full houses are all impossible. Villain's value is limited to pocket-pair full houses: 99, 44, 33.

options.label: Three kings accounted for — villain cannot hold a king, removing all K-based full houses
Hero reduces villain's king combos by one — 25% of kings removed
Villain can still have hands like KQ or K9 — full houses are still possible

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: The turn size was around 60-65% but you're not certain. Does this imprecision change the river call?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn sizing memory uncertainty vs river blocker fact
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The river call is driven by the exhaustive king blocker — three kings accounted for. Whether the turn was 60% or 65% affects the pot size by a small amount but doesn't change the blocker effect or the fundamental bluff-to-value analysis on the river.

options.label: No — the blocker is a hard fact; the 5% sizing variation doesn't change the river analysis
Yes — turn sizing changes villain's range composition on the river
Without exact turn sizing, you cannot correctly evaluate the river

coaching_context.key_concept: Turn sizing imprecision within a 10% band is acceptable reconstruction uncertainty when the river decision is driven by a blocker fact. Three kings accounted for is a hard truth that doesn't depend on whether the turn was 60% or 65%.
common_mistakes: Folding trips on a paired board before checking the exhaustive blocker: always ask 'how many of this card are accounted for?' first.
Treating turn sizing uncertainty as blocking the river analysis when the blocker effect is independent of pot size arithmetic.
Not separating the turn call question from the river call question: they are different decisions with different driving factors.

value_buckets.label: Villain value (no kings possible)
value_buckets.note: Flopped/turned sets converted to full houses. No K-based value possible — three kings accounted for. Very narrow range.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low — three specific unlikely pocket pairs only

bluff_buckets.label: Missed draws that triple-barrel (regular)
bluff_buckets.note: A regular fires these draws as triple-barrel bluffs. The paired king is a credible representation — villain 'represents' the king while hero actually holds it.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial for pool A — regular constructs multi-street bluffs on K-9-4-3 texture

threshold_notes: At 72% pot: hero needs ~30% bluffs. With value range limited to 99, 44, 33 (three specific hands) and a real bluff population from missed draws, the threshold is met against pool A.
The exhaustive king blocker converts a potentially borderline spot (trips with 8 kicker on paired board) into a clear call by eliminating villain's primary value category.
blocker_notes: Kd + Ks (board) + Kh (board) = three kings gone. Villain cannot hold Kc (the only remaining king). K9 full house, K4 full house, K3 full house are all impossible.
8s provides no meaningful secondary blocker effect on villain's remaining value range (99, 44, 33 don't involve an 8).

what_changed_by_street.detail: Ks-9h-4c: standard c-bet range. AK, KQ for value; QJ, T8, 65 for draws. Wide range, hero's K8 is a solid bluff-catcher.

3d: slight connectivity added (65 now has a straight draw with 3-4-5-6-7 needing another card; straight texture on K-9-4-3). Villain fires again — consistent with value and committed semi-bluffs. Turn call correct.

Kh: three kings accounted for. Villain's K-based value is entirely eliminated. A regular fires the river with slow-played monsters (99, 44) or triple-barrel bluffs with missed draws. Hero has trips with a critical blocker — the river is a clear call.

coaching_context.follow_up: Live reconstruction habit: when the river pairs a high card you hold, immediately count board + hand. If three are accounted for, write 'villain can't have [that card]' at the top of your analysis before evaluating anything else.

notes: BTN vs BB SRP river bluff-catch lane — Claude batch 4 (live-hand reconstruction)

title: Q93 BB — Paired Q River After Turn Check-Through, Trips Threshold
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. BTN c-bets 33% on Qh 7d 3c, you call. Turn 5s — blank. BTN checks back. River Qd — top card pairs. BTN bets 75% pot. You hold Qs9d. Call or fold?

options.label: Call
Fold
Raise

answer.explanation: Qs9d has trip queens, and the turn check-through is the reason this call is not close. On a Qh7d3c board, BTN's natural double-barrel hands include AQ, KQ, and strong Q-x holdings — hands that would charge protection against a 7 or back-door equity. The blank 5s turn invited a barrel; BTN's pass means those strong Q-x combos are underrepresented. What survived the check: slow-played sets (77, 33), marginal Q-x (QJ, QT that pot-controlled), delayed floats (A7, T8, 86 type missed air that now fires a Q river as a bluff), and backdoor-flush misses. The Qd pairing the top card reduces BTN's clean AQ/KQ value combos while leaving the delayed-float bluff range intact. Hero holds the Qs, which trims one Q combo from BTN's value, tightening the already-limited strong-Qx set further. Trips here sit comfortably above threshold. Folding hands this strong to a delayed paired-Q bet would let BTN print equity by checking-back turns indefinitely.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: What did BTN's turn check on the blank 5s change about their range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn check-through range filtering
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: BTN's check removed most AQ/KQ that would barrel for protection and equity, leaving delayed floats and slow plays that increase the bluff density of the river bet.

options.label: The turn check mostly means BTN is capped at Q-x weaker hands, so the river bet is still value-heavy.
The check removed AQ/KQ double-barrel hands and preserved delayed floats, so the river range has more bluffs mixed in.
The 75% size is large enough that we should fold even trips against a paired river.

coaching_context.key_concept: Turn check-throughs inflate the bluff density of subsequent river bets, raising the threshold of what must fold.
common_mistakes: Treating any sizeable bet on a paired river as representing a full house, ignoring that AQ/KQ would have barreled the turn.
Forgetting that the Qs in hero's hand reduces the number of Q combos remaining in BTN's value range.

value_buckets.label: 77 and 33 (sets, likely slow-played) — present in BTN's river bet
combos: 77 and 33 (sets, likely slow-played) — present in BTN's river bet

value_buckets.label: QJ, QT (marginal Q-x that pot-controlled the turn) — possible river value
combos: QJ, QT (marginal Q-x that pot-controlled the turn) — possible river value

value_buckets.label: Rare QQ or slow-played AQ (very thin) — a small subset that checked back turn
combos: Rare QQ or slow-played AQ (very thin) — a small subset that checked back turn

bluff_buckets.label: A7s, T8s, 86s — missed backdoor draws or gut-shots that arrived to river as pure bluffs
combos: A7s, T8s, 86s — missed backdoor draws or gut-shots that arrived to river as pure bluffs

bluff_buckets.label: Axs hands that had backdoor-spade equity on flop and now fire the paired Q as air
combos: Axs hands that had backdoor-spade equity on flop and now fire the paired Q as air

threshold_notes: Trips on paired-top boards after turn check-throughs sit above threshold at any normal river size because the delayed float range is too large to price out.
At overbet sizes (130%+), trips are still a call here — only hands well below pairs should fold.
blocker_notes: Qs in hero's hand removes Qs from BTN's value set, cutting AQs and KQs options by one combo each.
The Q blocker is pointing the right direction here: it reduces the value region without affecting the bluff pool.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Small 33% c-bet keeps BTN's range wide — not a strong range-bet signal.
Turn check-through on blank 5s removes AQ/KQ natural barrels and locks in a float-heavy river range for BTN.
Paired Q reduces BTN's remaining clean Q-x value combos while leaving missed backdoor floats as bluffs.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study paired-board rivers after single streets of aggression to see when trips fall below threshold — usually only when both streets were bet.

title: K9 BB — Ace Scare After Turn Check-Through, Wrong-Direction K Blocker
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. BTN c-bets 50% on Kh 9c 6d, you call. Turn 2s — blank. Both check. River Ah — top scare card. BTN bets 60% pot. You hold Kd7c. Call or fold?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: Kd7c is now second pair after the Ah rivers. The turn check-through is critical: on Kh9c6d, BTN would normally fire a second barrel with AK (top two, wants to build vs draws), KQ, or KJ (top pair protecting vs J or Q backdoors). The blank 2s turn invited a barrel and BTN passed — this removed most clean AK double-barrel combos. But the Ah arrival changes the calculus. What survives in BTN's delayed betting range after a turn check on K-high board? Slow-played A9/A6 (two pair now), A-x that floated flop and now has pair-plus value, trapped AK (some), and missed draws (T8, J8, 87) that are now pure bluffing with ace-high. Hero's Kd7c: second pair against a range that has reasonable Ax value coverage. The K blocker in hero's hand points the wrong direction — it reduces BTN's KQ/KJ two-pair combos but does nothing to reduce AK (the scariest two-pair value). Against baseline and passive pools, the ace scare after a turn check-through nets value-heavy because floats that miss do bluff, but Ax slow-plays are real.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players are highly likely to fire ace rivers as bluffs after checking back a blank turn. They see the ace as a story-telling card — 'I slow-played an ace the whole way.' Against Pool C, the bluff frequency on this line is high enough that second pair with a K kicker crosses the calling threshold. The wrong-direction K blocker matters less when BTN is bluffing at twice the expected frequency.

B.explanation: Passive recreational players rarely fire delayed ace-river barrels with missed air. If a Pool B player checked back the K-high turn and then fires when the ace arrives, they either trapped AK or picked up two pair with A9/A6. Second pair with a 7 kicker is too weak to defend against a value-concentrated range.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Kd. Does this blocker help or hurt the calling case?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker direction after turn check-through
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The Kd reduces KQ/KJ combos for BTN (less relevant value on an ace river) but does not reduce AK (the dominant new value). The K blocker is pointing at weaker, less dangerous value, leaving the AK region untouched.

options.label: The K blocker is helpful because it removes KQ and KJ from BTN's range, which are strong value hands.
The K blocker reduces secondary value (KQ, KJ) but leaves the scariest value (AK two pair) untouched — it points the wrong direction on an ace river.
At 60% pot the blocker is irrelevant — the size alone determines whether to call.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: What did BTN's turn check preserve that made the ace river more dangerous than it looks?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn check on K-high board preserves Ax slow-plays
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: BTN's turn check preserved A9/A6 slow-plays (two pair now) and floated AK (some) that didn't want to barrel a blank. When the ace rivers, those trapped value combos activate and bet for value.

options.label: The turn check means BTN's range is capped at one pair — the ace river just adds a bluff card.
The turn check preserved Ax slow-plays (A9, A6) and possibly trapped AK, all of which now bet for value when the ace comes.
Live players always bluff ace rivers — the turn line doesn't affect this.

coaching_context.key_concept: On K-high boards, ace rivers after turn check-throughs activate slow-played two-pair combos that stayed silent when the board was K-high. The blocker analysis shifts: the card that would help is an A, not a K.
common_mistakes: Calling with top pair on the flop and forgetting that second pair on a scare river is a different decision.
Assuming the K in hand is a useful blocker without checking which value combos it actually removes (it removes KQ/KJ, not AK).

value_buckets.label: AK (some slow-played or trapped, now top two pair) — activates on the ace river
combos: AK (some slow-played or trapped, now top two pair) — activates on the ace river

value_buckets.label: A9, A6 (two pair, slow-played from flop) — significant value contribution
combos: A9, A6 (two pair, slow-played from flop) — significant value contribution

value_buckets.label: 99, 66 (sets, slow-played all three streets) — possible but rare
combos: 99, 66 (sets, slow-played all three streets) — possible but rare

bluff_buckets.label: T8, J8, 87 type missed draw hands — now pure air, fire ace as bluff story
combos: T8, J8, 87 type missed draw hands — now pure air, fire ace as bluff story

bluff_buckets.label: Ax-hands that floated flop with ace-high and missed but now semi-bluff with pair
combos: Ax-hands that floated flop with ace-high and missed but now semi-bluff with pair

threshold_notes: Second pair (K-7 kicker) is borderline at 60% vs baseline — the Ax value combos activated by the ace river tilt the range slightly value-heavy.
Against Pool C the threshold shifts: the same hand is a call because the bluff density from aggressive players spiking ace-river fires is high enough to compensate.
blocker_notes: Kd blocks KQ and KJ combos for BTN — each removed by about 1-2 combos. But AK, the primary new value, is not reduced.
To make this a clear call, hero would need Ad or As — that would remove roughly 4-6 AK combos and significantly lower the value density.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Half-pot c-bet on K96 keeps BTN's range wide but signals some interest in the board.
Turn check on blank 2s removed AK/KQ natural double-barrel combos, but also preserved Ax slow-plays and trapped AK — the dangerous portion of the range.
Ace arrival activates A9/A6 two-pair value and trapped AK, converting passive turn-checkers into genuine river bettors. Bluffs (missed draws) are present but value-loaded.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study ace-river blocker construction: when the scare card itself is the key value card, you need the A blocker, not the board-pair blocker.

title: T85 BB — Middle Pair Probed Turn, BTN Called, River Pairs Top
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. BTN c-bets 50% on Th 8d 5c, you call. Turn 2s — blank. You lead 35% pot (probe). BTN calls. River Tc — top card pairs. You check. BTN bets 65% pot. You hold 9h 8h. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero's turn probe with middle pair (8s) on Th8d5c invited BTN to define their hand — and BTN called. A call of a probe on this board signals real strength: Tx (top pair with various kickers), 88/55 (sets), 98 (two pair), and strong pocket pairs that decided to pot-control and now hold their ground. When the Tc pairs the top card, BTN's calling range improves significantly: Tx becomes trips, 88/55 becomes full houses, 98 (if any) becomes trips. The river bet from BTN is firing into a range that already showed probe-calling strength, and the paired-T board hits that calling range hard. Hero's 9h8h is middle pair (8s) on a T-T-8-5-2 board — a second pair on the river. The turn probe created this problem: by betting into BTN's strong range, hero invited a caller whose range now runs over the river texture. Calling here would be assuming BTN's probe-call on a T-high board was made with air that doesn't improve — that is a poor pool assumption for live cash.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: BB probed 35% on the blank turn and BTN called. What does BTN's turn call tell you about the river decision?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: probe-call range on T-high board
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: BTN calling a probe on Th8d5c board signals Tx (strong top pair), sets, or two pair — hands that are content to call and collect. When the T pairs, those hands improve significantly, making BTN's river bet value-heavy against hero's middle pair.

options.label: The probe weakened BTN's range by folding out air, so the river is actually cleaner for hero.
BTN calling the probe on a T-high board means their range is concentrated around Tx, sets, and two pair — all of which improve on the paired T river.
Live players call probes with all kinds of hands, so BTN's range is still wide by river.

coaching_context.key_concept: When BB probes the turn and BTN calls, the probe has revealed information in the wrong direction — BTN's call now constrains what can arrive on the river from BTN's range. Paired boards that hit the probe-caller's range remove much of the call-equity.
common_mistakes: Probing the turn with a marginal middle pair without considering what hand range calls the probe on a top-pair-heavy board.
Treating the river call decision as if the probe never happened — the probe defined BTN's range and it arrived on the river.

value_buckets.label: Tx (T-J, T-Q, T-A, T-K) — called probe for value or protection, now has trips
combos: Tx (T-J, T-Q, T-A, T-K) — called probe for value or protection, now has trips

value_buckets.label: 88, 55 (sets on flop) — slow-played to collect more, now full houses
combos: 88, 55 (sets on flop) — slow-played to collect more, now full houses

value_buckets.label: 98 type two-pair (if applicable) — called probe with strong made hand
combos: 98 type two-pair (if applicable) — called probe with strong made hand

bluff_catchers.label: 9h8h (middle pair 8s, second pair on river) — a bluff-catcher that now sits below threshold
combos: 9h8h (middle pair 8s, second pair on river) — a bluff-catcher that now sits below threshold

bluff_catchers.label: 76s or 64s type draws — those hands folded to the probe, not present in river bluff range
combos: 76s or 64s type draws — those hands folded to the probe, not present in river bluff range

threshold_notes: Second pair (8s) on a paired-top board, facing a range that called a probe, is below threshold for any reasonable river bet size.
Hero would need a very strong blocker to change this — like holding Tc (but it's on the board) or 88 (but that would be a set).

what_changed_by_street.detail: BTN's 50% c-bet on T85 represents top pair, sets, and strong over-pairs — all present in the probe-calling range.
BB's probe defined the dynamic: BTN called with a range that beats middle pair. The probe preserved no new bluffs — it filtered them out.
Tc paired the top card. BTN's probe-calling range (Tx, sets) improved significantly. The value-to-bluff ratio in BTN's river betting range is now very skewed toward value.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study probe-bet discipline: probing thin hands on boards that strongly favor the c-bettor's range creates downstream river decisions from a positional disadvantage.

title: K94 BB — Probe-Raised by BTN on Turn, River Ace, Top Pair Folds
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. BTN c-bets 45% on Kd 9h 4c, you call. Turn Jh. You probe 40% pot. BTN raises to 2.5x your probe. You call. River As. You check. BTN bets 60% pot. You hold Kc 6s. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: The probe-raise on the turn already told the river story. On a Kd9h4c board with Jh arriving on the turn, BB's probe represented thin value or a semi-bluff. BTN's 2.5x raise of that probe in live cash is not a wide play — it narrows to KQ/KJ (top pair upgrades), KK/99/44/JJ (sets), and strong flush draws (KhQh, KhTh, AhXh for combo-draw semi-bluffs). Most live regulars and even aggressive recreationals do not raise probe bets as pure bluffs. Hero called the turn raise with TPTK — marginal but not a mistake given pot odds. The river As now activates AK (top two pair), AJ (two pair), and flush-draw semi-bluff combos that arrived holding Ah. BTN fires 60% into this board. Hero's Kc6s is second pair on the river — the K is now dominated by AK, and the 6 kicker is irrelevant. Calling would require BTN's raise-call-barrel line to include enough bluffs. In live $2/$5, turn probe-raises almost never include that level of bluff density from IP players.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players raise probe bets with a much wider range — mid-pair, gutshot plus pair, even stubborn K-x hands that refuse to fold. Against Pool C, the turn raise range includes enough semi-bluffs and impatient floats that the river barrel is not exclusively value. With a wide enough turn-raise range, the ace-river bet can include enough missed draws to justify calling with second pair.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: BTN raised BB's turn probe on Kd9h4c-Jh. What does the raise tell you about BTN's river betting range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: probe-raise range concentration
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: A turn probe-raise in live cash is a narrow action — it removes weaker one-pair hands and concentrates BTN's range around strong made hands, two pairs, sets, and combo semi-bluffs. When the ace rivers, those strong made hands now include AK (top two) and AJ, and the semi-bluffs that arrived as draws may have completed.

options.label: Live players raise probes with all sorts of hands, so BTN's range is still wide by river.
The probe-raise narrows BTN's range to strong made hands, strong draws, and sets — all of which benefit from an ace river. The raise already told us the river answer.
The ace river is the only thing that matters — ignore the turn raise when evaluating the river bet.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: What is the primary leak this hand is correcting?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: carrying the wrong range through a turn raise
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The player carried TPTK into a turn raise that clearly indicated BTN had a range beating or dominating TPTK. The river decision feels hard because hero is still thinking 'top pair is a call,' but the turn raise already reclassified hero's TPTK as below threshold.

options.label: The leak is calling the ace river — the turn call was fine but the river was the mistake.
The leak started on the turn: calling a probe-raise with TPTK when the raise range already beat or dominated TPTK. The river decision was pre-determined by the turn reading mistake.
The probe itself was the mistake — should not have bet the turn.

coaching_context.key_concept: When BTN raises a probe, the river decision is downstream of a turn reading mistake. A probe-raise in live cash concentrates BTN's range so heavily toward made hands that calling with top pair becomes a threshold error before any river card is dealt.
common_mistakes: Calling a probe-raise with TPTK and mentally treating it as a 'see what happens on river' call, rather than recognizing the raise already announced a range advantage.
Re-evaluating the river in isolation when the turn raise already communicated which range held the edge.

threshold_notes: Top pair with a 6 kicker (Kc6s) is below threshold when BTN's range was filtered to strong hands by a turn raise.
Against the baseline probe-raise range in live cash, hero needs at least two pair or better to continue on this river.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 45% c-bet on K94 represents BTN's value-oriented range — strong Kx and sets.
BB's probe was answered with a raise — the raise capped BBs 'hero call' stack while announcing BTN has a range that beats TPTK. The probe created the river problem by inviting the raise.
As activates AK (top two pair) and confirms flush-draw semi-bluffs that arrived. BTN's range was already value-concentrated from the turn raise; the ace only makes it more so.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study turn probe-raise dynamics: the raise is a stronger signal than any single river card. When BTN raises a probe in live cash, recalibrate your range estimate before the river card is dealt.

title: A96 BB — Middle Pair, Brick-River Overbet After Turn Check-Through
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. BTN c-bets 50% on Ah 9d 6c, you call. Turn 2s — blank. Both check. River 4h — brick. BTN overbets 130% pot. You hold 9c 8c. Call or fold?

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: The turn check-through on an ace-high board is the diagnostic key. On Ah9d6c, BTN's natural double-barrel range includes AK, AQ, AJ (top pair protecting against draws and backdoors), and strong Ax combos. The blank 2s turn invited a second barrel from any hand that wanted to rep top pair — BTN passed. This severely limits BTN's clean value density on the river. What arrives to an overbet after a turn check on an A-high board? Slow-played sets (99, 66), two pairs (A9, A6 from flop interaction), and delayed-float bluffs — hands like T8, J8, 87 that picked up nothing on turn or river and now fire the 4h as a polar bluff. Hero's 9c8c: middle pair (9s) + club blocker. Against a 130% overbet from a delayed position (checking the turn on A-high and overbetting a brick river), in live $2/$5 pools the bluff frequency is high enough to call. Players who want to rep an ace typically fire the turn. Players who overbet the river here are polarizing — and that polar bluff range is real and frequent enough in live cash to justify calling with a medium-strength made hand.

B.explanation: Passive recreational players almost never fire 130% pot overbets as bluffs after checking back a blank turn. If a Pool B player checks the A-high turn and then overbets the brick river, they have a slow-played set, two pair, or a very strong Ax hand. Against Pool B, the value density of this line is too high for middle pair to justify calling.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: BTN checked back the turn on Ah9d6c and now overbets a brick river 4h. What range structure does this create?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: overbet after turn check-through on A-high board
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn check removed most natural AK/AQ barrels. The river overbet after a turn check creates a polar range: slow-played value (sets, Ax two pairs) versus delayed-float bluffs that fire the brick as a polar play. On A-high boards, the bluff bucket is real because the turn check filtered the range in a way that leaves significant air.

options.label: A 130% overbet after turn check always means sets or two pair — fold middle pair.

The turn check removed natural barrels, leaving a polar river range: slow-played value and delayed float bluffs. The overbet represents both, and middle pair is above threshold given the bluff frequency.
Overbet sizing alone determines the decision — pool type and turn line don't matter.

coaching_context.key_concept: On ace-high boards, BTN's turn check-through eliminates most AK/AQ double-barrel range, inflating the bluff density of any subsequent river overbet. Calling with a medium made hand is the exploit against the over-polar bluffing pattern in live pools.
common_mistakes: Treating any overbet on an ace-high board as automatically value-heavy, regardless of whether BTN skipped a natural turn barrel.
Folding middle pair to any river overbet without reconstructing the range that arrives after the turn check.

combos: A9, A6 (two pair, slow-played from flop) — the most likely value in this line

combos: T8, J8, 87 type missed draws — fire the brick river as a polar bluff after turning nothing

bluff_buckets.label: Backdoor-flush-draw misses with Xh hands — the 4h river gives these a 'complete the story' bluff opportunity
combos: Backdoor-flush-draw misses with Xh hands — the 4h river gives these a 'complete the story' bluff opportunity

threshold_notes: Middle pair (9s) is above threshold vs the full overbet range when BTN's turn check inflates the bluff bucket significantly.
Against Pool B (passives), middle pair falls below threshold — passives rarely overbet-bluff on brick rivers after checking back turn.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on A96 is a standard bet — represents a wide range including Ax and pocket pairs.
Turn check on blank 2s is the key signal: BTN chose not to fire a second barrel, removing most AK/AQ combos from the pure-value river range.

Brick 4h confirms no draw improved. BTN's 130% overbet is polar: the value is slow-played, the bluffs are delayed floats. The turn check-through is what makes calling middle pair non-trivial but correct in baseline pools.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study how the turn check-through on A-high boards changes the calling threshold at different river sizes — at 65% pot the call is easier; at 200% the answer shifts.

title: 974 BB — Top Pair Probed Turn, BTN Called, Brick River Overbet
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. BTN c-bets 40% on 9d 7h 4c, you call. Turn Jd. You probe 45% pot. BTN calls. River 2s — brick. You check. BTN overbets 110% pot. You hold 9h 8h. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero's turn probe on the Jd turn with top pair (9s) was reasonable thin value: BTN c-bet 40% on 9d7h4c and BB called, so BB leading the turn J is an attempt to maintain value and deny BTN's equity from straights (T8 was an open-ender on the flop). BTN called the probe. BTN's turn calling range after a probe: Jx (top pair now on the J), 99/77/44 (sets), T8 (made the straight 7-8-9-T-J on the Jd turn!), pair-plus-draw combinations, and hands that floated with a plan. The J was not a blank — T8 made a straight on this turn. River 2s is a brick, and BTN fires 110% pot. Who fires a 110% overbet into BB after calling a probe on a J-completed-straight board? Primarily: hands that have a strong made hand (J-x trips, sets, straights) and are going for max value. The bluff bucket is thin: BTN calling a probe signals purpose, and live players rarely call probes as an IP float and then fire an overbet on the river brick. Hero's 9h8h = top pair on the flop, now just second pair (9s after J, lower than the J on board). The 8h in hero's hand does not block the made straight (T8 made it — hero has 8h, and T8 needs an 8 and a T, but hero's 8h would actually block some T8-suited combos: T8h... so hero does block some T8h hands but not all T8 combos). The blocker is marginally helpful but not enough to justify a call vs a concentrated range.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players call probes with a much wider range — pairs, gut-shots, even random floats — and then fire river overbets with position regardless of hand strength. Against Pool C, the probe-call range is wide enough to include enough air that the 110% overbet is not exclusively value. Second pair with a 8h partial blocker crosses the call threshold against Pool C's overbet frequency.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero probed the Jd turn with top pair (9s). BTN called. River is a brick. BTN overbets 110%. What does the probe-call change about the blocker analysis?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker relevance after probe-call dynamic
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Hero's 8h slightly reduces T8h combos (part of the straight that made on Jd turn). But the dominant made hands in BTN's probe-calling range (Jx trips, 99/77/44 sets, T8 straight) are mostly not blocked by hero's 8h and 9h. The blocker is too narrow to move the threshold materially.

options.label: The 8h in hero's hand blocks T8h, which made the straight on the J turn. This blocker is strong enough to justify a call.
Hero's 8h removes some T8h combos but does not block the full T8 straight or the J-x trips and sets in BTN's probe-calling range. The blocker effect is too small to change the threshold.
The probe result doesn't matter for the river decision — evaluate the river bet on its own.

coaching_context.key_concept: When BB probes the turn and BTN calls, the probe invitation was accepted — BTN's call signals a hand that beats or has equity against the probe. An overbet from BTN on the river brick after calling a probe is value-concentrated in baseline and passive pools.
common_mistakes: Over-valuing a partial blocker (8h removes some T8h combos) while ignoring that the turn probe-call dynamic itself concentrated BTN's range toward made hands.
Conflating 'I have a blocker' with 'I am above threshold' when the range analysis shows the blocker covers a small fraction of the danger.

combos: 99, 77, 44 (sets from flop) — called probe and now fire for full-value extraction

combos: 9h8h (top pair 8-kicker, now second pair behind J on board) — a bluff-catcher positioned below threshold

bluff_catchers.label: Second-pair type hands from BB's range: most would fold to an overbet given the probe-call context
combos: Second-pair type hands from BB's range: most would fold to an overbet given the probe-call context

threshold_notes: Second pair (9s) after calling a probe on a board that includes a completed straight (T8 on J-turn) is below threshold for a 110% overbet in baseline and passive pools.
Against Pool C (aggro rec), the threshold shifts: their probe-calling range is wider, including floats and lower-equity hands, making their overbet range contain enough bluffs to justify calling.
blocker_notes: Hero's 8h removes some T8h combos from BTN's range but not the full T8 off-suit straight. Net impact: reduces BTN's value by roughly 1-2 combos out of a concentrated value range.
The key blocker for this spot would be a T (reducing T8 and TJ combos) — hero doesn't have that.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 40% c-bet on 974 is a small probe from BTN. T8 picked up open-ended straight draw (to J for 7-8-9-T-J or to 5 for 4-5-6-7-8... actually T8 needs 6 for 6-7-8-9-T or J for 7-8-9-T-J).
Jd completed T8's straight. BB's probe on the J created the river problem: BTN's call range now includes the made straight, Jx top pair, and sets — all ahead of hero's second pair on the river.

Brick 2s confirms no draws improved. BTN's overbet is powered by the value that called the probe — Jx, T8 straight, sets. The probe-call dynamic created a river where BTN fires confidently into an OOP range.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study when turn probes create losing river dynamics: if the turn card improves the c-bettor's calling range more than your own range, probing invites a reverse dynamic where IP is now the aggressor with a range advantage.

title: K95 BB — Triple Barrel Into K-8 Kicker, Wrong-Direction K Blocker on Ace River
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. BTN c-bets 50% on Kc 9h 5d, you call. Turn 7s. BTN bets 65% pot, you call. River As. BTN bets 75% pot. You hold Kd 8c. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Three streets of aggression from BTN concentrate the range significantly. On Kc9h5d, BTN c-bet flop (standard). Then on the 7s turn, BTN fires again for 65%: this barrel range includes KQ/KJ (protecting vs gut-shot and backdoor equity on 9-7 board), 99/55 (sets building value), 77 (just made a set), 87s/86s (pair+draw semi-bluffs), and strong over-pairs. Hero called two streets with TPTK (Kd8c). The river As arrives. BTN fires a third barrel for 75%. AK is now the dominant value combination — two pair. The As also activates A9/A7/A5 type two pairs for any Ax hands that semi-bluffed the turn and arrived. Hero's Kd8c = second pair (8c is below the A and J on the board by rank — wait, actually the board is Kc9h5d7s-As, so hero's 8c is below the river card). Hero has top pair (Ks) but the board now has Ace as highest card. Hero's Kd8c = second pair K (behind A). The Kd blocker is the key diagnostic: holding Kd reduces KQ and KJ combos for BTN (removes Kd from their Kx holdings) but does absolutely nothing to reduce AK — BTN's AK would need an A and a K, and hero only removes the Kd component. Against a triple barrel with an ace river, the dominant danger is AK (top two), and hero's K blocker points at weaker Kx value, not at AK.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players triple-barrel bluff at a noticeably higher frequency than regulars. In live $2/$5, a Pool C player who fires all three streets may well be firing the ace as a bluff-narrative ('I had AK the whole time'). Against Pool C, the triple-barrel bluff frequency on this line is high enough that second pair with a Kd blocker crosses the calling threshold.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Kd, which blocks some of BTN's Kx value. Does this blocker justify a call on the As river?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker direction: blocking secondary value vs primary danger
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Kd reduces KQ/KJ for BTN by one combo each, but the primary dangerous value on an ace river is AK. Kd removes the K component of AK from BTN's combos (Kd is in hero's hand, so BTN can't hold AKd). That removes roughly 3 AK combos from 12 possible (Kd plus 3 aces = 3 combos removed). But 9 AK combos remain. The K blocker reduces secondary value (KQ, KJ) while leaving most of the dangerous AK range intact.

options.label: Kd removes KQ and KJ from BTN's range — those are strong value hands, so the K blocker helps significantly.
Kd blocks KQ/KJ (secondary value) but leaves AK mostly intact — 9 of 12 AK combos remain. On an ace river, the dangerous value is AK, and K is the wrong blocker to have.
Against a triple barrel, blockers are irrelevant — just fold everything.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: BTN barreled the 7s turn for 65% on a Kc9h5d board. What did that turn barrel preserve and remove from BTN's range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn barrel range shaping
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The 65% turn barrel on Kc9h5d-7s filters out weak air and keeps strong Kx, sets (including 77 newly made), pair+draw hands (87s, 86s), and over-pairs. It removes hands that were bluffing and didn't want to continue. The barrel concentrates value and semi-bluffs with real equity — leaving less 'pure air' that would bluff a third street.

options.label: The turn barrel preserves all kinds of hands — BTN can barrel bluffs just as well as value.
The 65% turn barrel filters out weak air and concentrates BTN's range toward strong Kx, sets, and semi-bluffs with real equity. A third barrel from this range is value-leaning.
The turn barrel doesn't tell us anything new — it's just a standard barrel.

coaching_context.key_concept: Three streets of aggression narrow BTN's range significantly. On an ace river, the K blocker in hero's hand reduces wrong-direction value (KQ/KJ) while leaving the primary danger (AK two pair) mostly intact. The turn barrel — not the river card — is the real range filter.
common_mistakes: Thinking 'I have a K so I block value' without checking which value the K actually removes on this specific river (AK vs KQ/KJ are very different blockers on an ace river).
Calling a triple barrel with a blocking-justification that only applies to secondary value while the primary danger is unblocked.

combos: AK (top two pair after ace river) — 9 of 12 combos remain after hero holds Kd

value_buckets.label: 99, 55, 77 (sets — 77 made on turn) — unaffected by hero's Kd
combos: 99, 55, 77 (sets — 77 made on turn) — unaffected by hero's Kd

value_buckets.label: KQ, KJ (strong Kx that barreled for value on K-high turn) — reduced by Kd but not eliminated
combos: KQ, KJ (strong Kx that barreled for value on K-high turn) — reduced by Kd but not eliminated

bluff_buckets.label: 87s, 86s, 76s (pair+draw semi-bluffs that fired turn) — some missed and are now river bluffs
combos: 87s, 86s, 76s (pair+draw semi-bluffs that fired turn) — some missed and are now river bluffs

bluff_buckets.label: Axs hands that fired turn as semi-bluffs — now have Ax pair value on river, reducing pure bluff count
combos: Axs hands that fired turn as semi-bluffs — now have Ax pair value on river, reducing pure bluff count

threshold_notes: Second pair (Kd8c on As-K-9-7-5 board = hero holds K which is now second-ranked behind A) is below threshold against a concentrated three-street betting range.
Against Pool C (triple-barrel bluffers), second pair crosses back above threshold because the bluff frequency of the full triple-barrel range is high enough.
blocker_notes: Kd removes 3 AK combos from BTN's range (A-Kd, so Ad, Ah, Ac all paired with BTN's K are gone — but BTN's Kd is blocked, meaning BTN can't have AKd). Roughly 9 AK combos remain unblocked.
The ideal blocker for this spot would be an A (blocking AK completely across all suits) or the 9 (blocking 99 sets). Hero has neither.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on K95 — standard value range, includes AK, KQ, KJ, 99, 55.

65% barrel on 7s concentrates BTN's range: removes weak air, keeps strong Kx, sets (including new 77), and semi-bluffs with equity. This barrel is the main range filter — by river, BTN is committed to value or strong draws.

As activates AK (top two), AJ, A7 (two pair), and rewards any Ax semi-bluff that survived the turn barrel. The triple barrel on this river is value-concentrated except against pool C's aggro-bluff frequency.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study the turn barrel as the primary range filter in three-street sequences. By identifying what the turn barrel removes and preserves, the river decision becomes a readout of what survived — not a fresh evaluation.

title: J95 BB — Multi-Step: Blocker Shifts When Turn Line Changes from Check to Barrel
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. Board: Jh 9c 5d | 8s | Ks. You hold Jd 7h. Evaluate two different turn lines and their effect on your river bluff-catching threshold.

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: Hero's Jd pairs the flop but is now second pair behind the Ks river. The 7h is a partial straight-draw blocker: on Jh9c5d|8s board, T7 and 76 had straight draws. Hero holding 7h blocks 7T-suited hearts and 76-suited hearts, but these are a small fraction of BTN's bluff range. The more important analysis is the turn: both players checked. BTN's turn check on J9-5-8 (a board that includes a nearly-connected run J-9-8) removed the barrel range of hands like KJ, QJ, AT type strong hands that would want to build value. What survived the turn check: slow-played sets (99/55, possibly 88 if BTN had it), Jx that pot-controlled, and missed draws (T7, T6, 76) that gained nothing on the 8s. When the Ks arrives and BTN fires 70%: value includes Ks combos activating (KJ top two, K9 two pair, K8 two pair for 88/8x), sets (99/55 now have boat potential on K river), and missed draw hands that fire the K as a story card. Against this range, hero's Jd7h = second pair (J) with a partial 7 blocker — well below threshold for a 70% bet from a delayed-barrel player.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds 7h. On this board (J9-5-8-K), does the turn check-through make the 7 blocker more or less valuable?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker value conditioned on turn line
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The 7 blocker is most useful if BTN's bluffs are concentrated around 7x hands (T7, 76). But the turn check removed many of those straight-draw hands from BTN's betting range (they would fire the 8 turn for equity). After a turn check, BTN's bluff range on the Ks river is more likely to be Kx air or pure story-bluffs — not 7x type missed draws. The 7 blocker loses value when the turn check filters out the very hands it was meant to block.

options.label: The turn check means BTN still has 7x draws, so the 7h blocker is very useful here.
BTN's turn check filtered out most T7/76 draw hands that would have fired. The 7h blocker now covers a smaller fraction of BTN's actual river bluff range — it's less useful than it looks.
The 7h blocker is irrelevant because blockers never matter against a turn check-through.

steps.prompt: SCENARIO A (actual): Both players checked the 8s turn. BTN fires 70% on Ks river. You hold Jd 7h (second pair after K). Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Turn check-through on J9-5-8 removes BTN's natural barrel range (KJ, QJ, Ax draws). River K activates KJ/K9/K8 (KJ now top two, K9/K8 two pair), and remaining air fires as a story bluff. Hero's second pair (J) with a 7 blocker that now covers a smaller bluff pool after the turn filter is well below threshold at 70%. Fold.

steps.prompt: SCENARIO B (alternate): BTN barreled 55% on the 8s turn instead of checking. You called. Same Ks river, BTN bets 70%. Same hand Jd 7h. Does the turn barrel change whether you call?

options.label: Fold — turn barrel concentrated range, still fold
Call — turn barrel preserved more bluffs, makes it closer

answer.explanation: When BTN barreled the 8s turn, their range is even more concentrated than the check-through scenario. Turn barrels on a J-9-8 board represent strong made hands (KJ, QJ, sets, two pair) and semi-bluffs (T7, 76 equity). By the time those hands barrel the turn and then the river K, the value density is highest — AK/KJ (if over-cards came) or strong sets. Second pair with the 7 blocker is even further below threshold when BTN showed turn aggression. The 7 blocker is marginally more useful in the barrel scenario (more T7/76 in BTN's range), but not enough to cross the threshold.

coaching_context.key_concept: Blocker value is conditioned on the turn line. The 7h blocks T7/76 type straight draws — but a turn check removes many of those hands from BTN's river betting range. The blocker is most useful when the hands it blocks are still in the range, and the turn line is the key input to that analysis.
common_mistakes: Evaluating blockers in isolation from the turn line — a 7 blocker on a straight-draw board is valuable only if BTN kept those draw hands into the river, which the turn check-through reduces.
Treating second pair + a partial blocker as sufficient to call a turn-check-through delayed barrel when the river card activates BTN's Kx value.

combos: KJ (now top two pair on K river, if BTN slow-played flop) — significant value activation

combos: K9, K8 (two pair from K river + board pair) — activated by Ks
