bluff_catchers.label: K8, K7 type hands — would also be below threshold in baseline; might call in specific pool contexts
combos: K8, K7 type hands — would also be below threshold in baseline; might call in specific pool contexts

threshold_notes: K6 kicker is at the bottom of the calling range or just below threshold against a probe-calling range in baseline live cash.
Against Pool C at 50% pot: K6 kicker crosses above threshold because Pool C calls probes with many weaker hands, diluting the value concentration.
Against Pool B at any size: K6 kicker is clearly below threshold — passives don't call probes with air.
blocker_notes: Ks blocks KsX combos for BTN — removes KsQ, KsJ, KsT from BTN's range (3 combos from ~12 strong Kx combos).
The K blocker reduces BTN's value by roughly 25%, which is meaningful but not enough to push K6 kicker above threshold against the concentrated probe-call range.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 40% c-bet on K74 is a small range bet — BTN's range is wide including all Kx, pocket pairs, and equity draws.
BB's probe on 9s turn was the decisive action. BTN's call concentrated the range: weak air folded, strong hands called. The probe invited the response that made the river difficult.

3d brick confirms no draw improved. BTN's river bet is powered by the hands that called the probe — Kx better kicker, sets, two pair. At both moderate and large sizing, the probe-call dynamic puts hero's K6 kicker below threshold in baseline pools.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study sizing interpretation after probe-call dynamics: unlike turn check-throughs (which inflate bluff density), probe-calls concentrate BTN's range toward value. Sizing up after calling a probe is almost never a bluff in live cash.

title: Q94 BB — Paired Q River, Turn Size Ambiguous, Threshold Holds Either Way
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. Reviewing a hand from last night: BTN c-bet small on Qc 9h 4s. You called. The turn 6d — you're fairly sure it went check-check, but you might be misremembering a small BTN bet you called. Either way you reached the river. Qc paired on the river Qs. BTN bets 65% pot. You hold Jd 9d. Call or fold?

options.label: Call
Fold
Raise

answer.explanation: This is a case where your memory uncertainty does not change the strategic conclusion. Whether the turn went check-check or BTN bet small (30–40%) and you called, the river threshold lands on the same side. In the check-through version: BTN's range at the river is inflated with delayed floats and missed draws — pairs like 87, 86, 75, A6, K6 that gained nothing on the blank 6d and now bluff the paired-Q river. In the small-bet version: the range is slightly tighter but still carries enough missed semi-bluffs. Hero holds Jd9d: middle pair (9s on the Qc9h4s board) with a J kicker, plus the 9d blocks one combo of 99 (set). Against a delayed or lightly-barreled betting line, middle pair with a J kicker is above threshold at 65% pot. The Q pairing further reduces BTN's clean AQ/KQ value density since neither hand barreled the 6d turn, making the river bet lean bluff-mixed. Fold only if you are certain the turn was a full double-barrel — that version would tighten the range enough to push middle pair below threshold.

B.explanation: Passive recreational players firing a paired-Q river after checking or betting small on the turn are predominantly value-betting. Against Pool B, middle pair with a J kicker sits below threshold. The delayed float range that makes this a call in baseline pools is largely absent when the villain is a passive rec.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: You're not sure if the turn was check-check or a small BTN bet you called. Does this memory uncertainty change the river call?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: memory-ambiguous threshold: when the turn uncertainty does not flip the answer
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: At any normal turn sizing (check-through or small bet), BTN's river range after a blank 6d still contains enough delayed floats and missed draws to make middle pair + J kicker a call at 65%. The precise turn size is not strategically decisive here.

options.label: You need to know the exact turn action before you can make this river call — fold until you remember.
Whether the turn was check-check or a small bet you called, the river threshold lands on the same side. The memory ambiguity is not strategically decisive here.
The 65% pot bet size is the only thing that matters — ignore the turn entirely.

coaching_context.key_concept: Some turn-line memories are non-decisive: small size vs check-through on a blank turn produces similar enough river range compositions that the calling threshold does not change. The skill is knowing which turn details are load-bearing and which are not.
common_mistakes: Refusing to extract a lesson from a live hand review because the turn action is only approximately remembered.
Over-weighting a small turn sizing difference that doesn't materially change the bluff-to-value ratio on the river.

value_buckets.label: QQ (unlikely — would likely have played differently), Q-x with strong kicker (AQ, KQ) — some present but check-through reduces these
99, 44 (sets — slow-played through all streets) — rare but real
66 (set on the turn — possible) — firing for value

bluff_buckets.label: 87, 86, 75, A6, K6 — hands that floated the small flop c-bet and gained nothing on the 6d turn, now firing the paired Q as a bluff
Backdoor-flush misses with diamonds — Jd-type hands that had some equity and now fire as air

threshold_notes: Middle pair (9s) with a J kicker is above threshold at 65% pot in the check-through scenario because the delayed float range is large relative to the thin Q-x value.
In the small-bet-called scenario, the threshold shifts slightly but middle pair with J kicker still clears it — the turn bet was too small to filter out enough air.
The threshold flips to a fold only if the turn was a substantial barrel (55%+). Below that, memory precision does not affect the river call.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Small 33% c-bet on Q94 keeps BTN's range wide and doesn't signal strong Q-x commitment.
Whether checked or small-bet, the blank 6d did not filter BTN's air meaningfully — the delayed float range survives into the river under either version of the turn memory.
Qs pairs the top card, reducing BTN's clean AQ/KQ combos while preserving the delayed-float bluff range. Middle pair with J kicker sits above threshold.

coaching_context.follow_up: Identify which spots in your hand history truly require precise turn-size recall and which spots are robust to that uncertainty. Train the habit of classifying: 'memory-decisive' vs 'memory-tolerant' before extracting river lessons.

title: KT6 BB — Ace River After Double Barrel, Turn Size Fuzzy But Conclusion Clear
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. Reviewing a hand: BTN c-bet the Kd Tc 6h flop, you called. Turn 9s — BTN bet again. You called. You remember thinking it was a big turn bet, maybe 55–70% — not sure exactly. River Ah. BTN bets 70% pot. You hold Kh 8c. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Whether the turn bet was 55% or 70% is not what matters. What matters is that BTN barreled the turn — and that single fact shapes the river range. A double-barrel on a Kd-Tc-6h board with a 9s turn represents strong Kx (protecting against the connected board), sets (99/66, newly made or pre-existing), and two-pair hands (K9, T9, 96). When the Ah arrives and BTN fires a third barrel, the activated value includes AK (top two pair — the dominant danger) and AJ/AT (two pair). Hero holds Kh8c: second pair on the river (A now ranks higher), with the Kh blocking some Kx combos for BTN. But the Kh blocker points the wrong direction: it reduces KQ/KJ (secondary value) while leaving the primary danger — AK — mostly intact. Hero's K removes one AK combo per ace suit (Kh + Ac, Kh + Ad, Kh + As = 3 combos removed from roughly 12 AK combos pre-blocker). Nine AK combos remain unblocked. Against a three-street betting range where the river card activates AK top-two, fold is correct regardless of whether the turn was 55% or 70%.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players triple-barrel bluff at a notably higher frequency than standard live regulars. Against Pool C, the three-street betting range includes enough bluffs — 'I had AK the whole time' narratives fired on the ace — that second pair with K-8 kicker crosses the calling threshold. The K blocker in hero's hand marginally reduces AK combos, which helps against Pool C's bluff-heavy range.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Kh. On the Ah river after a double barrel, does the K blocker justify a call?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: K blocker wrong direction on ace-river after double barrel
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The Kh removes KQ/KJ from BTN's value but leaves AK — the primary danger on an ace river — mostly intact. After a double barrel, 9 of roughly 12 AK combos are still live. The K blocker reduces secondary value, not the dominant danger.

options.label: Kh blocks KQ and KJ strongly enough to justify calling — those are real value combos.
Kh removes KQ/KJ (secondary value) but leaves AK mostly intact. On an ace river, AK is the primary danger and K is the wrong blocker.
You need to know whether the turn was 55% or 70% before evaluating the blocker.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Your turn-size memory is fuzzy — somewhere between 55% and 70%. Does this change your fold decision?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: memory tolerance: double-barrel range is similar at any reasonable turn size
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: At both 55% and 70%, BTN's turn barrel represents a range concentrated around strong Kx, sets, and semi-bluffs with equity. The range composition is similar across that size range. The fold conclusion is robust to this particular memory gap.

options.label: At 55% the range is wider and you should call; at 70% it's narrower and you fold. You must remember the size.
Both 55% and 70% represent a committed double-barrel range. The fold is correct under either memory version — the size precision doesn't change the conclusion.
The ace river is all that matters — the turn size is irrelevant for evaluating the river bet.

coaching_context.key_concept: The turn size memory is fuzzy but the turn-barrel fact is clear — and the fact alone determines the range. When both 55% and 70% produce the same range composition for the subsequent decision, memory precision is not strategically required.
common_mistakes: Holding the K and thinking 'I block value' without tracing which value the K actually reduces on the specific river card.
Stalling on the review because the turn size is remembered only approximately, when the fold is correct under all plausible turn sizes.

value_buckets.label: AK (top two pair on Ah river) — 9 of 12 combos remain after hero's Kh blocker
99, 66 (sets — flop and turn sets) — full bet for value, present in double-barrel range
K9, T9 (two pair on 9s turn) — possible double-barrel value

bluff_buckets.label: QJ, J8, 87 type semi-bluffs that picked up straight equity on the 9s turn — some miss and bluff third street
Combo draws (flush + straight) that fired two streets and now miss on Ah

threshold_notes: Second pair (Kh8c on Ah-K-T-9-6 board) is below threshold against a double-barrel range that activates AK on the ace river.
The threshold is robust: even at the optimistic end of the turn-size memory (55%), the double-barrel range is concentrated enough to fold.
blocker_notes: Kh removes 3 AK combos out of approximately 12 (those containing Kh). Nine AK combos remain live.
The ideal blocker here is an Ah (blocking AK by the ace) — hero doesn't hold it. The K-side blocker is materially less useful on an ace river.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on KT6 signals a committed range — AK, strong Kx, sets, and suited over-pairs are all in the barrel range.

Turn barrel (approximately 55–70%) on the 9s confirms the range: filters out weak air, concentrates strong Kx, adds 99 (new set), K9/T9 (new two pairs). Whether 55% or 70%, the range composition is similar — the barrel fact is what matters, not the size precision.
Ah activates AK (top two pair) and AJ/AT (two pair). BTN's third barrel is value-concentrated with only pool-specific bluff exceptions.

coaching_context.follow_up: Practice categorizing live-hand memories into: (1) facts you know — was there a barrel or not; (2) details that are fuzzy — exact size. Train yourself to extract the lesson from the facts even when the details are approximate.

title: T73 BB — Middle Pair Probed Turn, Memory Blurs Whether You Led or Checked
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. You called BTN's 45% c-bet on Td 7c 3h. Turn 2s. You think you led small on the turn — maybe 30-35% — but you're honestly not sure if you probed or just checked. BTN called if you probed; or BTN checked if you didn't. River Tc — top card pairs. You checked. BTN bets 65% pot. You hold Ac 6d. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: The memory uncertainty here — whether you probed or checked — does not change the fold. In the probe scenario: you bet the turn, BTN called. On a T-7-3 board with a blank 2s, BTN calling your probe signals Tx (top pair), 77/33 (sets), and medium draws with pair equity. The Tc river improves all of those calling hands: Tx becomes trips, sets become full houses. BTN's river bet is heavily value-loaded. In the check-check scenario: BTN is now firing the Tc river as a delayed barrel. This range includes slow-played value and some floats — but even delayed, the paired T strongly favors BTN's flop-calling range. Hero holds Ac6d: ace-high with a 6. No pair, no flush draw on this board. Against BTN's river bet under either turn version, ace-high is clearly below threshold. This is the useful memory-tolerant lesson: even if you can't pin down the turn sequence, the fold is correct in both versions. The Ac provides zero relevant blocking on a paired-T board where the key value is Tx and sets.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: You're not sure if you probed the turn or checked. Does this memory gap change whether ace-high should call or fold?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: memory tolerance: fold is robust across both plausible turn lines
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Under both turn scenarios — probe-called or check-check — BTN's river bet on the paired-T is value-heavy relative to ace-high. The Ac6d has no bluff-catching value here regardless of turn sequence. The memory gap is irrelevant to the conclusion.

options.label: If you probed the turn, BTN's call showed strength and the fold is clear. If you checked, the delayed barrel might be a bluff — call in that version.
Ace-high is below threshold in both versions. Whether BTN called a probe or fired a delayed barrel, the paired-T river bet is too value-heavy for ace-high to call.
The Ac blocks AQ and AT type value — use it as a blocker justification to call.

coaching_context.key_concept: Some river decisions are 'memory-tolerant' at the level of the whole turn sequence: the conclusion holds even under the worst-case plausible version of the turn. When reviewing live hands with imperfect recall, confirm the fold is correct under all versions before moving on.
common_mistakes: Searching for a version of the turn that might justify a call, rather than checking whether the fold is correct under all plausible versions.
Treating the Ac as a relevant blocker on a paired-T board where the value is Tx and sets — the ace doesn't block any of those hands.

value_buckets.label: Tx (top pair on flop, trips on river) — present in both the probe-call and delayed-barrel range
77, 33 (sets on flop, full houses on river) — present in both versions
T7, T3 type two pairs — present in probe-call calling range

bluff_catchers.label: Ac6d (ace-high, no pair) — below threshold for any river bet on a paired-T board
Middle-pair hands (8x, 9x) might be marginally closer but still fold to most BTN bet sizes on this river

threshold_notes: Ace-high is below threshold against any normal river bet (50–80% pot) on a paired-T board, regardless of the turn action version.
The fold is correct whether you probed or checked — this is a memory-tolerant conclusion.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 45% c-bet on T73 by BTN is a committed bet on a board that hits their opening range well — Tx, sets, and over-pairs.

Approximate memory: either you probed and BTN called (range: Tx, sets, pair+draw) or check-check (range: slow-played value + some floats). Under both versions, the range arriving at the paired-T river is value-heavy relative to ace-high.
Tc pairs the top card. BTN's range — under either turn version — improves: Tx → trips, sets → full houses. River bet is value-concentrated relative to hero's ace-high.

coaching_context.follow_up: Practice 'worst-case turn' analysis: when a turn memory is unclear, ask which plausible version is most favorable to calling. If the fold is correct even in the most favorable version, the memory gap is irrelevant.

title: J85 BB — Brick River, Turn Check-Through Ambiguous, Pool Shifts the Threshold
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. Reviewing: BTN c-bet 45% on Jc 8d 5h. You called. Turn 3c. You're fairly sure both checked — you're 80% confident on this. River 2d — pure brick. BTN bets 65% pot. You hold Ah 9h. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: In the check-through version of the turn (which you believe is 80% likely), BTN's delayed river bet on a J-8-5-3-2 board is a polarized range: slow-played value (JJ, 88, 55 sets, J8/J5 two pairs) and delayed floats (T9, T7, 97 that gained nothing on the turn or river and now fire as a story bluff). Hero holds Ah9h: ace-high, no pair on this board. The A-high can beat bluffs but not value. The question is whether the bluff frequency is high enough at 65% pot for ace-high to call. Against a baseline live pool, the delayed-barrel frequency on a J-high brick-brick board is modest — most players who check back a J-high board have either given up or have a slow-play. The turn check removes the natural barrel range (AJ, KJ, QJ for protection), but the residual bluff pool on a brick-brick river is thin compared to boards with more draw density. Ace-high without a backdoor draw is below threshold against standard live pool delayed barrels.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players fire delayed river barrels with much higher frequency than regs or passive players — especially on brick-brick runouts where they can tell a 'slow-played jack' story. Against Pool C, the delayed-barrel bluff frequency is high enough that ace-high 9-kicker crosses the calling threshold at 65% pot. Punish the over-bluffer.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: BTN checked back the J85 turn and now fires a brick river. How does the turn check affect the bluff density of the river bet?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn check-through on J-high board: limited bluff preservation relative to draw-heavy boards
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: On a J-high board, the turn check removes natural AJ/KJ barrel range. The residual bluff pool (T9, T7 type floats) is present but thinner than on boards with more back-door draw density. The delayed-barrel bluff frequency is moderate, not high — and ace-high without pair value is borderline at best against baseline pools.

options.label: The turn check always creates a large bluff pool — call with ace-high because the delayed range is bluff-heavy.

On a J-high brick board, the turn check removes natural barrels but the residual bluff pool is modest — delayed bluffs exist but not at high frequency for standard pools. Ace-high is borderline, folding in baseline pools.
The pool type doesn't matter here — just evaluate whether the 65% size indicates value or bluff.

coaching_context.key_concept: Turn check-throughs inflate delayed bluff density most on boards with high back-door draw density. On flat J-high boards, the bluff inflation is real but modest — not enough for ace-high to call against standard populations. Pool type is decisive.
common_mistakes: Applying the generic 'turn check inflates bluffs' rule to J-high brick boards without adjusting for the limited back-door draw density.
Treating ace-high as a universal delayed-barrel call on any board where the turn was checked through.

value_buckets.label: JJ, 88, 55 (sets — slow-played through the turn check) — firing for value on brick river
J8, J5 (two pair) — checked turn to protect and now bet river
AJ (slow-played top two, though unlikely to check turn) — small presence

bluff_buckets.label: T9, T7, 97 (straight-draw floats on J85 flop that gained nothing on 3-2) — fire brick river as delayed bluff
A9/Ax type over-cards that floated with equity and now fire the brick as give-up bluffs

threshold_notes: Ace-high is below threshold at 65% in baseline and passive pools — the bluff pool from the turn check is present but not large enough on a J-high brick board.
Against Pool C (aggro rec), ace-high crosses the threshold because their delayed-barrel bluff frequency is significantly higher.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 45% c-bet on J85 represents a committed range — Jx, sets, AJ, and over-pairs. The flop c-bet is the primary signal of BTN's range.

Turn check-through on 3c (approximately recalled as check-check) removes AJ/KJ natural double-barrel range. Preserves slow-plays and limited floats. Bluff pool exists but is smaller than on boards with more draw density.
2d brick confirms no draws improved. BTN fires 65%. The bluff pool is real but only large enough for pool C's elevated bluffing frequency to justify a call — fold against baseline and passive pools.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study how board draw-density affects turn-check bluff inflation: T-9-8 type boards produce much larger delayed-bluff pools than J-8-5 brick boards. The same turn check has different strategic implications depending on how many draw hands it preserved.

title: K73 BB — Ace River, DECISIVE Memory: Check-Through Calls, Turn Barrel Folds
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. Reviewing from last session: BTN c-bet the Kd 7h 3c flop. You called. Turn 6s — here's your memory gap. You are genuinely not sure if the turn went check-check or if BTN bet (something around 40–50%) and you called. You know you reached the river. River Ac. BTN bets 65% pot. You hold Kh 9s. Call or fold?

options.label: Call — if it was check-check
Fold — if BTN barreled the turn

answer.explanation: This is a memory-decisive spot. The correct action depends specifically on which turn version occurred, and the gap between them is meaningful. Check-through version: BTN checked back the K-high turn with a blank 6s. This removes natural double-barrel hands (AK for top two, KQ/KJ for protection), inflates the delayed float range (7x, 5x, 86, 64 type straight-draws that picked up nothing), and preserves slow-played Ax combos (A7, A6 for two pair). When the Ac arrives and BTN fires 65%, the range includes AK (activated two pair), slow-played A7/A6, and a meaningful population of 'story-bluffs' — delayed floats that fire the ace as a narrative. Hero's Kh9s = second pair on the river but with a 9 kicker. Against the check-through range, the bluff density is high enough that Kh9s sits at or above threshold at 65%. Call. Turn-barrel version: BTN fired the turn with a 40–50% barrel, you called. This concentrates BTN's range toward AK, KQ, KJ and removes much of the pure-air float range. When the ace rivers and BTN fires again, AK two pair dominates the value side. Hero's Kh9s is now decisively below threshold. Fold. The takeaway: you must recover the correct turn memory before you can correctly label this hand.

B.explanation: In the check-through version, passive recreational players do not fire delayed ace-river barrels with missed floats — their 65% river bet after checking the K-high turn is value-heavy (slow-played AK, A7, A6). Against Pool B, second pair with 9 kicker folds under the check-through version too. The call-vs-fold memory distinction essentially collapses against passive players.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: This is a memory-decisive spot — the correct action depends on which turn version actually happened. What separates a memory-decisive spot from a memory-tolerant one?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: when memory precision is strategically required vs when it is not
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: A memory-decisive spot is one where the check-through range and the barrel range produce different enough bluff-to-value ratios that the threshold flips. Here, the check-through inflates the bluff pool enough for second pair to call, but the barrel concentrates the range enough that the same hand must fold. The memory gap is not ignorable.

options.label: Any time you're unsure of the exact size, it's a memory-decisive spot — you always need to know the size.
A spot is memory-decisive when the two plausible turn versions produce different enough range compositions that the river threshold flips — call under one version, fold under the other.
When memory is unclear, always fold — it's the safe default.

coaching_context.key_concept: Not all memory gaps are equal. A memory-decisive spot is one where the correct river action flips depending on which version of the turn occurred. These spots should be flagged in live-hand review as needing memory recovery — they cannot be resolved with generic principles alone.
common_mistakes: Applying the check-through calling rule to a hand where the turn might have been barreled, over-calling when the barrel version of the turn should produce a fold.
Giving up on the hand review entirely because the memory is fuzzy, rather than identifying which version was played and extracting the correct lesson.

value_buckets.label: AK (check-through: trapped for delayed value; barrel: fired twice) — activates on Ac river in both versions but with different frequency
A7, A6 (slow-played two pair from flop) — more present in check-through version, partially reduced in barrel version

bluff_buckets.label: 86, 64, 75, 5x hands (straight draw floats on K73 flop) — large population in check-through version, smaller in barrel version
Ax backdoor hands that float and now fire the ace as 'I had it the whole time'

threshold_notes: Check-through version: second pair (Kh9s) is at or above threshold at 65% — the bluff pool is large enough. CALL.
Barrel version: second pair is below threshold at 65% — the barrel concentrated the range and the Ac activates AK strongly. FOLD.
The threshold flip is the definition of a memory-decisive spot.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 45% c-bet on K73 is a standard range bet — BTN's range stays wide including all strong Kx, sets, and floats.
The memory gap lives here. Check-through: inflates delayed float bluffs and preserves air. Barrel: filters out air and concentrates strong Kx. This is the strategically decisive detail.
Ac arrives. Both versions have BTN betting 65%. But the range composition — and therefore the threshold — is different enough that the correct action flips between versions.

coaching_context.follow_up: When reviewing live hands, flag memory-decisive spots explicitly and attempt to recover the turn memory through context clues (stack size changes, timing, physical tells remembered). If recovery fails, note it as unresolved — do not default-assign a conclusion.

title: Q95 BB — Probed Turn, BTN Called, Brick-River Overbet, Blocker Insufficient
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. Reviewing: BTN c-bet 40% on Qd 9h 5c. You called with middle pair. Turn 4s — blank. You probed 40% pot with the pair. BTN called. You remember being surprised BTN called — you expected a fold. River 3d — brick. You check. BTN overbets 110% pot. You hold Th 9s. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: The surprise you felt when BTN called your probe is the signal you needed. On a Qd9h5c board with a blank 4s turn, your 40% probe with middle pair (9s) was a thin value/semi-bluff bet. When BTN called the probe instead of folding, their calling range concentrated around: Qx (top pair — ahead of your 9s), 99/55 (sets), straight draws with pair (T8, 86, 76 on the connected Q95 flop). On the brick 3d river, BTN overbets 110%. After calling a probe and overbetting a brick river, BTN's range is extremely concentrated toward strong made hands — Qx trips (if Q re-pairs), sets making full houses, or straight completions (A2 making wheel through 5-4-3-2-A, or 62 making 2-3-4-5-6 through the 5c-4s-3d run). Hero holds Th9s: middle pair (9s) with a T — the 9s blocks one 9 combo (with 9h on board, hero's 9s eliminates 9s from BTN's 99 set, leaving only 9d9c). But this partial blocker is insufficient against an overbet range that called a probe. The surprise BTN call was the real tell — that reaction was the signal to recalibrate on the river.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players call probes with a wide range — stubborn medium pairs, gut-shots, even complete air they 'felt like' calling with. Against Pool C, BTN's probe-calling range is diluted enough with non-value hands that their 110% river overbet could be a power move with nothing. Call and punish.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: You were surprised BTN called your turn probe. How should that surprise affect your river read?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: live tells as range information — unexpected call tightens the calling range
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The surprise indicates BTN called with something you didn't expect to survive. That means their range is either stronger than you assumed (Qx, sets) or they're a pool-C player who calls probes widely. Either recalibration makes the river fold more clear against baseline pools.

options.label: Live reads are unreliable — ignore the surprise and evaluate the river bet on its merits alone.

The surprise is useful information: BTN called when a fold was expected, which means their range is either stronger than assumed or they're a wide-range pool-C player. Either interpretation makes the river overbet a fold against baseline pools.
If they called unexpectedly, they're probably on a wild bluff — call the river overbet.

coaching_context.key_concept: In live hand review, the emotional data from the session — surprise, confusion, a felt tells-read — often contains useful strategic information. An unexpected call on the turn is a range signal, not just a curiosity.
common_mistakes: Discounting live impressions when reviewing hands at home because they feel too subjective to use.
Holding middle pair into an overbet after a probe-call because 'the 9s blocks their set' — one partial set-blocker is not sufficient against a probe-call overbet range.

value_buckets.label: Qx (top pair — called probe, now fires overbet for value) — primary value range
99, 55 (sets — called probe, now make full houses on board with paired potential) — present
T8, 86 (straight combos on Q95-4-3 board) — possible made straights after 5-4-3 run

bluff_catchers.label: Th9s (middle pair 9s, T kicker) — below threshold for 110% overbet after a probe-call

threshold_notes: Middle pair with a partial set-blocker (9s reduces 99 by one combo) is well below threshold for a 110% pot overbet following a probe-call.
The threshold only flips to a call against Pool C, where the probe-calling range is wide enough to include hands that will fire overbets with air.
blocker_notes: Hero's 9s removes 9s9x combos from BTN's 99 set (board has 9h, hero has 9s — remaining 9s are 9d and 9c for BTN's 99 = only 9d9c). One 99 combo blocked.
This partial 9 blocker is real but too small to justify calling a 110% overbet against a probe-calling range.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 40% c-bet on Q95 — BTN commits some range to the board. Qx, sets, and over-pairs are all present.

BB's probe was called — unexpectedly per hero's memory. The surprise-call concentrates BTN's range toward hands that comfortably beat middle pair. The emotional signal from the session is valuable range data.
3d brick. BTN overbets 110% after calling the probe — an extremely concentrated signal. Value-dominated against baseline pools.

coaching_context.follow_up: Build the habit of logging 'felt reads' during live session review — moments of surprise, confusion, or physical tells. These often encode range information that is otherwise lost by the time you sit down to review hands.

title: K83 BB — Ace River After Double Barrel, K Blocker Direction, Memory Confirms Fold
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. Reviewing a hand: BTN c-bet 50% on Ks 8h 3c. You called. Turn Jd — BTN bet again around 60%, you called. You're confident in this double-barrel recall. River As. BTN bets 80% pot. You hold Kd 7s. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: You're confident in your double-barrel recall — that's exactly what this hand requires. The double barrel on Ks8h3c-Jd concentrates BTN's range around strong KJ (top two pair — a powerful hand on this board), KQ/KT (strong Kx protecting), 88/33 (sets), JJ (top set on the Jd turn), and combo-draw semi-bluffs with flush+straight equity. That filtering means by the river As, only committed, strong, or semi-bluff hands are present. Hero holds Kd7s: second pair K on the river (As ranks higher), with Kd blocking some Kx combos. The Kd reduces BTN's KJ, KQ, KT by one combo each (Kd removed). But the primary danger on an ace river after a double barrel is AK — top two pair. Hero's Kd reduces AK combos: BTN can't hold AKd, removing AKd from the 8 possible AK combos (Ks on board, Kd in hero's hand: BTN's K options are Kh and Kc — so AK for BTN = {Ah, Ac, Ad} × {Kh, Kc} = 6 combos). Hero reduced AK from 8 to 6. That is a meaningful reduction but not decisive: 6 AK combos remain, plus sets, KJ top two, and semi-bluff misses. Second pair with 7 kicker is below threshold at 80% for any plausible pool composition against this line.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreational players double-barrel and fire river bluffs at a high enough frequency that even after a double barrel, the 80% river bet includes a meaningful bluff component. Against Pool C, second pair with Kd blocks enough AK combos to push the hand above threshold. Call and punish the triple-barrel bluff tendency.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Kd. After a double barrel with an ace river, what does the Kd actually remove from BTN's range?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: K blocker after double barrel: reduces secondary value more than primary danger
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Kd removes BTN's KJ/KQ/KT by one combo each (reducing secondary Kx value). For AK specifically: Ks is on the board, Kd is in hero's hand — BTN's remaining K options are Kh and Kc, so AK = {Ah, Ac, Ad} × {Kh, Kc} = 6 combos. AK is reduced from 8 to 6 but remains the primary danger. The K blocker is useful but not decisive.

options.label: Kd removes KJ and KQ completely and reduces AK enough to justify calling — the blocker is strong here.

Kd removes 2 AK combos (those containing Kd) and reduces KJ/KQ by one combo each. It points somewhat in the right direction but leaves 6 AK combos live — not enough to justify a call against a double-barrel range.
The double barrel doesn't matter — evaluate the blocker in isolation from the turn action.

coaching_context.key_concept: Confidence in your turn-line recall is itself strategically useful. When you're sure a double barrel occurred, you can apply the correct range concentration analysis. The K blocker provides partial AK reduction but not enough to overcome the concentration produced by the double barrel.
common_mistakes: Treating the K blocker as strongly helpful on an ace river because 'K blocks lots of value' — on a Ks-board with As river, Kd removes only 2 AK combos out of 8.
Using blocker logic to call when the turn-line concentration already indicates fold regardless of the blocker.

value_buckets.label: AK (top two pair on As river) — 6 combos live after hero's Kd blocker
KJ (top two pair on Jd turn) — firing three streets for max value
88, 33 (sets — full houses if board pairs) — committed double-barrel value

bluff_buckets.label: Combo draws (KhQh, KhTh, QhTh) that fired twice for equity — some miss and fire third street
JhTh, Jh9h type flush+straight combos from the Jd turn — occasional river bluffs

threshold_notes: Second pair (Kd7s as second pair behind As) is below threshold at 80% against a double-barrel range, even with the Kd reducing 2 AK combos.
Against Pool C (triple-barrel bluffers), the threshold flips — call is correct because the bluff frequency of the full three-street range is high enough.
blocker_notes: Kd removes AKd (BTN can't hold it with Kd in hero's hand). Ks is on the board. BTN's remaining K options: Kh, Kc. AK combos: {Ah, Ac, Ad} × {Kh, Kc} = 6. Hero reduced from 8 to 6.
KJ is also reduced: Kd removes KdJx combos for BTN, leaving KhJx and KcJx — partial reduction of 3 KJ combos.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on K83 signals a committed Kx range — AK, KQ, KJ, and sets are all firing.
Jd is a dynamic turn card (connects with KJ for top two, gives combo draws). BTN's 60% barrel here concentrates the range — most air folds, strong Kx and sets continue. Memory is confident here.
As arrives. Triple barrel from a double-barrel range concentrates value: AK (top two), KJ (top two from turn), sets. Kd blocker provides partial AK reduction but not enough for second pair to call.

coaching_context.follow_up: Practice the AK combo count when holding a K-blocker on ace rivers: board K cards plus hero's K determine how many K options BTN has, then multiply by available aces. If AK remains at 6+ combos, a K blocker is insufficient to overcome a double-barrel river bet.

title: JT4 BB — Multi-Step: Turn Line Memory Changes Which Blocker Is Load-Bearing
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. Board: Jh Tc 4d | 9s | Kd. You hold 9c 7h. Work through two versions of how this turn went to understand which blockers matter under each.

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero holds 9c7h: a pair of 9s (9c in hand + 9s on board) plus a 7. On Jh-Tc-4d-9s-Kd, the board runs J-T-9-K — four high cards with straight potential. Against a 65% river bet, hero is deciding whether middle pair (9s) is above threshold. The turn check version of this hand (scripted here) means BTN's range includes delayed floats (Q8, Q7, A8 type hands that had gut-shot equity on JT4 flop and gained nothing on 9s). But on the Kd river, BTN's value includes KJ (top two), KT (two pair), K9 (two pair), K4 (two pair), and slow-played sets. Hero's 9c blocks 99 full house (9c in hand + 9s on board means BTN can't have 9c, reducing 99 to 9d9h only — 1 combo). And 7h blocks 7x straight combos surviving as bluffs in the check-through version. The full analysis is in the steps.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds 9c (pairs with board 9s) and 7h. On a J-T-9-K board, which blocker matters more and why does the turn line affect this?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blocker relevance is conditioned on which hands survive the turn line
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The 9c blocks one 99 full house combo. The 7h blocks 7-8-9-T-J straight combos (if BTN had 7h+ one more card in the straight). After a turn check-through, BTN still has straight-draw combos (Q8, Q7, 87) in range — so the 7h blocker is more relevant in the check-through version. After a double barrel, the straight-draw bluffs would have fired the turn, so the 7h becomes less relevant on the river.

options.label: The 9c is more important — it blocks the 99 full house, which is the scariest value.
The 7h blocks 7x straight combos that survive as bluffs in the check-through version but would have fired the turn barrel version. The relevant blocker depends on the turn line.
Neither blocker changes the threshold enough to matter — ignore them.

steps.prompt: SCENARIO A — Turn check-through (both checked 9s): BTN fires 65% on Kd river. You hold 9c 7h. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Turn check-through means BTN's range includes delayed floats (Q8, 87, A8 type hands) and slow-played value (KJ, sets). River Kd activates KJ/KT/K9 two-pair combos and fires for value. Hero's 9c blocks 99 full house (1 combo) and 7h blocks some 7x straight combos. But middle pair (9s) faces a value-concentrated range on a K river. The delayed float bluffs are present (Q8 type) but the threshold analysis: KJ/KT value is numerous enough that middle pair sits below threshold at 65%.

steps.prompt: SCENARIO B — BTN barreled the 9s turn (~55%) and you called. BTN fires 65% on Kd river. Same hand 9c 7h. How does this change the blocker analysis?

options.label: Fold — double barrel concentrates range, 7h blocker less useful
Call — 7h still blocks enough to justify

answer.explanation: When BTN barreled the 9s turn, straight-draw combos (87, Q8) that wanted equity would have fired. Those hands mostly filtered out of the river range — they either made their hand or surrendered. The 7h blocker, which was most useful against 7x straight combos surviving to river, now covers a smaller portion of BTN's range. Meanwhile, the double barrel concentrates BTN's river range toward KJ (double-barreled for protection/value), 99 (now trips+), and KQ (arrived via double barrel). Middle pair is even further below threshold in this version, and the 7h blocker is now less load-bearing than in the check-through version.

coaching_context.key_concept: Blocker relevance is conditioned on the turn line. A blocker only helps if the hands it removes are still present in the villain's range. Turn barrels filter out bluff hands (straight draws that fired and missed) — reducing the relevance of the blocker that was meant to cover them.
common_mistakes: Evaluating blockers without checking whether the hands they block are still in villain's range after the turn action.
Applying the same blocker logic to both check-through and double-barrel turn versions as if the range composition were identical.

value_buckets.label: KJ (top two pair after Kd river) — present in both versions, heavier in double-barrel
KT, K9 (two pair combinations after Kd) — present
99 (full house — 9s on board + 99 in hand) — 1 combo blocked by hero's 9c

bluff_buckets.label: Q8, 87, A8 (delayed straight-draw floats) — present in check-through version, filtered in barrel version
Kx air hands that check turn and then fire river Kd as a 'I always had a K' narrative

threshold_notes: Middle pair (9s) is below threshold in both versions, but the margin is tighter in the check-through version where the bluff pool is larger.
The 7h blocker reduces the deficit in the check-through version but not enough to cross the calling threshold.
blocker_notes: 9c blocks 99 full house: BTN's 99 = {9d, 9h} after hero's 9c and board's 9s removed. Only 9d9h = 1 combo.
7h blocks 7x straight combos in villain's range — most useful in the check-through version where those combos survive as bluffs.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 50% c-bet on JT4 — a connected board. Straight draws (Q8, K8, A8, Q7) are all present in BTN's range alongside value.

The 9s turn is significant: Q8 on JT4-9 board makes the 8-9-T-J-Q straight. Q8 is now value, not a bluff. Check-through preserved this value quietly. The 7h blocker becomes more relevant against remaining 7x draws in the check-through pool.
Kd river. BTN fires 65%. Range includes straights (Q8 made on turn), two pairs (KJ, KT, K9), and sets. Hero's middle pair faces a value-heavy range in both turn-line scenarios.

coaching_context.follow_up: When reviewing hands with board cards that complete straights (like 9 on J-T-4 board completing Q8), track which hands quietly made their draw — those shift from bluffs to value and change the blocker analysis entirely.

title: K94 BB — Multi-Step: Same Pair, Blockers Decisive Under One Turn Memory, Irrelevant Under Other
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. Board: Kh 9c 4d | 5h | Kd. You hold Ad 9d. K paired on the river. Work through how your blockers matter differently depending on the turn-line version you're remembering.

options.label: Call
Fold

answer.explanation: The check-through version of this turn (scripted here) is the key context. Hero holds Ad9d: middle pair (9s — 9d + 9c on board) plus an A. On Kh9c4d-5h-Kd, the K paired on the river. Hero's Ad provides a meaningful blocker: Ad removes AK combos containing the Ad (BTN can't hold AdKx). Hero's 9d removes 99 combos: with 9c on board and 9d in hero's hand, BTN's 99 = 9h9s only (1 combo out of 3). These two blockers together reduce the primary danger combos (AK two pair, 99 full house) by a meaningful fraction. Against the check-through range — which also includes delayed floats and missed draws — middle pair with strong A+9 blockers sits at or above threshold at 65%.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Ad and 9d — both reduce primary danger combos. How does the turn line version determine whether these blockers are sufficient?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: blockers must overcome range concentration; turn line sets the concentration level
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: In the check-through version, the bluff pool is inflated and the value pool is reduced (AK didn't barrel, 99 didn't barrel). With both the Ad and 9d reducing the primary danger combos, middle pair is at threshold. In the double-barrel version, the value pool is concentrated enough that even removing AK and 99 combos doesn't overcome the overall value density — fold.

options.label: Ad and 9d are strong enough blockers to justify calling in any turn scenario.

In the check-through version, Ad+9d blockers make middle pair a call because the bluff pool is large and the blockers meaningfully reduce primary danger. In the double-barrel version, range concentration overwhelms the blocker edge — fold.
The blocker math doesn't matter — middle pair is always a fold on a paired-K river.

steps.prompt: SCENARIO A — Turn check-through (both checked 5h): BTN bets 65% on Kd river. You hold Ad9d. The A and 9 both block primary danger combos. Call or fold?

options.label: Call — blockers + inflated bluff pool = above threshold
Fold — middle pair not enough even with blockers

answer.explanation: Turn check-through inflates the delayed float range (A6, A7, 86, 64 type hands that picked up nothing on 5h). River Kd pairs the K. BTN's value after check-through: KQ/KJ (thin Kx that didn't barrel), AK (trapped), 99 (slow-played set now full house), 44 (set). Hero's Ad reduces AK by 3 combos, 9d reduces 99 to 1 combo. Against the inflated bluff pool and reduced danger combos, middle pair with A9 blockers sits above threshold at 65%.

steps.prompt: SCENARIO B — BTN barreled 5h turn (~55%), you called. BTN bets 65% on Kd river. Same hand Ad9d. Do your blockers still justify the call?

options.label: Fold — double barrel concentrated range overwhelms the blockers
Call — Ad and 9d are still strong enough

answer.explanation: BTN's turn barrel on the 5h concentrates the range: KQ/KJ fire for protection, AK commits to two barrels, 99 builds the pot. The bluff pool shrinks (air hands fold the turn). River Kd brings a third bet from this concentrated range. Hero's Ad reduces AK by 3 combos (6 remain) and 9d reduces 99 to 1 combo. But the total value density — KJ, KQ, AK (6 combos), 99 (1 combo), 44 (3 combos) — is still high enough relative to the thinned bluff pool that middle pair is below threshold. The blockers help but cannot overcome double-barrel range concentration at this sizing.

coaching_context.key_concept: The same blockers (Ad9d) can push a hand above or below threshold depending on whether the bluff pool is inflated (check-through) or deflated (double barrel). Blockers only overcome range concentration up to a point — and that point is determined by the turn line.
common_mistakes: Concluding that strong blockers (Ad + 9d) are sufficient for a call without considering how the turn line shaped the bluff-to-value ratio that the blockers must overcome.
Applying the same blocker logic across both turn scenarios as if they produce identical range compositions.

value_buckets.label: AK (top two pair on Kd river) — reduced by Ad: ~6 combos remaining in check-through, ~6 in barrel (similar reduction but different context)
99 (full house) — reduced to 1 combo by 9d; key reduction in check-through version
KQ, KJ (two-pair candidates) — thin in check-through, heavier in barrel

bluff_buckets.label: A6, A7, 86, 64 (delayed floats on K94-5 board) — large in check-through, mostly filtered in barrel
Flush/straight misses that fire Kd as a delayed story bluff

threshold_notes: Check-through: Ad+9d blockers + inflated bluff pool = middle pair above threshold at 65%. CALL.
Double barrel: Ad+9d blockers insufficient against concentrated range even after reductions. FOLD.
The blocker edge is real in both versions; it only tips the balance in the check-through version.
blocker_notes: Ad removes AK combos where BTN has Ad: BTN's AK = {Ah, Ac, As} × {Kc, Ks} (Kh on flop, Kd on river: both board Ks consumed). AK = 6 combos. Ad removed ~3 combos.
9d removes 99 combos: 9c on board + 9d in hero's hand means BTN's 99 = 9h9s = 1 combo only. A 2-combo reduction from 3.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 40% small c-bet on K94 — range bet keeping BTN wide. Sets, strong Kx, and floats all remain.
The turn action is the decisive variable for whether the blockers tip the call. Check-through: bluff pool intact, blockers make a difference. Barrel: bluff pool filtered, blockers insufficient.
Kd pairs the K. Hero's Ad9d reduces primary danger combos under both versions. The difference is whether the bluff pool is large enough for the blocker-reduced value density to tip the threshold.

coaching_context.follow_up: Build a mental model for 'blocker plus bluff pool' analysis: a blocker's strategic power depends on (a) how many danger combos it removes and (b) how large the bluff pool is after the turn line. Both inputs are required.

title: QT6 BB — Multi-Step: Probe Recalled, Overbet Sizing Determines Pool-Adjusted Fold
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP, 100bb effective. You remember probing the Qh Th 6d flop (turn 4c — blank). You probed about 40% on the turn. BTN called. You're confident in this. River 2s — brick. You check. BTN fires. Evaluate two bet sizes from BTN to find where the threshold flips.

options.label: Fold
Call

answer.explanation: Hero probed the 4c turn (blank on Qh-Th-6d) with top pair (Qd7s). BTN called the probe. On the Qh-Th-6d board, BTN's probe-calling range is strong: Qx with better kickers (QJ, QK, QA ahead of Q7), 99 (over-pair that called for value), TT/66 (sets), and straight/flush combos with pair (J9h, 87h, KJh type hands). Hero's Qd7s is top pair with a 7 kicker — the weakest reasonable Qx holding. Against BTN's probe-calling range on this board, Q7 kicker is already borderline on the flop; by river after BTN called the probe, hero's top pair 7-kicker is below threshold for any reasonable bet. The 2s is a brick — no draws completed. BTN fires into hero who showed thin-value aggression by probing and then checked river. The probe created the river problem: it invited a call from a range that beats Q7 kicker.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: BB probed with Q7 kicker and BTN called. What is the core leak this hand exposes?
diagnostic_prompts.concept: probing thin hands invites a calling range that dominates the probe hand
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Probing top pair with the weakest kicker (Q7) on a Q-T-6 board invites BTN to call with all Qx that has a better kicker (QJ, QK, QA). Those hands beat the probe hand and are content to call. By river, hero holds the weakest Qx in the range — a kicker-dominated hand that cannot beat BTN's probe-calling value.

options.label: The probe was fine — the leak is calling too wide on the flop with Q7.

The probe with Q7 kicker invited BTN to call with all better Qx, creating a reverse-domination dynamic. Hero's top pair is the weakest Qx in the value range and is now dominated by BTN's probe-calling range.
The probe was fine — the leak is not raising the river when BTN bets.

steps.prompt: SCENARIO A: BTN bets 55% pot on brick river 2s after calling your probe. You hold Qd7s (top pair, 7 kicker). Call or fold?

options.label: Fold — probe-call range beats Q7 kicker
Call — 55% is too small to be only value

answer.explanation: At 55%, BTN's range after calling a probe on Qh-Th-6d includes Qx-better-kicker (QJ/QK/QA), TT/66 (sets), 99 (over-pair), and possibly some floats (J9h, 87h that have equity). Hero's Q7 kicker is dominated by the Qx portion of BTN's calling range. Fold in baseline and passive pools. Against Pool C (who calls probes with wider hands including air), closer to call.

steps.prompt: SCENARIO B: Same line, BTN bets 120% pot overbet on 2s river instead. Same hand Qd7s. Does the larger size change the answer?

options.label: Fold — overbet after probe-call is even more value-heavy
Call — overbet creates a polar range with enough bluffs

answer.explanation: At 120% pot, the range becomes more polar but the probe-call dynamic means the bluff pool is thin. Live players who call probes and then overbet rivers are almost exclusively value-betting (sets, two pair, Qx-better-kicker). The polar overbet argument (more bluffs justify calling) applies when the bluff pool is large — but probe-calls filter out weak floats, leaving a value-dense range. Top pair 7-kicker is even further below threshold at an overbet. Pool C is the only exception: their probe-calling range is wide enough that even the overbet could include enough air to justify a call.

coaching_context.key_concept: Probing with the weakest holding of a made-hand category (Q7 kicker when Qx is top pair) creates a reverse-domination dynamic: the probe invites calls from all superior versions of the same hand class. By the river, hero holds the worst Qx and faces a range full of better Qx.
common_mistakes: Treating top pair as a generic calling hand on the river without checking whether hero's kicker is dominated by BTN's probe-calling range.
Applying the polar-overbet calling logic to a probe-called line where the bluff pool has been filtered by the turn call.

value_buckets.label: Qx better kicker (QJ, QK, QA) — called probe, now beats Q7 kicker
TT, 66 (sets — called probe with disguised hand) — now firing for value
99 (over-pair that called probe) — still ahead of Q7

bluff_catchers.label: Qd7s (top pair, 7 kicker) — below threshold: dominated by BTN's probe-calling value

threshold_notes: Q7 kicker is below threshold at both 55% and 120% after a probe-call on this board.
The threshold does not flip to a call at overbet sizing because the probe-call dynamic already filtered out the air that would make an overbet bluff-justifiable.

what_changed_by_street.detail: 45% c-bet on Q-T-6 — a draw-heavy board. BTN's range includes strong Qx, sets, and flush/straight draws.
BB's probe on 4c turn with Q7 kicker: thin probe that invited all better Qx to call. BTN's call confirmed a range beating or dominating Q7. The probe created the river problem.
2s brick. BTN fires into the range that probed-then-checked. At both 55% and 120%, the probe-call range is value-heavy relative to Q7 kicker. Fold under all realistic sizing scenarios.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study kicker-dominated probing spots: when your probe hand is the weakest member of its value class (Q7 when Q-x is the relevant hand type), reconsider whether probing is creating a favorable or unfavorable dynamic for subsequent streets.

title: KQ BB - Turn Bet-Call Kills the River Call: A Blank River FOLD After Turn Barrel
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 33% on Kc 9h 4d, then bets 50% pot on the Js turn after you check. You call. River 2c blank. You check. BTN bets 70% pot. You hold Kd Qh. This is a fold - but if the turn had checked through, it would be a call. What changed?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: The decisive fact is the turn bet-call, not the river card. BTN barreled the Js turn into a K-9-4 board - that action removes most of BTN's wide delayed-float range and concentrates it toward AK, KJ, KQ (hero blocks with Qh), QQ, JJ, 99, 44, and KJ two-pair. On the blank 2c river, BTN's third barrel is credibly value-heavy. KdQh is one pair, and even with the Qh partially blocking KQ and QJ combinations, it falls below the threshold for this condensed line. If the turn had checked through, KQ would sit above threshold because the delayed range includes far more air - missed backdoor draws, K-high floats, and hands that were waiting to fire river as bluffs. The turn check-through is what makes the river call profitable; the turn barrel is what makes it a fold.

A.explanation: Competent regulars construct the K-9-4 turn barrel tightly. KQ is too far down the range on a three-street line to justify a call.

B.explanation: Passive live players almost never fire three streets without a very strong hand. KQ should fold clearly against a passive pool after a turn barrel.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreationals barrel the J turn at a much higher frequency than the field, including with thin holdings and semi-bluff combinations that arrive at the river with no real value. Against a known aggro-rec overbluffer, KQ crosses the threshold as a thin call.

diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn action filter - memory-decisive
diagnostic_prompts.prompt: If the turn had checked through instead of BTN barreling, how does the river decision change for KdQh?
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn check-through preserves the full width of BTN's delayed floating range - missed gutshots, K-high air, backdoor draw miss. That density makes KQ above threshold. The turn barrel compresses that width and shifts BTN toward value, making KQ below threshold on a blank river.

options.label: The river card is what matters. The turn action is already in the past - it doesn't change the threshold.
Turn check-through preserves a wide delayed range. KQ crosses threshold when that range is wide; it falls below threshold once BTN condenses it with a barrel.
KQ is too strong to fold to a river bet in SRP - top pair with a Q kicker always calls one street.

diagnostic_prompts.concept: partial blocker insufficient to cross threshold
diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Hero holds Qh. Does that blocker make calling profitable here?
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: Qh partially removes KQ and QJ from BTN's value range, but it does not reduce AK, KJ, JJ, 99, 44 - the dominant combos in a bet-called turn barrel. The blocker trims value by one or two combos but does nothing to the core of BTN's range. It is not enough to cross threshold.

options.label: Yes - Qh blocks KQ and QJ. That removes enough value combos to justify calling.
Qh removes a few combos at the edges of BTN's value region but does nothing to AK, KJ, JJ, 99. The blocker is insufficient to cross threshold after a turn barrel.
Blockers are irrelevant at these stack depths. Just pot odds.

coaching_context.key_concept: The turn bet-call is the decisive line event. It removes the delayed-float bluff density that makes KQ a river call in check-through spots. Carrying the wrong range into the river is the root error.
common_mistakes: Calling river bets with top pair because 'I have a decent hand' without accounting for what the turn barrel did to BTN's range composition.
Treating the Qh as a meaningful blocker without checking which part of BTN's range it actually removes.

value_buckets.label: Two-barrel value on K-9-4-J-2
value_buckets.note: These hands have genuine multi-street value density and represent most of BTN's bet-call line.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Dominant value region after turn barrel

bluff_buckets.label: Surviving bluffs in the barrel line
bluff_buckets.note: A small semi-bluff fraction survives the turn barrel with straight equity. Much thinner than in a check-through line.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Low - barrel compresses the bluff ratio

bluff_catchers.label: Hands above threshold in check-through scenario
bluff_catchers.note: These sit above threshold when the turn checked through and BTN's range is wide. They fall below threshold after a turn barrel.
bluff_catchers.frequency_hint: Context-dependent - only call in check-through version

threshold_notes: KQ is above threshold in check-through lines because the delayed-float range is wide enough to justify a call.
After a turn barrel, KQ falls below threshold. The Qh blocker provides insufficient offset.
blocker_notes: Qh removes a few KQ and QJ combos from BTN's value region, but AK, KJ, JJ, 99, 44 are untouched.
The key insight: blockers must remove a meaningful slice of the threatening range - not just trim its edges.

hero_hand_bucket.label: One pair - line-dependent threshold
hero_hand_bucket.summary: CALL after check-through; FOLD after bet-call
hero_hand_bucket.note: KQ is not inherently a fold or a call. The turn line is the controlling variable.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Small c-bet 33% on K-9-4. BTN's range is kept wide - includes all top-pair combos, sets, and the full floating range.

The Js barrel is the decisive filter. BTN double-barrels into a K-9-4-J board, compressing the range toward value: AK, KJ, KQ (hero blocks), JJ, 99, 44 and semi-bluffs with straight draws. The wide delayed-float range exits here - there is no longer enough air in BTN's range for KQ to bluff-catch profitably.
2c blank adds nothing meaningful. The correct river answer was determined on the turn when BTN fired. Hero should recognize the fold decision was locked in by the prior street filter.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study how the turn barrel threshold changes based on board texture: a barrel on a scare card (J on K-9-4) has different range implications than a barrel on a blank.

notes: BTN vs BB SRP river bluff-catch lane - Claude batch 4 - memory-decisive reconstruction spots

title: QJ BB - Turn Check-Through Makes the Ace River a Call; Overbluff Punish After Check-Back
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. BTN c-bets 33% on Qh 8s 4c, then checks back the Kd turn after you check. River Ah. You check. BTN bets 80% pot. You hold Qd Jh. The turn check is the reason this is a call - not the hand strength alone.

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: The Kd turn check-through is the controlling fact. It preserves BTN's entire delayed-float range: K-high air that takes a free turn and stabs the ace river, KJ and KT that checked the K as a trap but rarely arrive with a pure barrel plan, missed backdoor gutshots, and Q-x hands that float flop and use A-K as scare cards to bluff with. QdJh beats all of those. If BTN had instead fired the K turn and received a call, QJ would be a clear fold - BTN's range would be concentrated toward AK, KQ, KK, AQ, and strong two pairs, and the Ah river would improve much of that. The turn check is what turns the A river from a catastrophe into a manageable bluff-catch.

A.explanation: Against competent regulars this is close. QJ calls because the turn check-through still preserves a meaningful bluff region, but the 80% sizing is toward the high end of what makes the call clear.

B.explanation: Passive live players rarely build this three-street story as a bluff after checking back a K turn. Against a passive pool, QJ can fold when the ace fires at 80%.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreationals attack the ace river after free turns at a much higher frequency. QJ should call clearly in an aggro-rec pool.

diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn check-through unlocks scare-river call
diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Why does the turn check-through change the river call from a fold into a call?
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn check preserves BTN's full delayed-float range - K-high bluffs, missed gutshots, and floated draws - while removing the barrel pressure that would have concentrated the range toward value. On the Ah river, BTN's bluffing story is easy and natural with that wide range. If the turn had been bet and called, AK, AQ, KK, and KQ would dominate the range instead, making QJ a fold.

options.label: The ace is always a good river bluff card regardless of prior streets. The turn check is irrelevant.
The turn check preserves BTN's wide floating range. That range bluffs the ace naturally. A turn barrel would have condensed BTN's range toward value, turning the ace into a value card instead.
QJ is second pair. It always calls one river bet regardless of prior streets.

diagnostic_prompts.concept: memory-decisive flip - the turn bet-call version
diagnostic_prompts.prompt: If BTN had instead bet 50% on the Kd turn and you called, how does the river Ah change?
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: A Kd barrel from BTN represents a range heavy in AK, KQ, KK, and top-pair+ hands. The Ah river improves a large part of that range. QJ is now below threshold because the bluff fraction in BTN's range is thin after the turn barrel, and the Ah gives value to too many of the hands that fired.

options.label: The hand is the same either way. QJ calls in both versions.
After a turn barrel, BTN's range is condensed toward value. The Ah river improves much of that range. QJ falls below threshold and should fold.
QJ should fold regardless. The ace river is too scary for second pair in either version.

coaching_context.key_concept: The Kd turn check-through is the decisive event. It preserves the wide delayed range that makes the Ah river a bluff-catch node, not a value-heavy disaster.
common_mistakes: Treating the ace river as automatically dangerous without checking whether the turn action limited BTN's value density.
Folding QJ to big river bets on scary rivers without carrying the turn filter forward.

value_buckets.label: Value that survived the turn check
value_buckets.note: Some premium hands check back K turn with a trapping plan. Present but not dominant.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low to moderate - not the typical line

value_buckets.label: Hands with Q-pair value
value_buckets.note: Two pairs that checked the K turn for pot control.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Sparse

bluff_buckets.label: Delayed floats - K-high and J-high air
bluff_buckets.note: These check back K turn to avoid losing hero's continuing range, then use the A river as a scare narrative.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Primary bluff region in check-through line

bluff_buckets.label: Backdoor miss and floats
bluff_buckets.note: These arrive as A-high air or missed draws, now bluffing the ace river naturally.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Substantial in check-through scenario

bluff_catchers.label: Hands that beat the bluff region
bluff_catchers.note: Second pair above threshold because the check-through range contains real air density.
bluff_catchers.frequency_hint: Defend - do not fold to delayed big bets with this texture

threshold_notes: QJ sits above threshold because the delayed-float bluff region is large enough to justify the 80% call.
Against pool B (passive), the pool under-bluffs delayed lines sufficiently to make this a fold.
The decisive variable is not the river size - it is the turn action that set the bluff-to-value ratio.
blocker_notes: Qd partially blocks QK, QQ type combos in BTN's range, but these are rare in a check-through line anyway.
Jh provides minimal blocking effect here.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Second pair - line-dependent threshold
hero_hand_bucket.summary: CALL after check-through; FOLD after turn barrel
hero_hand_bucket.note: This is a reconstruction spot. The memory that BTN checked back the turn is the reason the call is correct.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Small c-bet 33% on Q-8-4. Wide range stays in. BTN has top pair, second pair, backdoor draws, and the full delayed-float constituency.

BTN checks back the Kd. This is the controlling event. It preserves the full delayed range - K-high air, J-high floats, backdoor misses. No barrels remove those hands. The check-through converts this from an expensive call-down spot into a profitable bluff-catch node.
Ah arrives. BTN bets 80%. The ace is a natural scare card for BTN's delayed range - it fits the bluffing story for hands like KT, A9, and J9 that float K turn. QJ beats all of them.

coaching_context.follow_up: Practice identifying when a turn check-through makes a scare-river call correct vs incorrect. The key variable is whether enough floats survive the turn to bluff the scary card.

steps.prompt: BTN checks back the Kd after c-betting the Q-8-4 flop. Before the river card arrives: what does this turn check do to BTN's range - and why does it matter for the next street?

options.label: It preserves BTN's full delayed-float range. The river call threshold depends on this being wide.
BTN checking means mostly trap-strength. The range is narrow and strong after a turn check.

answer.explanation: Turn check-through preserves the full width: K-high floats, missed backdoors, J-high air. These all retain the ability to bluff a scary river card. A BTN barrel would have stripped that width out.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The check-through is the event that creates the profitable river bluff-catch. Lock in this read before the river arrives.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Recognizing the turn check-through before the river arrives sets the correct prior for the threshold calculation.

bluff_buckets.label: Delayed floats preserved by the turn check
bluff_buckets.note: These hands exist in BTN's river bluffing range only because the turn checked through.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Turn read
hero_hand_bucket.summary: Check-through = wide bluff density on river
hero_hand_bucket.note: This step is a reminder to carry the line forward before acting on the river.

steps.prompt: River Ah. BTN bets 80% pot. You confirmed the turn checked through. Now execute: call or fold with QdJh?

options.label: Fold - ace river is too dangerous for second pair
Call - turn check-through means this range bluffs the ace naturally

answer.explanation: The turn check-through preserved enough delayed floats that the ace river bluffing narrative is well-populated. QJ beats all of it and sits above threshold.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Ace arrives. BTN's delayed-float range uses this as a natural bluff trigger. Second pair calls because the turn established the bluff density.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: This call is directly downstream of the turn read in Step 1. The river decision is not made in isolation - it is the conclusion of the two-street analysis.

bluff_catchers.note: Sufficient to call because the check-through confirmed wide bluff density.

title: J8 BB - Hero Probe-Call Collapses River Call on Paired Seven: Fold After Range Compression
prompt: $2/$5 live. BB vs BTN SRP. After calling a 33% flop c-bet on Jd 7c 3h, you probe 40% pot on the 9s turn. BTN calls. River 7s pairs the flop seven. You check. BTN bets 65%. You hold Js 8d. The line you built on the turn changed this into a fold. If the turn had checked through, this would be a call.

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: The probe bet-call is the decisive event. When BB probes 40% and BTN calls on the 9s turn (on a J-7-3 board), BTN's calling range is condensed: it removes most of BTN's air and delayed floats, and retains hands that want equity or pot control with real board interaction - KJ, QJ, JT, T8 straight draws, TT, 88, and slow-played sets. The 7s river pairs the flop seven - BTN's set of 7s and 77-type hands improve to full houses, and the paired board also activates BTN's bluff story for any 7-x range with showdown problems. JS8d is one pair (top pair with an 8 kicker) after a probe that invited the best BTN hands to continue. If the turn had checked through instead, BTN would arrive at the river with the full delayed float range intact, and J8 would sit above threshold as a bluff-catch. The probe changed the problem fundamentally, and the paired seven adds additional value-hand density to BTN's range.

A.explanation: A competent regular's probe-call range on J-7-3-9 is very tight. J8 is well below threshold after inviting those continues.

B.explanation: Passive live players almost never call a probe with air. BTN's call range is real. J8 folds clearly.

C.explanation: Aggressive recreationals call probes with a wider range including floats and weak draws, and then fire river as bluffs more frequently than other pools. Against a known aggro-rec, J8 becomes a thin call - their probe-call range contains more air that arrives bluffing the river.

diagnostic_prompts.concept: probe-call compresses BTN's range toward value
diagnostic_prompts.prompt: BTN called your 40% probe on the 9s turn. What does that call tell you about BTN's range reaching the river?
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: A BTN call of a OOP probe bet on J-7-3-9 removes most of BTN's air and weak floats. Calling hands have real equity or board interaction: KJ, QJ, JT, T8, sets, and some overcards with backdoor equity. This is the opposite of a check-through range where air and delayed floats remain.

options.label: BTN can call probes with anything - it doesn't tell me much about their range.
BTN calling a probe removes most of the air. The remaining range is board-connected - top pairs, straight draws with equity, and slow-played sets. It's the opposite of a wide check-through range.
BTN only calls probes with air or pure bluffs - they would have raised their value hands.

diagnostic_prompts.concept: turn check-through would unlock the river call
diagnostic_prompts.prompt: If the turn had checked through instead of you probing, would J8 be above or below threshold on this 4c river?
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: If the turn checked through, BTN arrives at the river with the full delayed range - floats, K-high air, and gutshots that didn't improve. J8 as top pair beats all of that and sits above threshold for a bluff-catch call. The probe is what destroyed that scenario.

options.label: J8 would still fold on the river even if the turn checked through. One pair is too thin regardless.
After a turn check-through, BTN has a wide range with real delayed-float bluffs. J8 beats those and sits above threshold - the call is correct in that version.
The probe doesn't matter for the river decision. The river card and bet size are what control the threshold.

coaching_context.key_concept: The OOP probe converts a bluff-catch node into a value-heavy confrontation. J8 is above threshold in check-through scenarios; it falls below threshold after a probe-call because the probe invited BTN's best continuing hands.
common_mistakes: Probing the turn with a marginal hand and then calling the river as if the probe never happened.
Treating the river decision in isolation without accounting for what the probe did to BTN's range composition.

value_buckets.label: BTN probe-call value range
value_buckets.note: These hands are exactly what BTN keeps after calling a probe on J-7-3-9. They dominate J8.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Core value region after probe-call

value_buckets.label: Slow-played and disguised
value_buckets.note: Some sets float the probe with trapping intent and barrel river.
value_buckets.frequency_hint: Low but present

bluff_buckets.label: Surviving equity hands that fire river as bluffs
bluff_buckets.note: These had equity on the turn, missed the river, and now fire as semi-bluffs. Thin bluff population after a probe-call.
bluff_buckets.frequency_hint: Low - most air folded to the probe

bluff_catchers.label: Hands above threshold only in check-through scenario
bluff_catchers.note: These would call in a check-through scenario where BTN's range is wide. After a probe-call, they fall below threshold.

threshold_notes: J8 crosses the bluff-catch threshold when BTN has a wide range after check-through.
J8 falls below threshold when BTN's range is condensed by calling a probe.
The probe was the decisive action - not the river card, not the river bet size.
blocker_notes: Js blocks JJ (but BTN rarely slow-plays JJ into three streets here) and some KJ combos. Not enough to change the threshold.
The Js blocker is insufficient to make this a call given the concentration of value in the probe-call range.

hero_hand_bucket.label: One pair - line-constructed FOLD
hero_hand_bucket.summary: FOLD after probe-call; CALL after check-through
hero_hand_bucket.note: The probe created this problem. Recognizing that the probe changed the river threshold is the coaching target.

what_changed_by_street.detail: Wide flop call after 33% c-bet on J-7-3. BTN keeps a wide range including all the floats that would later be relevant to a check-through scenario.

BB probes 40% into the 9s turn. This is the decisive event. BTN calling removes air and keeps value. The probe invited exactly the range that will crush J8 on the river. If hero had checked the turn instead, BTN's check-through would have preserved the floats that make J8 a profitable call.
4c blank. BTN bets 65%. The river bet comes from BTN's probe-call range - board-connected, value-heavy. J8 is below threshold.

coaching_context.follow_up: Study when OOP probes are profitable vs when they build a range composition problem. The key is whether the probe range is polar enough to fold out the relevant air.
